IC 10 has been classified as an Ir IV galaxy (e.g. vandenBergh1999 ), but RicherBullejosBorissova2001 argue that it has more properties of a blue compact dwarf galaxy. It is also the nearest galaxy hosting a small starburst, evidenced by its large number of Wolf-Rayet stars ( MasseyArmandroffConti1992 ) and the discovery of 144 H II regions ( HodgeLee1990 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 10 as a reddened Local Group dwarf irregular and starburst galaxy, then uses outer-field NIR observations and known C stars to assess AGB-star classification.

quotes.quote: IC 10 is a dwarf irregular galaxy, most probably associated with M31 and located at a rather low Galactic latitude ( = 119^ , b = -3^ ). It is often described as the only starburst galaxy of the Local Group. Its study is hindered by the high reddening E(B-V) 0.8 along the line of sight. IC 10 is not extremely metal poor, from the oxygen abundance of its HII regions Garnett (1990) determined [ Fe/H ] = --0.8, this value would correspond to its youngest population but intermediate-age stars should have slightly lower metallicities.

Since the starburst makes the central region difficult to investigate, our NICS observations target an outer region. IC 10 is particularly suited for NIR observations, it is relatively near at (m-M)_0 = 24.35, (Demers et al. 2004) and contains nearly 700 C stars distributed over an area much larger than its starburst core. From Demers et al. (2004) we have the R,I magnitudes of these C stars along with their narrow-band colors.

sources.summary: The paper presents long-slit spectroscopy of IC 10, deriving ionized-gas abundances in HII regions and emphasizing its violent star formation, shell complex, and starburst activity.

quotes.quote: Spectroscopic long-slit observations of the dwarf Irr galaxy IC 10 were conducted at the 6-m Special Astrophysical Observatory telescope with the SCORPIO focal reducer. The ionized-gas emission spectra in the regions of intense current star formation were obtained for a large number of regions in IC 10. The relative abundances of oxygen, N ^+ , and S ^+ in about twenty HII regions and in the synchrotron superbubble were estimated. We found that the galaxy-averaged oxygen abundance is 12+ ( O/H ) = 8.17 0.35 and the metallicity is Z=0.18 0.14 Z_ .

The dwarf Irr galaxy IC 10 is a unique testing ground for investigating the interstellar medium in regions of violent star formation. The galaxy's images in the H and [ SII ] lines appear as a single giant complex of multiple shells and supershells with sizes from 50 to 800--1000 pc (Zucker 2000; Wilcots and Miller 1998; Gil de Paz et al. 2003; Leroy et al. 2006; Chyzy et al. 2003; Lozinskaya et al. 2008). Its stellar population suggests a recent starburst (t=4 -- 10 Myr) and an older starburst ( t>350 Myr) (Hunter 2001; Zucker 2002; Massey et al. 2007; Vacca et al. 2007; and references therein).

sources.summary: The paper models IC10 as a local dwarf irregular whose observed abundances, gas fraction, mass, and star formation history are best reproduced by bursty formation with metal-enhanced winds and slow accretion.

quotes.quote: We attempt to determine the star formation and gas accretion history of IC10, a local dwarf irregular for which abundance, gas, and mass determinations are available. We apply detailed chemical evolution models to predict the evolution of several chemical elements (He, O, N, S) and compared our predictions with the observational data. We consider additional constraints such as the present-time gas fraction, the star formation rate (SFR), and the total estimated mass of IC10. We assume a dark matter halo for this galaxy and study the development of a galactic wind.

We find that only models with metal-enhanced galactic winds can reproduce the properties of IC10. The star formation must have proceeded in bursts rather than continuously and the bursts must have been less numerous than 10 over the whole galactic lifetime. Finally, IC10 must have formed by a slow process of gas accretion with a timescale of the order of 8 Gyr.

sources.summary: The paper studies IC 10 as a nearby dwarf irregular starburst galaxy, using MPFS and long-slit spectra to analyze ionized gas, metallicity, HII-region conditions, and young ionizing clusters.

quotes.quote: We present the results of observations of the Irr galaxy IC 10 at the 6-m SAO telescope with the panoramic Multi-Pupil Fiber Spectrograph (MPFS). Based on the results of these observations and our long-slit spectroscopy performed previously, we have investigated the ionized-gas emission spectrum in the region of intense star formation and refined the gas metallicity estimates. We show that the ``diagnostic diagrams'' constructed from our observations agree best with the new improved ionization models by Mart in-Manj on et al. Using these models, we have determined the electron density and gas ionization parameter and ionizing-cluster characteristics, the age and mass, from the spectra of the investigated HII regions.

The topicality of detailed studies of the gas emission spectrum and ionization conditions in the nearest dwarf Irr galaxy IC 10 is determined by a number of its peculiarities. The anomalously large number of Wolf--Rayet (WR) stars in IC 10 arouses the greatest interest. The space density of WR stars here is highest among the dwarf galaxies, comparable to that in massive spiral galaxies (Massey et al. 1992; Richer et al. 2001; Massey and Holmes 2002; Crowther et al. 2003; Vacca et al. 2007).

sources.summary: The paper uses IC 10 as a nearby dwarf irregular test case, smoothing earlier ionized-gas data to evaluate how poorer resolution changes kinematic diagrams.

quotes.quote: In order to analyze the effects of spatial resolution on the observed kinematics, we examined two nearby and well-studied dwarf irregular galaxies of the Local Group: IC 10 and IC 1613. We have earlier observed both galaxies at the 6-m telescope with the same equipment, described in Section  obs . The spatial size of regions, encompassed by current star formation is about 1 kpc, just like in the galaxies described above (hereinafter -- the `main sample'). A detailed description of the distributions of ionized and neutral gas, the identification of the expanding HI and HII bubbles, and a discussion of the relation between these bubbles and young stellar groups are given in Lozinskaya2008 and Egorov2010 for IC 10, and in Lozinskaya2003 for IC 1613.

To imitate the effect of low resolution, the original data cubes for IC 10 and IC 1613 in the line were first smoothed by the two-dimensional Gaussians, and then binned, so that the resulting pixel size amounted to 5.6 arcsec. Table  tab_ic lists the distance to the galaxy, and the initial ( ) and smoothed ( _ smo ) spatial resolution. The smoothed data show how these two galaxies would look like when observed with a resolution of _ smo 40 pc, i.e. under the same conditions as the objects of the main sample.

sources.summary: The paper uses IC 10/I10 as a Local Group dynamical constraint, finding its proper motion difficult to fit and coupled to choices about M31's distance.

quotes.quote: In many solutions with M31 distances near the allowed 4- upper limit the proper motions _ of I10 are near the low end of its allowed range. Reducing the M31 distance in these solutions tends to produce an unacceptable reduction of _ for I10. In some of the plotted solutions the M31 distance was moved into the 4- upper bound at the expense of moving _ of I10 toward its 4- lower bound. Many other solutions were found that fit all constraints except that the M31 distance and the I10 _ both are just outside the 4- bounds.

The situation is even more confused for IC10. We noted that many of the solutions with M31 distance near its upper bound place _ for I10 near its lower bound. The solutions plotted in black that have the better M31 distances have no better fit to the measured proper motion of I10.

sources.summary: The paper reports a 3 mm spectral survey of IC10, detecting several molecular lines and finding LMC-like low-metallicity chemistry with deficient nitrogen species, high CCH, and weak CH3OH.

quotes.quote: We have conducted a spectral line survey observation in the 3 mm band toward the low-metallicity dwarf galaxy IC10 with the 45 m radio telescope of Nobeyama Radio Observatory to explore its chemical composition at a molecular-cloud scale ( 80 pc). The CS, SO, CCH, HCN, HCO ^+ , and HNC lines are detected for the first time in this galaxy in addition to the CO and ^ 13 CO lines, while C _3 H _2 , CH _3 OH, CN, C ^ 18 O, and N _2 H ^+ lines are not detected. The spectral intensity pattern is found to be similar to those observed toward molecular clouds in the Large Magellanic Cloud, whose metallicity is as low as IC10. Nitrogen-bearing species are deficient in comparison with the Galactic molecular clouds due to a lower elemental abundance of nitrogen. CCH is abundant in comparison with Galactic translucent clouds, whereas CH _3 OH may be deficient.

The molecular-cloud-scale chemical composition of IC10 is found to be very similar to that of the LMC clouds. It is characterized by deficiency of N-bearing molecules, relatively high abundant CCH, and deficiency of CH _3 OH.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 10 as a nearby starburst dwarf galaxy whose radio-continuum observations are complicated by Galactic-plane sidelobes and low-frequency absorption.

quotes.quote: IC 10, NGC 1569, and NGC 4449 are nearby starbursting dwarf galaxies. They have properties that are rather similar to those of BCDGs. They are gas-rich, too, but their metallicities are not so low.

Because of its proximity to the Galactic plane, IC 10 is a tricky case for radio continuum observations. In particular at low frequencies, at which interferometric observations are indispensable, imaging suffers from contamination by spurious sidelobes from radio continuum structures in the Galactic plane. Another worry is thermal absorption through the plane at the lowest frequencies. Useful measurements for our purpose have been carried out klein83a , klein86 , chyzy03 , and by 2016ApJ...819...39C .

sources.summary: The paper adopts IC 10 proper motion, distance, and halo properties, building an NFW profile and deriving a 48 km/s maximum circular velocity for its halo.

quotes.quote: Measuring a new distance to IC 10 was not possible due to high extinction, so we adopt the mcquinn17 distance based on the tip of the red giant branch.

IC 10 We use the PM of IC 10, the furthest satellite galaxy considered in this work, as measured with the VLBA brunthaler07 . To determine the maximum circular velocity of IC 10's dark matter halo, we adopt the following properties from Table 2 of oh15 : R_ max , M_ dyn (R_ max ) , and M_ 200 . We first convert M_ 200 to virial units, which yields a virial mass of =1.9 10^ 10 M_ .

Then, we subtract the stellar and gaseous masses from and use this mass to construct an NFW profile. We vary the concentration of the NFW profile, until the NFW profile's enclosed mass at R_ max is equivalent to M_ dyn (R_ max ) . The best-fitting NFW profile results in a maximum circular velocity of 48 km s ^ 1 for IC 10's halo.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a low-latitude dwarf-galaxy overdensity as the dense part of IC 10, reports its distance and Gaia proper motion, and compares that motion with VLBA results.

quotes.quote: Meanwhile, when we analyzed the results, we noticed a dwarf galaxy located at ( l = 118.97 deg, b = -3.33 deg) (Figure  fig:dwarf a), with a radius of approximately 0.05 deg (core member) to 0.1 deg (outer member). When we cross-matched the simbad database  http://simbad.cds.unistra.fr/simbad/sim-fid , we found that the object is the dense part of IC 10, a local dwarf galaxy located at a distance of approximately 660 kpc  Huchra99,Borissova2000 , which has been discovered 135 years ago  Swift1888 . As a result, it also displays a unique distribution in the k _ th NND histogram (Figure  fig:dwarf b). Although the object have a median parallax of - 0.02 mas ( _ = 0.13 mas), which is not advantageous for distance measurement, the proper motion values of IC 10 are still useful.

The median proper motion of _ ^ = 0.02 mas yr ^ 1 ( _ _ ^ = 0.15 mas yr ^ 1 ), _ = - 0.09 mas yr ^ 1 ( _ _ = 0.18 mas yr ^ 1 ), it shows a tangent velocity of approximately 280  , which is also comparable to its radial velocity  RV = -348  McConnachie12 . Although previous observations of the proper motion of IC 10 through VLBA showed a smaller value  _ ^ = -0.039 0.009 mas yr ^ 1 , _ = 0.031 0.008 mas yr ^ 1 Brunthaler06 . However, the difference between the two values is not significant.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC10 as a Local Group dwarf irregular/starburst galaxy, discusses its distance, extinction, star formation, and revises its planetary-nebula luminosity function distance.

quotes.quote: Adopting the value of 12+ (O/H) = 8.19, [ O/H ] = 0.68 and M_ IC10,*5007 = 0.29, respectively for \ we expect the `corrected' cutoff magnitude for the \ PNLF to be M_ *5007 = 4 18 . Thus, the calculated M _ *5007 for PN 7 appears 1 15 brighter than the standard value. After the rejection of the PN 7 candidate as an H ii region based on the results of our observations, the PNLF for PN candidates in \ is shown in Figure  fig:PNLF_IC10 , where data from Magr03 have been binned into 0 6 intervals.

With this small sample we obtained the distance modulus (m-M) = 24.30^ +0.18 _ 0.10 ( including the error in M_ *5007 ), corresponding to a distance D = 725 ^ +63 _ 33 kpc; this is very close to the value from Dem04 . This distance modulus is also consistent with the recent estimate (m-M) = 24.48 0.18 in Vac07 , obtained from the study of individual RGB stars. With the PN data in hand, a distance to \ of 950 Mpc MA95, H01 seems implausible.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC10 as a nearby Local Group starburst/blue compact dwarf and maps its metallicity from HII regions and planetary nebulae, finding non-homogeneous metals and only modest global enrichment.

quotes.quote: The irregular galaxy IC10 is the nearest starburst galaxy, sometimes defined as the closest example of a blue compact dwarf richer01 . It is located in the outskirts of the Local Group (LG), at low Galactic latitude. Its location implies quite uncertain determinations of its reddening and distance.

The direct availability of the electron temperature diagnostics in several nebulae allowed an accurate determination of the metallicity map of IC10 at two epochs: the present-time from \ regions and the old/intermediate-age from PNe. We found a non-homogeneous distribution of metals at both epochs, but similar average abundances were found for the two populations. The derived age-metallicity relation shows a little global enrichment interpreted as the loss of metals by SN winds and to differential gas outflows.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 10 as the Local Group starburst case, but argues its current burst may be visually dramatic rather than transformative over gigayear timescales.

quotes.quote: The gas-rich dwarf (M _B = -15.6 ) IC 10 has long been considered the only starburst galaxy in the Local Group and thought of as something of an anomaly e.g. hun01 . IC 10 has an unusually high surface brightness for a galaxy of its size, and unusually high numbers of Wolf-Rayet stars mas98 , owing to its high current SFR of 0.03 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 hun01 . At a distance of 800 kpc from the Milky Way (and a mere 250 kpc from M31), IC 10 is clearly an outlier among the dwarf irregular galaxies of the Local Group, but how much of an outlier is still a matter of debate.

The lack of bright main-sequence stars in this specific field is reflected in the low SFR over the past 400 Myr. However, this apparently quiescent field far from the current center of starburst activity experienced its own peaks in SFR at ages of approximately 700--800 Myr and 1.5--2.5 Gyr. During both time periods, the specific SFR at this location was higher than the current galaxy-wide specific SFR of 0.03 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 hun01 , marked with a star at 10 Myr and extended for reference back to 14 Gyr by the dashed line.

sources.summary: The paper includes IC10 as a Local Group proper-motion constraint, but says its modeled motion is uncertain and sensitive to the still-uncertain orbit of M31.

quotes.quote: The model also fits the proper motions of M33 and IC10. Analyzing the past orbit of the Magellanic Clouds relative to the Milky Way is an interesting problem in dynamics, and it may be a useful preliminary exercise for analyses of ongoing advances in measurements of galaxy distances and proper motions that will test ideas about the evolving mass distribution around galaxies.

The galaxies M33 and IC10 are in this analysis because their proper motions (Brunthaler et al. 2005; Brunthaler et al. 2007) are important constraints. The stated uncertainties in the measured proper motions are treated as standard deviations.

In these two models the components of proper motion of IC10 differ from the measurement by less than two times the stated error, which seems acceptable, and the other proper motions are closer to the measurements. The distance of IC10 in model 2 is large, but obscuration complicates this measurement.

sources.summary: The paper studies an intense star-forming region in IC 10, describing the galaxy as the Local Group's only known starburst, a low-mass BCDG with recent galaxy-wide star formation likely triggered by gas infall.

quotes.quote: Here, we present an optical 2D spectroscopical ana lysis of a particularly interesting and intense star-forming region within the nearest starburst, the galaxy IC 10. Although our main aim is to look for a localized chemical enrichment within the \ region as a consequence of the massive stars, we will also analyze the extinction, ionization structure, chemical abundances and age of the most recent star-formation burst in this particular area of IC 10.

Sometimes defined as an irregular galaxy, IC 10 (Fig. mapa ) actually is the only known starburst within the Local Group. Because of its low mass Mateo98 , high star-formation activity i.e., HL90, Leroy06 , low metallicity -- =8.26, -, its very extended, clumpy, and peculiar distribution of neutral gas Juette et al., in prep; ShostakSkillman89, WilcotsMiller98 , high far-infrared emission MelisseIsrael94 , non-thermal radio-continuum emission YangSkillman93 , and the relative importance of its young stellar population i.e., Sakai99, Borissova00, Hunter01, Vacca+07 which includes a large number of WR stars Massey92, MasseyJohnson98, MasseyHolmes02 , IC 10 is usually classified as a BCDG Richer+01 . The high carbon-rich WR stars to nitrogen-rich WR stars ratio in IC 10 (the WC/WN ratio), which is unusually high for its metallicity Crowther03 , suggests that the galaxy has experience a brief but intense galaxy-wide burst of star-formation within the last 10 Myr. The starburst was probably triggered by gas falling in from the large, extended \ reservoir Saito92, WilcotsMiller98 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 10 as the strongest-field Local Group dwarf in its sample, with slightly resolved radio emission, a massive starburst, gas-compression synchrotron emission, and blue compact dwarf properties.

quotes.quote: IC 10. -- The map of total intensity at 2.64 GHz (Fig. ic10t ) clearly detects, slightly resolved, and elongated radio emission. It appears to be the strongest (250 mJy) source in our sample. The small extension to the west is caused by a weak (9 mJy) background source, also visible in the NVSS map at 1.4 GHz.

IC 10 is experiencing a massive starburst, apparently triggered by infalling H i gas from the southeast (Grebel ). The synchrotron emission detected in this portion of the galaxy indicates gas compression (Chy zy et al. ). According to several authors (see Grebel and references therein), the properties of this galaxy suggest that it should be classified as a blue compact dwarf.

sources.summary: The paper presents EVLA radio-continuum observations of IC 10, describing its post-starburst dwarf irregular nature, radio/H-alpha correspondence, steep nonthermal emission, and suitability for dwarf-galaxy magnetic-field studies.

quotes.quote: We exploit the vastly increased sensitivity of the Expanded Very Large Array (EVLA) to study the radio continuum and polarization properties of the post--starburst, dwarf irregular galaxy IC 10 at 6 cm, at a linear resolution of 50 pc . We find close agreement between radio continuum and H emission, from the brightest H 2 regions to the weaker emission in the disk. A quantitative analysis shows a strictly linear correlation, where the thermal component contributes 50 to the total radio emission, the remainder being due to a non--thermal component with a surprisingly steep radio spectral index of between 0.7 and 1.0 suggesting substantial radiation losses of the cosmic--ray electrons.

IC 10 is a perfect candidate for an in depth study of a dwarf galaxy at radio continuum wavelengths because it is relatively bright and nearby, being a member of the Local Group san08a . It has a high star formation rate and a high density of Wolf--Rayet stars and is regarded to be in a starburst phase mas02a,crow03a . The aftermath of massive star formation leaves its imprint on the ISM in the form of expanding bubbles and shells that can be traced in H 1 and H .

sources.summary: The paper highlights IC 10 as an actively star-forming M31 satellite with broad distance scatter from extinction, and cites newer TRGB work supporting M31-subgroup membership.

quotes.quote: Among the M31 satellite galaxies, IC 10 plays a key role, since it is a very actively star-forming galaxy. It is, in fact, considered the only analog to the so-called ``post-starburst'' dwarf galaxies in the Local Group (Gil de Paz et al. 2003). However, it is located at low Galactic latitude, and its photometry is severely affected by foreground extinction. This partially explains the broad range in distance estimates associated with IC 10.

More recently, Sanna et al. (2008)---using a new calibration of the TRGB method and very accurate HST /Advanced Camera for Surveys photometry---derived new estimates of both the distance, (m-M)_0^ IC 10 = 24.56 0.08 (statistical) 0.08 (systematic) mag, and the galaxy's reddening, E(B-V)= 0.78 0.06 mag. This, in turn, supports additional extant evidence that IC 10 is a likely member of the M31 subgroup.

sources.summary: The paper studies IC 10 as a nearby dwarf starburst galaxy, using Chandra and XMM-Newton monitoring to characterize its variable X-ray binary population and young starburst environment.

quotes.quote: We have monitored the Cassiopeia dwarf galaxy (IC 10) in a series of 10 ACIS-S observations to capture its variable and transient X-ray source population, which is expected to be dominated by High Mass X-ray Binaries (HMXBs). We present a sample of 21 X-ray sources that are variable between observations at the 3 level, from a catalog of 110 unique point sources. We find 4 transients (flux variability ratio greater than 10) and a further 8 objects with ratio > 5.

The observations span years 2003 - 2010 and reach a limiting luminosity of >10^ 35 , providing sensitivity to X-ray binaries in IC 10 as well as flare stars in the foreground Milky Way. The nature of the variable sources is investigated from light-curves, X-ray spectra, energy quantiles, and optical counterparts. IC 10 provides a sharp contrast in stellar population age ( < 10 My) when compared to the Magellanic Clouds (40-200 My) where most of the known HMXBs reside.

sources.summary: The review describes IC 10 as a blue compact dwarf satellite of M31, with disturbed H I, inflowing nearly starless gas, and substantial gas mass inside M31's CGM.

quotes.quote: IC 10 This blue compact dwarf galaxy, a satellite of M31, is undergoing a burst of star formation and has a complex \ structure with counter-rotating components and two tails or streamers shown in Figure fig:IC10 Shostak1989,Nidever2013,Ashley2014 . Kinematic analysis of the \ suggests that the gas is inflowing rather than outflowing.

Its stellar component, however, does not show evidence of streams or shells, or any kind of disturbance, though the young stellar populations are spatially offset from the older stellar population Gerbrandt2015 . The data thus suggest that IC 10 is accreting nearly starless gas and not a companion. The mass in the southern and northern \ features is 10^7 \ and 6 10^5 , respectively, out of a total \ for the entire system of 8 10^7 .

With a stellar mass of 8.6 10^7 \ McConnachie2012 , IC 10 has a M_ HI /L_V 1 , similar to gas-rich Local Group dwarfs, even though it is only 250 kpc from M31 and thus is not only a satellite of M31, but lies well within the CGM of that galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper studies IC10's evolved stellar population, deriving its reddening and distance, finding carbon stars dominate bright evolved sources, and linking star-formation history to dust production.

quotes.quote: We study the evolved stellar population of the Local Group galaxy IC10, with the aim of characterizing the individual sources observed and to derive global information on the galaxy, primarily the star formation history and the dust production rate.

From the analysis of the distribution of stars in the observational planes obtained with IR bands, we find that the reddening and distance of IC10 are E(B-V)=1.85 mag and d=0.77 Mpc, respectively. The evolved stellar population is dominated by carbon stars, that account for 40 of the sources brighter than the tip of the red giant branch. Most of these stars descend from 1.1-1.3 M_ progenitors, formed during the major epoch of star formation, which occurred 2.5 Gyr ago. The presence of a significant number of bright stars indicates that IC10 has been site of significant star formation in recent epochs and currently hosts a group of massive stars in the core helium-burning phase. Dust production in this galaxy is largely dominated by carbon stars; the overall dust production rate estimated is 7 10^ 6 M_ /yr.

sources.summary: The paper uses IC10's maser proper motion to show that in-plane orbits are ruled out at least at 3 sigma, making plane membership unlikely.

quotes.quote: Proper Motion of IC10 The existence of a maser in IC10 allowed the authors of 2007A&A...462..101B to measure the PM of this dwarf galaxy: _ , IC10 =& -20 5 _ , IC10,exp =& +31 8 . We have shown the value of this measurement in both Figs. theplot and theotherplot . The 1 , 2 and 3 error-ellipsoids are the result of combining the error bars from the PM of M31 and PM of IC10 in quadrature.

For heavier M31, Fig. theplot , the bound orbit is within 2 and technically still not ruled out. However, for light M31, all bound orbits with apsis less than 500kpc are ruled out by 3 . In both scenarios, all in-plane orbits are ruled out by at least 3 and therefore it is safe to conclude the IC10 is unlikely to be a member of the plane.

sources.summary: The paper includes IC10 as a dwarf irregular target with strong rotation-curve support and discusses how its NFW and Burkert fits yield appreciably different generalized J factors.

quotes.quote: According to the observed RCs, NGC6822, IC10 and WLM are the ones with more available data, thus in these cases the fits are more robust and stable than for the rest of objects in their sample. The mentioned three objects also yield the highest J-factor values independently of the selected DM profile or substructure boost values. Taking these findings in into account, we thus decided to include NGC6822, IC10 and WLM in our sample, whose parameters are gathered in Table tab:dIrr .

However, for IC10, the best fit using the NFW profile differs more appreciably from the Burkert one: (i) in the Coulomb regime (small _ values), R _ J_ S 3 for both the s and p wave cases; (ii) in the saturation regime off-resonance (intermediate _ ), and in the standard regime with no enhancement (large _ ), R _ J_ S 5 for s wave while R _ J_ S 10 for p wave; (iii) on resonance, R _ J_ S 1 for s wave while R _ J_ S 5 for p wave.

In fig:comparison_host_halo , we compare the J factors (as a function of _ ) for a selection of representative objects among the target classes considered in this work, namely Reticulum II (ultra-faint dSph), Draco (classical dSph), IC10 (dIrr), and Fornax (galaxy cluster). To ease the comparison, we highlight our estimated uncertainties as shaded/hatched areas, although we remind that the different bands neither have the same origin nor the same statistical meaning (see discussion in ssec:host_uncertainties ). For s wave annihilation (left panel), the uncertainty bands overlap in the regime with no Sommerfeld enhancement (large _ values) for Fornax and IC10, while this overlap disappears and is replaced by a gap for decreasing _ ; there is for instance almost a factor 10 difference between the Fornax lower edge (black-hatched band) and IC10 upper edge (gray-hatched band) in the Coulomb regime (small _ values).

sources.summary: The paper uses IC 10 as one of six isolated Local Group dwarfs with extended stellar-halo observations, fitting ghostly-halo models to infer pre-reionization star-formation efficiency.

quotes.quote: We compare the model to available observations of stellar haloes in six isolated dwarf galaxies in the Local Group (Leo T, Leo A, IC 10, WLM, IC 1613, NGC 6822) to infer the star formation efficiency in dwarf galaxies at the epoch of reionization. We find an efficiency of star formation in dark matter haloes with masses 10^6-10^8 M _ at z 7 in rough agreement with independent methods using data on the luminosity function of ultra-faint dwarf galaxies but systematically higher by a factor of 3-5. The systematic uncertainty of our results is still large, mainly because available observations of stellar halo profiles do not extend over a sufficiently large distance from the center of the host dwarf galaxy. Additional observations, easily within reach of current telescopes, can significantly improve the accuracy of this method and can also be used to constrain the present day dark matter masses of dwarf galaxies in the Local Group.

KangR:2019 have compiled a list of six dwarf galaxies (Leo T, Leo A, WLM, IC 1613, IC 10, and NGC 6822) located in the Local Group, but outside the virial radii of the Milky Way and Andromeda, showing robust or tentative evidence of the existence of an extended stellar halo. In the collected observational data sets, radial density profiles of stars are provided out to several half-light radii from the central galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 10 as a prime Local Group VLBA maser target, measures its proper motion, and combines that with radial velocity to obtain its space motion.

quotes.quote: The most suitable candidates for such a VLBI phase-referencing experiment are the strong H _2 O masers in IC 10 ( 10 Jy peak flux in 0.5 km s ^ 1 line) and IC 133 in M33 ( 2 Jy, the first extragalactic maser discovered) . The two galaxies belong to the brightest members of the Local Group and are thought to be associated with M31. In both cases a relatively bright phase-referencing source is known to exist within a degree. In addition, their galactic rotation is well known from H I observations. Consequently, M33 and IC 10 seem to be the best known targets for attempting to measure Local Group proper motions with the VLBA. Observations and Data Reduction We observed two regions of H _2 O maser activity in M33 (M33/19 and IC 133) eight times with the NRAO Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) between March 2001 and June 2005 . M33/19 is located in the south-eastern part of M33, while IC 133 is located in the north-east of M33 (see Fig. m33_pred ). We observed the usually brightest maser in IC 10-SE with the VLBA thirteen times between February 2001 and June 2005 .

Proper Motion of IC 10 In IC 10 only the strongest maser component was detected in all epochs. The position offsets of this maser feature in IC 10 are shown in Fig. pos_ic10 . The uncertainties in the observations of the first epoch are larger than the others, because no geodetic-like observations were done to compensate the zenith delay errors. A rectilinear motion was fit to the data and yielded a value of 6 5 as yr ^ 1 toward the East and 23 5 as yr ^ 1 toward the North. Once again, the contributions v_ rot and v_ can be calculated from the known motion of the Sun and the known rotation of IC 10 . The true proper motion of IC 10 is then given by: _ IC 10 &=& _ prop _ rot _ &=&-39 9 as yr ^ 1 =-122 31 km s ^ 1 and _ IC 10 &=& _ prop _ rot _ &=&31 8 as yr ^ 1 =97 27 km s ^ 1 The measured systematic heliocentric velocity of IC 10 ( 344 3 km s ^ 1 , ) is the sum of the radial motion of IC 10 toward the Sun and the component of the solar motion about the Galactic Center toward IC 10 which is -196 10 km s ^ 1 . Hence, IC 10 is moving with 148 10 km s ^ 1 toward the Sun.

sources.summary: The paper measures IC 10 maser proper motions with VLBA, combines them with radial velocity to obtain its space motion, and uses possible binding to M31 to constrain M31 mass.

quotes.quote: We observed the usually brightest maser in IC 10-SE with the VLBA thirteen times between February 2001 and June 2005 . The motions of 4 components in M33/19 and 6 components in IC 133 could be followed over all epochs. The component identification was based on the positions and radial velocities of the maser emission. A rectilinear motion was fit to each maser feature in each velocity channel separately. Then, the variance weighted average of all motions was calculated. This yields an average motion of the maser components in M33/19 of 35.5 2.7 as yr ^ 1 in right ascension and 12.5 6.3 as yr ^ 1 in declination relative to the background source J0137+312. For IC 133 one gets an average motion of 4.7 3.2 as yr ^ 1 in right ascension and 14.1 6.4 as yr ^ 1 in declination. The observed proper motion of a maser region in M33 can be decomposed into the motion of the masers due to the internal galactic rotation in M33, the apparent motion of M33 caused by the rotation of the Sun around the Galactic Center, and the true proper motion of M33 relative to the Galaxy. Since the motion of the Sun and the rotation of M33 are known, one can calculate the true proper motion of M33: 101 35 km s ^ 1 in right ascension and 156 47 km s ^ 1 in declination, relative to the center of the Milky Way. Finally, the systemic radial velocity of M33 is 179 km s ^ 1 . The radial component of the rotation of the Milky Way toward M33 is 140 9 km s ^ 1 . Hence, M33 is moving with 39 9 km s ^ 1 toward the Milky Way. Combining the proper motions and radial velocities, gives the three dimensional velocity vector of M33. The total velocity of M33 relative to the Milky Way is 190 59 km s ^ 1 . In IC 10 only the strongest maser component was detected in all epochs. Rectilinear motion was fit to the data and yielded a value of 6 5 as yr ^ 1 toward the East and 23 5 as yr ^ 1 toward the North. Once again, the contributions of the known motion of the Sun and the known rotation of IC 10 can be calculated. The true proper motion of IC 10 is 122 31 km s ^ 1 in right ascension and 97 27 km s ^ 1 in declination. The measured systematic heliocentric velocity of IC 10 ( 344 3 km s ^ 1 ) is the sum of the radial motion of IC 10 toward the Sun and the component of the solar motion about the Galactic Center toward IC 10 which is 196 10 km s ^ 1 . Hence, IC 10 is moving with 148 10 km s ^ 1 toward the Sun. The proper motion and the radial velocity combined give the three-dimensional space velocity of IC 10. The total velocity is 215 42 km s ^ 1 relative to the Milky Way. If IC 10 or M33 are bound to M31, then the velocity of the two galaxies relative to M31 must be smaller than the escape velocity and one can deduce a lower limit on the mass of M31: M_ M31 > v_ rel ^2R 2G . A relative velocity of 147 km s ^ 1 - for a zero tangential motion of M31 -- and a distance of 262 kpc between IC 10 and M31 gives a lower limit of 6.6 10^ 11 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper describes IC10 as a low-metallicity, nearby marginal starburst with many Wolf-Rayet stars, motivating a hotter no-AGN hot-core chemistry model for the galaxy.

quotes.quote: Galaxies of the type of IC10 are also interesting to study. Garn98b found a value of IC 10 metallicity 1 5 z _ . This source is usually considered as one of the closest starburst sources in the Universe even if IC 10 has been recently recognized as only marginally starburst Hida05 . Adding the presence of numerous Wolf-Rayet stars in IC 10, we could however suppose a temperature higher than in quiet galaxy. Consequently, a model with a z= 1 5 z _ , temperature of hot core of 500 K, = 1 (no AGN) appears appropriate.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC10 as a Local Group barred irregular galaxy with rapid star formation, many Wolf-Rayet stars, and low metallicity adopted as Z=0.3 solar.

quotes.quote: IC10 is a barred irregular galaxy in the Local Group at the distance between 600 and 800 kpc (Saha et al. 1996; Sakai, Madore Freeman, 1999). It is undergoing a very rapid star formation and has a very high number of Wolf Rayet stars. IC10 has low metallicity: Lequeux et al. (1997) estimates it to be Z=0.15 Z_ , but later studies by Massey et al. (2007) place it somewhere between the values for LMC and SMC. Thus we conservatively adopt the value of Z=0.3 Z_ . IC10 is a galaxy with a large star formation rate and it contains more than a hundred WR stars (Massey Holmes, 2002)

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 10 as a Local Group star-forming dwarf projected behind FSR 486, with non-overlapping stellar sequences and unusual properties possibly affected by Galactic contamination.

quotes.quote: We point out that FSR 486 is projected towards the Local Group star forming galaxy IC 10 (Fig. fig:12 ), which has been studied with HST data Sanna10 . Comparing the CMDs and considering the different photometric bands of both studies, the CMD sequences of the star cluster and galaxy hardly overlap (Fig. fig:13 ). The set of Wolf-Rayet and O stars from IC 10 are too faint to be present in the 2MASS photometry see http://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-fcoo .

The derived distance modulus to IC 10 in previous works is larger than 24 mag Borissova00, Hunter01, Sanna08, Gonçalves12 . However, we derive a distance modulus of 14 mag for FSR 486. We also point out the possible presence of an HII region where the EC FSR 486 is forming. IC 10 presents unusual gas kinematics, star formation and physical parameters and references therein Cohen79, Bolatto00, Sanna10 , but part of this scenario may be a contamination by the Galaxy.

sources.summary: The model includes IC 10 because of measured proper motion; its mass is near catalog, removing it disrupts tracer fits, and its distance may be uncertain.

quotes.quote: IC 10 and M 33 are even less luminous, but since they are particularly interesting because they have measured proper motions they are included among the twelve massive actors. Since their model masses are close to catalog values I suspect they could have been treated as massless with little effect on the model. (This is difficult to check without repeating the whole computation, because setting the masses of IC 10 and M 33 to zero in the present solution seriously disturbs the redshifts and distances of some of the tracers, though it only modestly affects the other massive actors.)
The low galactic latitude of IC 10 might make its distance more uncertain. In LU its distance is 0.79 0.04 Mpc, and in NED the mean is 0.88 Mpc.

sources.summary: The paper calls IC 10 a surprising dwarf-galaxy WR case, with many confirmed candidates despite small size, low metallicity, a high WC fraction, and still-uncertain current WR statistics.

quotes.quote: A search for WR stars in the dwarf galaxy IC 10 proved the most surprising of any of these early studies. Despite its small size, 16 WR candidates were initially found by Massey, Armandroff, and Conti , 15 of which were quickly confirmed , causing the authors to recognize this as the nearest starburst galaxy. Despite the galaxy's low metallicity, the relative proportion of WC stars was very large.

Clearly the biggest improvements have come about for M31 and IC10. However, even for IC 10 the results are still very uncertain, with finding many additional candidates that have not yet been certified by spectroscopy, and finding a small number which have also not yet been observed.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 10 as a Local Group blue compact dwarf and reports 20 detected H I holes, mostly Type 1, revising earlier work.

quotes.quote: IC 10 IC 10, PGC 1305, or UGC 192 is a well-studied Local Group blue compact dwarf galaxy. It is located in the constellation Cassiopeia, at a distance of 0.7 Mpc, with V-band magnitude 16.3 . first studied the holes in IC 10 and found eight holes. Based on our criteria, we detected 20 holes, most of which are Type 1. Our analysis shows that the large holes discovered by contain two or more smaller holes and some of them are overlapped.

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 10 as a starburst dwarf irregular galaxy, but excludes it from further analysis because foreground extinction causes a poor CMD fit.

quotes.quote: IC 10 is known as a starburst dwarf irregular galaxy. In this work, as shown in the extended version of Figure fig:SFH_NGC6822 , the residual CMD indicates a significant mismatch between the observed CMD and best fit CMD, so IC 10 is not included in further analysis. The Galactic latitudes of IC 10 is 3^ , hence a large foreground extinction A_ V 4.3 . The large and inhomogeneous extinction can result in such a significant mismatch. This problem would be solved by building a 2D extinction map and performing a more accurate extinction correction for IC 10.

sources.summary: The review discusses IC 10 as a nearby dwarf galaxy with direct H I evidence for gas accretion, large-scale inflow, disturbed disk kinematics, and stream impact sites.

quotes.quote: An example of this is in IC 10, which is a dwarf galaxy at a distance of 0.7 Mpc ashley14,namumba19 . Figure fig-IC10 a from ashley14 shows an optical image with \ from the Karl G. \ Jansky Very Large Array superimposed and Figure fig-IC10 b shows the \ velocities with the near and far sides of the galaxy indicated along with the approaching and receding components of . The nearside has gas receding from us, which means it is accreting onto the galaxy. A larger scale view from the Green Bank Telescope is in Figure fig-IC10 from ashley14 , which shows \ to much larger distances following the same sense of motion, i.e., approaching the galaxy on both the near (red color) and far (blue color) sides. These accretions apparently agitate the \ in the disk also, as shown in Figure fig-IC10 d which plots the \ velocity dispersion.

sources.summary: The Euclid paper presents IC 10 as a Local Group dwarf irregular starburst, difficult to observe through foreground extinction, with clusters, an X-ray binary, and an interacting H I envelope.

quotes.quote: IC 10 is a dwarf irregular member of the Local Group, considered to be the closest example of a starburst galaxy, and a likely member of the Andromeda subgroup vandenbergh99 . Its location at low Galactic latitude behind 4 magnitudes of visual extinction (see Table tab:sample ) makes it challenging to study at UV and optical wavelengths. IC 10 hosts numerous star clusters e.g., hunter01,lim15 and possibly the most massive known stellar-mass black hole, associated with a highly variable ultra-luminous X-ray binary, IC 10 X--1 silverman08 . IC 10 X--1 may be powering a large non-thermal superbubble, possibly also associated with an \ cavity heesen15,heesen18 . The galaxy is embedded within a huge \ envelope that shows signs of interaction and possibly late merger with another dwarf galaxy e.g., wilcots98,nidever13,ashley14,namumba19 .

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 10 as a massive irregular whose red giants reveal a thick disk, sharp boundary, and larger halo-like structure unlike the lower-mass irregulars.

quotes.quote: The ratio of the sizes of the corresponding spatial structures can reach five (IC 10), but its mean value is 2.5--3.0. The domains occupied by red giants have fairly welldefined boundaries, with very few, mostly foreground, stars seen beyond them. An analysis of the density distribution of red giants in IC 10 revealed a thick disk with a sharp boundary [ 15 ] . An even larger structure extending beyond the thick disk has a different density gradient than the thick disk. Thus, this galaxy exhibits a complex structure consisting of a thick disk and halo, similar to the structure of spiral galaxies [ 19, 20 ] .

sources.summary: The paper finds IC 10 has single-power-law H I power spectra from 50 pc to 2 kpc, suggesting a line-of-sight depth comparable to its transverse scales.

quotes.quote: For the two nearest galaxies in our sample, IC 10 and IC 1613, the power spectra also can be well fitted with a single power law between linear scales of 50 pc and 2 kpc. \ IC 1613 has a tidal index 0.9 (Karachentsev et al. \ 2004), which indicates that this galaxy has been significantly influenced by the neighboring galaxies (e.g. \ M 33). \ Our kinematics analysis (Oh et al. \ in preparation) suggests that these two galaxies, and also the very low luminosity galaxies, such as DDO 210 and DDO 155, have ratios of the maximum rotational velocity to velocity dispersion 2, which indicates that the gas distribution may be a relatively thick disk or even triaxial ellipsoid.

The power spectra of IC 1613 and IC 10, which are the two nearest galaxies in our sample, can be well fitted with a single power law between linear scales of 50 pc and 2 kpc. \ This suggests that the line-of-sight depth may be comparable with the maximum transverse scales in these two galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC10 as a nearby dwarf starburst galaxy rich in young massive stellar populations, HII structures, Wolf-Rayet stars, and X-ray point sources.

quotes.quote: The dwarf starburst galaxy IC10 provides a unique setting to study X-ray binaries containing youngest and most massive stars. According to the IC 10 starburst peaked 7 10^ 6 yrs ago and the galaxy is filled with diffuse HII emission, HII regions, wind-bubbles, and an unusually high space-density of Wolf-Rayet stars bauerbrandt2004 . Young stellar populations such as in IC 10, host massive stars that are rapidly approaching the end of their main sequence lifetimes.
IC 10 contains 100 X-ray point sources, whose combined spectrum is indicative of a population of High Mass X-ray Binaries wang2005 .

sources.summary: IC 10 is identified as a luminous Local Group galaxy without confirmed globular clusters, despite many catalogued star clusters, H I gas, and a high halo-mass estimate.

quotes.quote: IC 10 and IC 1613 IC 10 ( M_V = -15.0 ) and IC 1613 ( M_V = -15.2 ) appear to be the highest luminosity Local Group galaxies (excluding special case of M32) without a single GC. We note that a number (66) of star clusters have been catalogued in IC 10 by 2015ApJ...804..123L but it is not clear if they are old GC-like. Both galaxies reveal the presence of HI gas 2012AJ....144....4M and have halo masses estimated from HI rotation curves of log M_ 200 = 10.2 for IC 10 and 8.8 for IC 1613 2015AJ....149..180O .
Based on these halo mass estimates we would expect IC 10 and IC 1613 to host several GCs.

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 10 as an M31-radius HI detection, places it 252 kpc from M31, and describes its HI as irregular or disturbed and possibly being removed.

quotes.quote: The HI detections within the 266 kpc radius for M31 are its dwarf elliptical satellites (NGC205 and NGC185) and IC 10. The dwarf ellipticals have smaller amounts of HI relative to their luminosity and dynamical mass compared to other dwarf galaxies (Figures histar and hitotal ; see also geha06 ), and IC 10 is at 252 kpc from M31 and has irregular/disturbed HI, similar to LGS3 at 267 kpc ashley14,hunter12 . This may indicate the HI gas of these galaxies is beginning to be removed.

There are 6 detected exceptions within the surface, 3 of which are the most optically luminous dwarf galaxies in the sample (LMC, SMC, NGC 205) and two others in the top 11 most luminous (IC10 and NGC185).

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 10 as the Local Group exception with an overabundance of water masers, suggesting concentrated central star formation may explain its unusually high detection rate.

quotes.quote: However, the galaxy IC 10 exhibits an overabundance of masers, which may result from a compact central starburst.

The number of masers in all galaxies except IC 10 is consistent with the expected number of maser sources. However, IC 10 shows an overabundance. Possibly, star formation in this galaxy is concentrated in two particular regions near the center of the galaxy, where a small 'starburst' creates conditions different from those expected from an average galaxy containing little molecular gas.

sources.summary: The paper cites IC 10 as having a weak, almost random magnetic field structure and lacking a large-scale field, resembling the mostly random field in NGC 4214.

quotes.quote: The magnetic field structures of NGC 6822 and IC 10 are weak and almost completely random. The lack of a large-scale magnetic field in these galaxies may be caused by a combination of their intense star-formation and their lower rotation velocities .
The mostly random magnetic field structure of NGC 4214 closely resembles the magnetic field structure of NGC 6822 and IC 10.

sources.summary: The paper treats IC 10 as an extragalactic analogue for the Galactic Center lobe, describing its dwarf irregular starburst outflow, ionized nonthermal radio lobe, nonexpanding superbubble, energy, and age.

quotes.quote: A good extragalactic analogue of the GC lobe might be the outflow in the nearest starburst galaxy, the dwarf irregular IC 10 t05 . That outflow is composed of a nonthermal radio continuum lobe with a diameter of about 200 pc that is filled with ionized gas y93,t05 . The IC 10 superbubble is also similar to the GC lobe in that it has no clear sign of expansion; it has a kinetic (turbulent) energy of 5 10^ 52 ergs and age of roughly 7 10^6 years.

sources.summary: The paper compares a simulated merger remnant to IC10, emphasizing disturbed gas and stars, multiple star-forming cores, extended H I structure, and low-metallicity H II regions.

quotes.quote: The system depicted in Fig. merger-lessradial-highC shows a distribution of gas and stars that shares characteristics with the irregular dwarf galaxy IC10: a disturbed gas and stellar distribution with multiple star-forming cores and an extended HI distribution with plums and spurs with velocities that differ from that of the main gas disk see for example Ashleyetal2014 . On the other hand, the HII regions have a low metallicity Garnett1990 which has been suggested as being due to the influx of fresh pristine gas from the environment SanchezAlmeidaetal2014 . However, another interpretation is possible since as we have seen the merger leads to an extended starburst that is fueled from gas that was originally present in the outskirts of the main system, and which presumably also had a lower metallicity see also SHS16 .

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 10 as an isolated irregular galaxy with a large AGB population concentrated centrally, contrasting with more extended RGB and RSG stars.

quotes.quote: Here we present results of published papers for three galaxies: And I and And VII, two dwarf spheroidal and satellite of Andromeda, and IC 10, an irregular and isolated galaxy. ( 2019IAUS..344...70G ) detected around 10000 AGB stars in IC 10 in the area of CCD4 ( 0.07 square-degree). They found that the AGB population concentrated more in the central region of IC 10, while the red giant branch (RGB) and RSG stars are more spread ( mahtab2020 ).

sources.summary: The paper notes elevated H I velocity dispersion for IC10, places it among low-q less-massive galaxies, and quotes it as the very-oblate end of stellar-dominated q values.

quotes.quote: We also included two galaxies NGC 4190 and UGC 8833 from the VLA ACS Nearby Galaxy Survey Treasury (VLA ANGST) survey ott.etal.2012 . Table 1 shows the sample of 20 galaxies, of which first 8 are relatively large, massive spirals (NGC 0628, NGC 3184, NGC 4736, NGC 5194, NGC 5236 and NGC 6946), and the remaining 12 are smaller spiral, dwarf or irregular galaxies. The procedure for determining the velocity dispersion across the galaxy disks as a function of radius is described in ianjamasimanana12 and ianjamasimanana.etal.2017 as well as in das.etal.2020 . We basically co-added the individual velocity profiles over radial bins to obtain azimuthally averaged, high signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) velocity dispersion values for each radial bin. The stacked profiles were fitted with single Gaussian functions, where the half width of the fitted Gaussian represents the velocity dispersion of the gas. This method gives a better estimate of the velocity dispersion compared to the moment2 maps. Figure 4 in the appendix shows the radial variation of the velocity dispersion across the galaxy disks. Its value is less than 10 kms ^ 1 at the R _ 25 radius, except for a few cases (NGC 5194, NGC 4190, DDO 187 and IC 10), where _ H i lies between 11 and 14 kms ^ 1 .

Figure 2 and Table 2 show that galaxies with more massive stellar masses (M(*) > 10 ^9 M _ ) have q values close to q 1 at R_ 25 and beyond (e.g. NGC 628 and NGC 6946). The exceptions are NGC 4214 and NGC 5236. Of these two galaxies, NGC 4214 is a relatively small galaxy and close to being a dwarf olling.1996 . In fact, based on its rotation velocity, stellar mass, and absolute B-band magnitude values, NGC 4214 has been classified as a dwarf galaxy by 2008AJ....136.2782L . But NGC 5236 is quite massive ( M(*) 6.9 10^ 10 M_ and so its low q value is quite surprising. For the less massive galaxies, a large fraction of which are LSB dwarfs, q is much smaller (Figure 2 and table 2). Six of them have q=0.2-0.3 (Holmberg1, M81dwfA, IC10, DDO75, DDO187 and UGC8833) and 3 of them have q=0.4-0.5 (Holmberg2, F564-V3, DDO46). The exception is IC1613 which has a relatively large q 1 value at R_ 25 .

It is interesting to note that the gas rich galaxies (9 of them) all have oblate halos, with q<0.6 but the stellar dominated galaxies have a range of q values with q 0.2 (very oblate) to q 1.25 (prolate). Perhaps the most important result from this study is that the gas rich galaxies all have oblate halos at R_ 25 (Fig. 3). There are 9 gas rich galaxies that have M(gas)/M(baryon>0.5 and they are all dwarf galaxies : Holmberg1, Holmberg2, M81dwfA, DDO46, DDO53, DDO75, DDO187, F564-V3 and UGC8833. The q varies between 0.11 in DDO187 to 0.55 in Holmberg2. The stellar dominated galaxies, however, have a variety of q values ranging from q=0.21 in IC10 to q=1.25 for NGC5194 at R_ 25 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 10 as one of four Local Group disk galaxies selected for in-depth stellar-population gradient work using wide-field and HST imaging plus chemical-evolution modeling.

quotes.quote: As a next step in our study we selected four prominent Local Group disk galaxies, NGC 6822, NGC 3109, IC 1613, and IC 10 to analyze in-depth the radial (in-plane) and vertical (extraplanar) gradients as a function of stellar age in a wide range of galactocentric distances. Our data include wide-field imaging with ground-based telescopes aimed at reaching the old ( 10 Gyr) stellar population, such as horizontal branch (HB) stars --signposts of an ancient stellar population--, and deep pencil beam fields with HST/ACS WFPC2 reaching the old main-sequence turnoffs (based in part on the LCID team data). We are working on the modeling of the star formation history and chemical evolution of the different stellar sub-structures in these galaxies (see also Aparicio et al., Gallart et al., and Hidalgo et al., this meeting).

gmw_00000403.name: J0928+4446
gmw_00000403.overview: J0928+4446 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1505.03394] and a candidate blazar [arXiv:1305.3371, arXiv:1505.03394]. High-resolution observations reveal a core-jet morphology [arXiv:1505.03394] consisting of an inverted-spectrum core [arXiv:1305.3371] and a steep-spectrum, one-sided jet [arXiv:1305.3371, arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: The VLBA study finds J0928+4446 has an inverted-spectrum core, steep one-sided jets, detected core polarization, and rotation measure consistent with a blazar-candidate BAL quasar.

quotes.quote: J0928+4446 : The radio structure in Figure fig:J0928_I is consistent with that obtained by the VLBA Imaging and Polarimetry Survey (VIPS; 2007ApJ...658..203H ) at 5 GHz. The spectral index of each component indicates (see Table tbl:flux ) an inverted-spectrum core (the component A) and steep spectrum one-side jets (the components B-D). Its morphology can be classified as a one-sided structure.

We obtained | RM_ obs | for the core region of J0928+4446 and J1018+0530 as 120 7 rad m ^ 2 and 139 5 rad m ^ 2 , respectively. Then, | RM_ rest | is obtained as 1,010 59 rad m ^ 2 and 1,200 43 rad m ^ 2 for J0928+4446 and J1018+0530, respectively.

sources.summary: 0928+4446 has a flat-spectrum radio core and steep one-sided jet, increased VLBI flux densities, a flat-spectrum radio quasar classification, and core-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: Our 1.7- and 8.4-GHz observations are consistent with those observations showing flat spectrum central component C being a radio core and steep spectrum one-sided jet E. The same results were obtained by VLBA observations made by taka13 . We report however, 50 and 23 increase of the 1.7- and 8.4-GHz flux density, respectively in our observations compared to single dish measurements (Table basic ). This could be connected with the nature of 0928+4446. This source has been classified as flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) by hewe10 and indicated as a good candidate for variable, blazar-type object by taka13 . We classified the morphology of 0928+4446 as a core-jet.

gmw_00000404.name: J1018+0530
gmw_00000404.overview: J1018+0530 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1505.03394] and a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1305.3371]. Its compact parsec-scale radio structure is dominated by an inverted-spectrum core [arXiv:1305.3371, arXiv:1505.03394] and a one-sided jet [arXiv:1305.3371, arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: The paper reports J1018+0530 as a one-sided BAL quasar with an inverted-spectrum core, a parsec-scale jet, detected polarization, and a measured rest-frame rotation measure.

quotes.quote: J1018+0530 : The images at 5 and 8 GHz show an extended emission, which emerges at position angle (PA) of 170 deg (Figure fig:J1018_I ), while the source is unresolved at 1.7 GHz. The pc-scale radio structure is dominated by an inverted-spectrum core (Table tbl:flux ). The extended emission found at 5 and 8 GHz is a jet. Its morphology can be classified as a one-sided structure.

We obtained | RM_ obs | for the core region of J0928+4446 and J1018+0530 as 120 7 rad m ^ 2 and 139 5 rad m ^ 2 , respectively. Then, | RM_ rest | is obtained as 1,010 59 rad m ^ 2 and 1,200 43 rad m ^ 2 for J0928+4446 and J1018+0530, respectively.

sources.summary: 1018+0530 is among the most compact AI sources, with a radio core, probable jet component, flat-spectrum radio quasar classification, and blazar-like variability.

quotes.quote: The brightest component C1 is a radio core and steep spectrum C2 is probably a radio jet. The angular size of 1018+0530 indicates it is one of the most compact objects in our sample of AI sources. 1018+0530 has been classified as flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) by hewe10 . The large flux variations observed in this quasar Gorshkov2008 make it a good candidate for variable, blazar-type object.

gmw_00000405.name: J1159+0112
gmw_00000405.overview: J1159+0112 is a radio-loud broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251] that exhibits a core-dominated morphology [arXiv:1505.03394] and a two-sided, kiloparsec-scale radio jet structure [arXiv:1305.3371]. The source displays radio flux-density variability [arXiv:1203.4509, arXiv:1505.03394] alongside variable absorption profiles [arXiv:0907.2251, arXiv:1407.5998].

sources.summary: The paper treats Q1157+014 as a mini-BAL quasar whose Si IV varies while saturated C IV and N V profiles mostly do not, favoring ionization changes.

quotes.quote: Q1157+014 ( = 2.00 ), mini-BAL system at 3,000 \ ( 1.97 ): wri79 first studied the spectrum of this quasar in detail, and noted that it has C 4 and C 3 ] emission lines (although the \ emission line is absent or extremely weak) and several broad absorption lines such as , N 5 , and C 4 . This system is classified as a BAL by the traditional definition because of the broad absorption profiles of C 4 and N 5 . However, we regard it as a mini-BAL in this study because Si 4 is not so strong as in typical BALs. We collected 5 epochs of observations with VLT/UVES from 2000 -- 2007, covering C 4 , Si 4 , and N 5 at multiple epochs.

Summary: This system shows variability, but mostly in the weaker Si 4 absorption, and not in the saturated C 4 and N 5 . The most plausible explanation for this is that the coverage fraction remains similar, but that the ionization state changes, because a change in the C 4 and N 5 column density won't make a significant difference in the profile if it is saturated e.g., cap12 . This would be consistent with these profiles having changed only in their unsaturated parts. Any strict constraints cannot be placed on density and distance because recombination from/to Si 4 is not simple.

sources.summary: 1159+0112 remained unresolved in the authors' VLBA maps, appears core dominated, shows reported flux-density variability, and lower-frequency imaging reveals extended emission near the core.

quotes.quote: 1159+0112 . This source was observed by us only at 5 and 8.4 GHz with VLBA and remained unresolved (Fig. images2 ). Spectral index ^ 5 _ 8.5 = -0.37 indicates this object is core dominated. bruni12 compared their flux densities measurements of 1159+0112 with those previously reported by monte08 . The higher frequency they contrasts the more significant variability occurred. Nevertheless they could not conclude whether it is intrinsic or due to different resolution (dissimilar VLA configuration).

1159+0112 has been also observed by taka13 on L, C and X band with VLBA. Their radio map on 1.7 GHz proves that this source posses extended emission on lower frequencies and our images at 5 GHz and 8.4 GHz are zoom in version of probable radio core. Interestingly, taka13 detected additional component on C-band in a core proximity - likely a radio jet.

sources.summary: The paper interprets J1159+0112 as a young radio source with a two-sided kiloparsec-scale structure, GHz-peaked core component, steep-spectrum blobs, and hotspot-like jet termination.

quotes.quote: J1159+0112 exhibits an unpolarized gigahertz peaked spectrum component and several discrete blobs with steep spectra on both sides of the central component across 1 kpc. These properties are consistent with those of young radio sources.

J1159+0112 : Table tbl:flux indicates inverted spectrum at the component A, which is a radio core. Additionally, images at 1.7 and 5 GHz show the linear alignment of several discrete components that extend 90 mas towards the southeast and a significant counter feature 50 mas northwest across 1 kpc in total (Figure fig:J1159_I ). The southeast components are consistent with the previous study by . They show steep spectra (Table tbl:flux ) and thus morphology that can be classified as a two-sided structure.
These evidences allow us to infer that the components D and E are hot spots produced by the jet termination by ISM e.g., 1981AJ.....86..833D,1982MNRAS.200..377T .

sources.summary: The paper finds that several star-formation histories can match this DLA galaxy, with the spiral outer disk model giving the minimum-distance favored solution but not excluding alternatives.

quotes.quote: PKS 1157+014, z _ DLA =1.944 Few abundance ratios of interest are available in this DLA system: [ Si/Fe ] , [ Si/Zn ] , [ Mg/Fe ] , [ Ni/Fe ] , and a lower limit to [ N/Si ] . We consider the dust-corrected values given in Table abundances , since they are relatively important in this system with [ Zn/Fe ] = +0.37 0.07 . t The best spiral model is the spiral outer disk model.

This leads again to an important degeneracy between these three models tracing three different star formation histories: an episodic bursting SFH, a continuous SFH, and a single burst SFH, respectively. If we compare the weighted means of the minimal distances between the model curves and the available data points, then the episodic bursting SFH (spiral outer disk model) with the minimum ^2 is the favorite one. However, there is no physical reason to exclude the two other star formation histories, since they all agree with the abundance patterns.

sources.summary: The paper describes J115944.82+011206.9 as a BAL radio-loud quasar with two absorption troughs, double-peaked radio emission, milliarcsecond jet-like structure, and enough X-ray counts for spectral analysis.

quotes.quote: J115944.82+011206.9 (also B1157+014) was identified as a BAL RLQ by Menou et al. (2001), who noted that in addition to the primary low-velocity BAL there is an additional absorption trough near 8000 km s ^ 1 . The depth of this secondary absorption may have increased slightly between the SDSS and HET observations. The radio spectrum appears to be double-peaked (Montenegro-Montes et al. 2008). The source shows symmetric jet-like extended emission on milliarcsecond scales and a one-sided misaligned sequence of faint knots stretching to 100 milliarcseconds (Montenegro-Montes et al. 2009). J115944.82+011206.9 has the lowest m_ i in the snapshot sample and has sufficient X-ray counts ( 170 from 0.5--8 keV ) for basic spectral analysis (Figure 8).

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1159+01 is a BAL QSO with VLBA core-jet morphology, notable 22 GHz flux-density variation, and consistent rotation-measure measurements.

quotes.quote: Two of the unresolved BAL QSOs, 1159+01 and 1624+37, were resolved by VLBA (Montenegro-Montes et al., 2008b, 2012 in preparation), both showing a core-jet morphology with sizes of 0.85 kpc and 60 pc respectively.

For 1159+01 we found _ var > 4 in the comparison of VLA data at the frequencies of 8.4 GHz and 22 GHz, although in the first case yielding a low fractional variability, of 9 . At 22 GHz the flux density variation is large, with _ var =21 and fractional variability 60 .
There is good agreement between the observed rotation measure for 1159+01 in this work, of 79.2 1.8 and the result from MM08, of 72.1 1.4.

gmw_00000406.name: J1405+4056
gmw_00000406.overview: J1405+4056 is a broad-absorption line quasar and blazar candidate [arXiv:1305.3371]. Its radio structure consists of an inverted-spectrum core and steep, one-sided jets [arXiv:1305.3371], which have also been imaged as a double structure [arXiv:1505.03394].

sources.summary: The study classifies J1405+4056 as a blazar-candidate BAL quasar with an inverted-spectrum core, steep one-sided jets, core polarization, and possible 5 GHz variability.

quotes.quote: J1405+4056 : The structure in Figures fig:J1405_I is consistent with that obtained by the VIPS. Table tbl:flux indicates that the radio structure consists of an inverted-spectrum core (the component A) and steep spectrum one-side jets (the components B and C). Its morphology can be classified as a one-sided structure.

For J1405+4056, no polarized emission at 1.663 and 1.671 GHz was detected at the core. Polarized emission at low frequencies could suffer from depolarization within a bandwidth and/or within a beamwidth.
J1405+4056 shows S > 3 _ var at 5 GHz between the VIPS and our VLBA observation at 5 GHz; however, we cannot rule out possible effects due to a different uv coverage at the short baselines.

sources.summary: 1405+4056 has VLBI images consistent with VIPS, a double structure with component C1 as the radio core, and meaningful flux differences possibly caused by variability.

quotes.quote: 1405+4056 . Our 1.7- and 8.4-GHz images are consistent with the 5-GHz VIPS image helm07 . They show double structure with component C1 being a radio core (Fig. images1 ). The same result was obtained in VLBA observations made by taka13 . Contrasting single dish flux density measurements with those reported in this paper and provided by taka13 meaningful differences ( 30 ) are clearly visible at 1.7 and 5 GHz. However, we are unable to unambiguously determine whether it is a result of different uv coverage or due to intrinsic variability.

gmw_00000407.name: SDSS J0832+0643
gmw_00000407.overview: SDSS J0832+0643 is a type 2 active galaxy with pure narrow emission lines [arXiv:1501.04811]. It exhibits a unique double-peaked narrow H-beta profile alongside a single-peaked narrow H-alpha profile, which is attributed to radiative transfer effects rather than kinematic models [arXiv:1501.04811].

sources.summary: The letter presents SDSS J0832+0643 as a type 2 active galaxy whose unusual Balmer profiles favor a radiative-transfer explanation over kinematic dual-black-hole models.

quotes.quote: In this letter, we firstly report one unique object SDSS J0832+0643 with particular features of narrow balmer emission lines: double-peaked narrow H but single-peaked narrow H . The particular features can not be expected by currently proposed kinematic models for double-peaked narrow emission lines, because the proposed kinematic models lead to similar line profiles of narrow balmer emission lines. However, due to radiative transfer effects, the non-kinematic model can be naturally applied to well explain the particular features of narrow balmer emission lines: larger optical depth in H than 10 leads to observed double-peaked narrow H , but smaller optical depth in H around 2 leads to observed single-peaked narrow H . Therefore, SDSS J0832+0643 can be used as strong evidence to support the non-kinematic model for double-peaked narrow emission lines.

Based on the pPXF determined results, the stellar velocity dispersion of host galaxy is about 155 17 km/s in SDSS J0832+0643, which is consistent with the reported value 158 15 km/s determined by the STARLIGHT code (Cid Fernandes et al. 2005) in Ge et al. (2012). The coincident results indicate our determined stellar components are reliable. Then, after stellar lights being removed, emission lines can be well checked. And we can find that SDSS J0832+0643 is one type 2 objects with pure narrow emission lines: strong narrow balmer lines and much weak [ O iii ] 5007 line.

gmw_00000408.name: MRC 1138 262
gmw_00000408.overview: MRC 1138-262 is a radio galaxy [arXiv:1610.01627, arXiv:2203.02208] at a redshift of z ~ 2.16 [arXiv:0710.4553, arXiv:2209.15467] that acts as the progenitor of a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2203.02208, arXiv:2310.05206]. It is surrounded by a giant Lyman-alpha halo [arXiv:1802.08742, arXiv:2203.02208] and numerous clumpy, star-forming satellite galaxies undergoing active mergers [arXiv:astro-ph/0610909, arXiv:2203.02208].

sources.summary: The paper describes MRC 1138-262 as a z~2 radio galaxy in the Spiderweb system, surrounded by diffuse UV intergalactic light from young stars across a halo of merging satellites.

quotes.quote: We present detailed images of diffuse UV intergalactic light (IGL), situated in a 60 kpc halo that surrounds the radio galaxy MRC 1138-262 at z=2. We discuss the nature of the IGL and rule out faint cluster galaxies, nebular continuum emission, synchrotron, inverse Compton, synchrotron self-Compton emission and scattering of galactic stellar light as possible sources of the IGL. Dust scattered quasar light is an unlikely possibility that cannot be ruled out entirely. We conclude that the source of the IGL is most likely to be a young stellar population distributed in a halo encompassing the radio and satellite galaxies, undergoing star formation at a rate greater than 57 8 .

The deepest image of a distant radio galaxy with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) is a recent Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) observation of MRC 1138-262 at z = 2.156 Miley2006 . This system has been dubbed the Spiderweb galaxy as its complex morphology resembles a spider's web. The ACS image shown in Fig. 1 shows tens of UV bright galaxies surrounding a central galaxy (marked with a white cross). As the radio core is cospatial with the central galaxy we refer to this central galaxy as the radio galaxy, and all the surrounding galaxies are referred to as the satellite galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Spiderweb Galaxy as a z=2.2 forming massive galaxy with a metal-rich, non-diffuse molecular CGM embedded in a larger proto-cluster.

quotes.quote: We recently discovered that the coldest gas phase can also exist in such environments, by revealing a molecular gas reservoir across the halo of the massive forming Spiderweb Galaxy at z = 2.2 hereafter EM16 emo16 . The Spiderweb Galaxy is a conglomerate of starforming galaxies that surround the radio galaxy MRC 1138-262, and that are embedded in a giant Ly halo pen97,mil06 . We refer to the entire 200 kpc region of the Ly halo as the ``Spiderweb Galaxy'', because it will likely evolve into a single dominant cluster galaxy hat09 . The Spiderweb Galaxy is part of a larger proto-cluster kur04,kod07,dan14 .

Mixing in the CGM Our findings of extended , CO(1-0) and CO(4-3) imply that the cold molecular CGM is metal-rich and not diffuse. As we showed in EM16, the surface densities of the molecular CGM and the rate of in-situ star formation across the halo fall on the same Kennicutt-Schmidt relation as for star-forming galaxies ken98 . The fact that the gas excitation and \ abundance of the CGM are similar to that of the ISM in star-forming galaxies strengthens this claim. Despite the similarities between the CGM of the Spiderweb and the ISM in surrounding proto-cluster galaxies, it is unlikely that the cold CGM consists mainly of gas that is currently being tidally stripped from proto-cluster galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper identifies MRC 1138-262 as the central radio galaxy in the Spiderweb system, with unusually high CO and carbon-line excitation relative to comparison galaxies.

quotes.quote: The central radio galaxy MRC 1138-262 is covered fully by the ALMA beam and shows an extraordinary high global L^ _ CO(4-3) / L^ _ CO(1-0) 1 (Table tab:results ). In metal-rich environments, such high global gas excitation states are hard to achieve with far-UV photons from star formation, and cloud-heating mechanisms due to cosmic rays, jet-induced shocks, or gas turbulence must be prevailing pap08,pap12,ivi12 . MRC 1138-262 also has a high L^ _ [ CI ] / L^ _ CO 0.67, exceeding that of most submm galaxies (SMGs), quasi-stellar objects (QSOs), and lensed galaxies wal11, ala13, bot17 . We compare our \ detection with [ C i ] ^ 3 P _ 2 ^ 3 P _ 1 data from gul16 , which we tapered and smoothed to the same spatial resolution (Fig. fig:galaxies ). We derive a \ fine-structure ratio of L^ _ [ CI ] 2 1 / L^ _ [ CI ] 1 0 0.62, which implies an excitation temperature T _ ex 32 K for optically thin gas stu97 . ^ The ATCA profile in Fig. fig:galaxies provides an upper limit to the CO(1-0) content of MRC 1138-262, because the CO(1-0) data have a larger beam than the \ and CO(4-3) data, and therefore include more extended CO(1-0).

Second, both the gas excitation and relative \ brightness are substantially higher in the radio galaxy MRC 1138-262. A possible explanation for the latter is that the CO(1-0) luminosity is reduced due to a high cosmic ray flux near the AGN bis17 . Alternatively, the \ luminosity may depend on processes that also affect the gas excitation, and thus the luminosity of high- J CO lines like CO(4-3) (Sect. sec:results ).

sources.summary: The paper detects [CII] emission from the z=2.1612 Spiderweb galaxy, separating AGN and CGM components and arguing the extended CGM dominates the [CII] signal.

quotes.quote: We present the detection of [ CII ] 158 m emission from the Spiderweb galaxy at z =2.1612 using the Atacama Pathfinder EXperiment. The line profile splits into an active galactic nucleus (AGN) and circum galacic medium (CGM) component previously identified in CO and [ CI ] . We find that these individual [ CII ] components are consistent in terms of CO and far-IR luminosity ratios with the populations of other z 1 AGN and dusty star-forming galaxies. The CGM component dominates the [ CII ] emission in the 10 \ APEX beam. Although we do not have spatially resolved data, the close correspondence of the velocity profile with the CO(1-0) detected only on scales of tens of kiloparsecs in CO(1-0) suggests that the [ CII ] emission is similarly extended, reminiscent of [ CII ] halos recently found around z > 5 galaxies.

In this Letter, we present Atacama Pathfinder EXperiment (APEX) [ CII ] observations of the Spiderweb galaxy at z =2.1612, one of the best studied high redshift radio galaxies (HzRG), located at the centre of a protocluster e.g. pentericci2000,miley2006 . emonts2013 first detected CO(1-0) in the Spiderweb galaxy using the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA). Deeper observations showed that this emission splits into two components dominated by the AGN and the CGM, which is over-resolved in longer baseline observations with the Karl J. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) emonts2016 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Spiderweb Galaxy/J1140-2629 as a massive z=2.156 radio galaxy embedded in a giant Ly halo, surrounded by merging satellites, with broad nuclear emission and an overmassive black hole.

quotes.quote: Here we address these issues using deep Chandra ACIS-S observations ( 700 ks) of the remarkable Spiderweb Galaxy (J1140-2629) complex at z=2.156 redshift obtained from low-resolution spectroscopy by 1995vanOjik . The central, powerful radio galaxy, which is embedded in a giant Ly halo 1997Pentericci,2006Miley , is surrounded by a 2 Mpc-sized overdensity (corresponding to a radius of 4 arcmin) of star forming galaxies (Ly and H emitters), dusty starbursts, and red galaxies likely in the process of shaping a nascent red sequence 2008Zirm . The complex around the Spiderweb Galaxy is considered to be a typical protocluster region and is expected to evolve into a massive cluster in less than 1.5 Gyr, with the radio galaxy itself showing characteristics of a cD progenitor 2006Miley .

Using the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) observations, the clumpy features, embedded in the 200 kpc Ly halo, whose distribution resembles that of flies trapped in a spiderweb, have been shown to correspond to approximately ten star-forming satellite galaxies moving with peculiar velocities of several hundred km/s and presumed to be merging with the central radio galaxy 2006Miley . Eventually, 2011Nesvadba found that the optical and near-infrared (NIR) spectrum of the nucleus of J1140-2629 shows a broad, spatially unresolved H line (blended with [ NII ] ) with a FWHM of 14900 km/s.
