The presence of broad nuclear lines is rare in optical counterparts of high-redshift radio sources typically classified as TypeII AGN. The black hole mass is estimated to be M_ 2 10^ 10 M_ , putting J1140-2629 a factor of 2 above the local M_ relation, in agreement with its expected evolution. Taken together, the observed characteristics suggest that the Spiderweb is indeed a massive radio galaxy forming at the center of a dynamically evolving protocluster region and is likely to evolve into a brightest cluster galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLT imaging and spectroscopy of MRC 1138-262, describing its clumpy morphology, giant line-emitting halo, AGN nucleus, metallicity, and hierarchical formation as a radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: MRC 1138-262 at redshift 2.16 is one of the best candidates for such a search. The indications for 1138-262 being at the center of a cluster include (a) the very clumpy morphology as observed by the HST , reminiscent of model predictions for a massive merging system ; (b) the extremely distorted radio morphology and largest radio rotation measure (6200 rad m ^ 2 ) in a sample of more than 70 HzRGs, indicating that 1138-262 is surrounded by a hot, clumpy and dense magnetized medium . On the basis of these arguments we targetted 1138-262 for a cluster search using narrow band imaging and multi object spectroscopy.

Optical imaging Image reduction resulted in 6 8 6 8 narrow and broad band images with seeing 0 9 and 1 limiting AB magnitude per square arcsecond of 27.8 and 28.1 respectively. The B band image (see greyscale in Fig.  blya ) shows that MRC 1138-262 has a very clumpy morphology, quite unlike modern-day massive ellipticals. Instead, there are 7 seperate barely resolved knots in a 15 10 \ region, some aligned with the direction of the radio jet axis.

The new images and spectra obtained with the VLT show that the complex system of stars, gas and ionizing radiation, which we call MRC 1138-262, contains significant amounts of neutral hydrogen gas, but has also stellar components with properties similar to that of LBGs. This is further evidence that MRC 1138-262 at z = 2.16 is a galaxy in the process of formation by hierarchical merging of LBGs.

sources.summary: The paper presents MRC 1138--262 as a radio galaxy/proto-BCG undergoing hierarchical merging, with clumpy satellite structure, extended star formation, and gaseous-halo outflows.

quotes.quote: We obtained deep images of the radio galaxy MRC 1138--262 with the Hubble Space Telescope Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS, Ford et al. 1998), as part of an ACS GTO programme to map the central regions of radio galaxy protoclusters in order to study star formation and galaxy morphologies in overdense environments at 2<z<6 . By coadding the images taken through the filters g_ 475 and I_ 814 (both in the rest-frame UV) we created a 44 ksec image of a 100 100 kpc ^2 region surrounding the radio galaxy (Fig. 1). Initially studied in detail by Pentericci et al. (1997,1998,2001), the increased depth of the ACS image reveals more fine details and complexity in this remarkable object (Miley et al. 2006): The optical continuum emission of the galaxy consists of at least 10 distinct clumps, presumably satellite galaxies that are still merging with MRC 1138--262.

We interpret the new HST image as showing hierarchical merging in a proto-BCG. The morphological complexity of the MRC 1138--262 system agrees, at least qualitatively, with predictions of massive galaxy formation (e.g. Dubinsky 1998, Gao et al. 2004). The extensive merging provides a plausible mechanism for fueling the radio source, which may subsequently interact with its surrounding medium to regulate its own growth by preventing gas accretion from the larger halo when the source is `on' (Croton et al. 2006). Evidence for this kind of coupling between the radio structure and the surrounding medium has been revealed through recent integral field spectroscopy of the gaseous halo surrounding MRC 1138--262 (Fig. 2). Nesvadba et al. (2006) found very high gas velocities and velocity dispersions associated with the radio structure.

sources.summary: The paper presents MRC 1138--262 as a z=2.2 radio galaxy and cD progenitor whose Spiderweb morphology shows clumpy satellites, extended star formation, and active merging.

quotes.quote: We present a deep image of the radio galaxy MRC 1138--262 taken with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) at a redshift of z = 2.2 . The galaxy is known to have properties of a cD galaxy progenitor and be surrounded by a 3 Mpc-sized structure, identified with a protocluster. The morphology shown on the new deep HST ACS image is reminiscent of a spider's web. More than 10 individual clumpy features are observed, apparently star-forming satellite galaxies in the process of merging with the progenitor of a dominant cluster galaxy 11 Gyr ago. There is an extended emission component, implying that star formation was occurring over a 50 40 kpc region at a rate of more than 100 M_ yr ^ 1 .

Because of its striking appearance and its probable nature, we name the host galaxy of MRC 1138--262 the ``Spiderweb galaxy'' and refer to the small satellite galaxies located within and around it as ``flies.'' With relative velocities of several hundred kilometers per second (Fig. 1), these flies will traverse the 100 kpc extent of the Spiderweb many times in the interval between z 2.2 and 0, consistent with the merger scenario.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS1138-26 as a z=2.15 radio galaxy in a rich proto-cluster, with clumpy morphology, a central location, and surrounding over-densities of confirmed galaxies.

quotes.quote: Among the several proto-clusters reported so far, PKS1138-26 at z=2.15 is one of the most promising proto-clusters for its clear over-density of spectroscopically confirmed galaxies by previous studies miley08 . Early studies of the the PKS1138-26 radio galaxy at z=2.15 were performed by pentericci97,pentericci98 , who reported a clumpy morphology of the radio galaxy. Followed by these initial observations, kurk00 first reported an over-density of star forming galaxies around the radio galaxy.

We could not obtain a good enough photometric redshift for the PKS1138 radio galaxy, z_ phot =2.38^ +0.08 _ 0.10 (i.e., it is 2.3 away from the spectroscopic redshift) and it goes out of our main sample. But, the radio galaxy is a powerful AGN and our photometric redshift is relatively good for such an extreme object. We show the radio galaxy in Fig. fig:lss just to illustrate the location of the radio galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses MRC 1138-262 as a z=2.156 high-redshift radio galaxy in a well-studied dense environment, with many emission-line candidates and spectroscopic redshifts around it.

quotes.quote: For the \ environment we selected the fields surrounding the HzRGs MRC 1138-262 at z=2.156 and \ at z=2.349 . Both of these radio galaxies have redshifts that place \ emission from nearby galaxies within the bandpasses of available ESO/ISAAC filters. MRC 1138-262 lies within a well-studied , with more than 30 spectroscopic redshifts ( Pentericci2000,Kurk2004a,Kurk2004b,Doherty2010 ; Kuiper et al. sumbitted).

The MRC 1138-262 The \ surrounding the radio galaxy MRC 1138-262 is one of the best studied dense regions at z>2 e.g. Kurk2000,Pentericci2000,Kurk2004a,Kurk2004b,Miley2006,Hatch2008,Hatch2009,Tanaka2010b . We used the results of Kurk2004a who identified \ emitters in a 12.5 arcmin ^ 2 field surrounding MRC 1138-262. There are 38 sources with >2 and EW >30 , of which 3 are within the Ly halo of the radio galaxy, and thus not counted as part of the overall large-scale structure.

sources.summary: The paper models the Spiderweb Galaxy infrared SED, finding rapid concurrent black-hole accretion and star formation, likely merger-driven, with strong outflow and future satellite merging.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed study of the infrared spectral energy distribution of the high-redshift radio galaxy MRC 1138-26 at z=2.156 , also known as MRC 1138-26 the Spiderweb Galaxy . By combining photometry from Spitzer , Herschel and LABOCA we fit the rest-frame 5-300 m emission using a two component, starburst and active galactic nucleus (AGN), model. The total infrared ( 8-1000 m) luminosity of this galaxy is ( 1.97 0.28 ) 10^ 13 L_ with ( 1.17 0.27 ) and ( 0.79 0.09 ) 10^ 13 L_ due to the AGN and starburst components respectively. The high derived AGN accretion rate of 20 Eddington, and the measured star formation rate (SFR) of 1390 150 M _ yr ^ 1 , suggest that this massive system is in a special phase of rapid central black hole and host galaxy growth, likely caused by a gas rich merger in a dense environment.

The high SFR and strong outflow suggest this galaxy could potentially exhaust its fuel for stellar growth in a few tens of Myr, although the likely merger of the radio galaxy with nearby satellites suggest bursts of star formation may recur again on time scales of several hundreds of Myr. The age of the radio lobes implies the jet started after the current burst of star formation, and therefore we are possibly witnessing the transition from a merger-induced starburst phase to a radio-loud AGN phase.

sources.summary: The paper studies the z=2.16 Spiderweb radio galaxy, finding a mildly absorbed variable quasar, diffuse 100 kpc X-ray emission, narrow-line optical classification, and substantial star formation in its system.

quotes.quote: We present the X-ray imaging and spectral analysis of the diffuse emission around the radio galaxy J1140-2629 (the Spiderweb galaxy) at z=2.16 and of its nuclear emission, based on a deep (700 ks) Chandra observation. We find that the Spiderweb galaxy hosts a mildly absorbed quasar, showing a modest yet significant spectral and flux variability on a timescale of 1 year. We also find a roughly symmetric, diffuse emission within a radius of 100 kpc centered on the Spiderweb galaxy.

The Spiderweb Galaxy was identified as a high-z radio galaxy (HzRG) while targeting ultra-steep spectrum radio sources 1994Roettgering in an ESO Key Program. From the optical point of view, the Spiderweb Galaxy has been classified as a narrow emission line galaxy at z=2.16 see 1997Rottgering . The Spiderweb Galaxy shows a star formation rate of 15.6 0.7 M_ /yr from dust-uncorrected optical/UV emission, as opposed to a total of 50 M_ /yr contributed from all the "flies" 2009Hatch .

sources.summary: The paper studies the Spiderweb galaxy's protocluster, noting its powerful AGN and radio jets dominate millimetre continuum while the SZ centroid offset supports a merging proto-ICM scenario.

quotes.quote: To measure the Sunyaev-Zeldovich (SZ) effect of the protocluster complex surrounding PKS 1138-262 ( z=2.156 ; commonly known as the Spiderweb galaxy), we used the the Atacama Large Millimeter/Submillimeter Array and obtained deep Band 3 ( 94.5-110.5 GHz ) observations, exploiting both the 12-meter array (ALMA) and the 7-meter Atacama Compact Array (ACA). ht The transition from sparse protocluster complexes to mature, nearly virialized systems is a tumultuous process Kravtsov2012,Overzier2016 . Energetic events --- for example, infall and accretion of the diffuse medium from surrounding filaments, mergers with substructures and feedback from active galactic nuclei (AGN) --- affect the regularity of the assembling intracluster medium (ICM), with simulations predicting that their effects could persist for more than a Hubble time Zhang2016 . From an observational point of view, this implies that building a simple, analytical model describing the morphology of the disturbed proto-ICM is not a trivial task. Further, the possibility of mapping the ICM within galaxy protoclusters relies on our ability to separate the SZ footprint from any contaminating sources within the field. In fact, the Spiderweb galaxy harbours a powerful AGN Carilli1997,Pentericci1997 , with associated hybrid-morphology Anderson2022,Carilli2022 jets extending over scales of about 100 kpc . The result is that the millimetre-wavelength continuum signal in the direction of the Spiderweb protocluster is dominated by the emission from the central radio galaxy. In the available Band 3 ACA data (probing the SZ signal thanks to its ability to recover larger angular scales), we measure a peak surface brightness for the continuum emission from the central radio source of 2.39 0.12 mJy beam^ 1 . By assuming that, also at the Spiderweb redshift, the total mass and volume-integrated SZ signal scale covariantly

The observed discrepancy between the SZ mass estimate and the value from velocity-dispersion measurements, in combination with the evidence for a positional offset between the Spiderweb galaxy and the SZ centroid is thus consistent with a merging scenario, in which the complex structure of the pressure distribution of the proto-ICM is not necessarily well captured by the simple analytical models used in our analysis.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Spiderweb Galaxy as a proto-BCG/radio galaxy with associated overdensities and a large cold molecular reservoir in the Spiderweb protocluster.

quotes.quote: Based on deep HST imaging Miley_2006ApJ...650L..29M , this radio galaxy is dubbed the ``Spiderweb Galaxy'' and the protocluster associated was later called the ``Spiderweb protocluster''. Rigby_2014MNRAS.437.1882R found a Herschel-SPIRE overdensity around the Spiderweb Galaxy based on simultaneously conducted 250, 350 and 500 m observations. Through observations with the Australian Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) of the ground transition of carbon monoxide (CO(1-0)) as tracer, large cold molecular reservoirs have been revealed being physically related to the starbursting (proto-)BCG (Spiderweb Galaxy) and the star-forming galaxy HAE229 Emonts_2016Sci...354.1128E, Dannerbauer_2017AA...608A..48D .

With the ATCA, a large reservoir of molecular gas ( 70 kpc) was found surrounding the Spiderweb radio galaxy, suggesting that the galaxy is growing from the recycled gas in the circum-galactic medium (CGM) rather than through direct accretion from the cosmic web Emonts_2016Sci...354.1128E, Emonts_2018MNRAS.477L..60E . In this paper, the ``extended/large molecular gas reservoirs'' refers to the gas reservoirs with super-galactic scale of tens of kpc ( 40 kpc; 40 kpc corresponds to the typical beam-size of the ATCA mosaic data we used in this paper) just like those in the Spiderweb galaxy and HAE229. Besides the AGNs, which has a violent gas exchange with the surrounding environments (e.g., the Spiderweb galaxy seems to evolve from the recycling of metal-enriched outflow gas; Nesvadba_2006ApJ...650..693N ).

sources.summary: SHARC-II detects PKS 1138 262 as a double-knotted 350 micron source aligned with its radio jets, while most surrounding SCUBA sources are not confirmed.

quotes.quote: PKS 1138 262 At 350- m the central radio galaxy (abbreviated RG, and marked by a cross in Figure 1) is seen to be associated with a 5 emission feature consisting of two connected knots roughly aligned with the axis defined by the kiloparsec scale radio jets (marked with tick marks around the central RGs in Figure 1). This double-knotted structure is most likely real since it is also discernable at 850- m, and in both maps the south-western most source ( S_ 350 m =(92 18) mJy, 7 south-west from the RG) is the brightest, see Table 1.

However, at the position of the RG, which coincided with the weaker knot ( S_ 350 m =(61 15) mJy), a 1200- m flux density of (1.5 0.5) mJy was detected using mambo on the IRAM 30 m Telescope (Carilli et al. \ 2001). The brightest scuba source ( S_ 850 m =(7.8 2.2) mJy) detected by Stevens et al. \ in this field lies about 70 east of the RG and coincides with a 1.9 peak in the 350- m map. Several explanations may account for the lack of a significant 350- m detection of this very bright scuba source: a) the 850- m detection could be spurious (it lies at the edge of the scuba map), b) the source lies near the edge of the sharc-ii map where the noise is higher which makes it less likely to be detectable, or ) the source is real, but at such a high redshift that the 350- m band is shifted beyond the dust peak and samples the SED so far down the Wien tail that it is undetectable given the depth of the maps. We are able to put a 3 upper limit on its 350- m flux of S_ 350 m 95 mJy.
Thus, while we verify the double-knot structure of the central radio galaxy, we do not confirm any of the apparently associated sources seen with scuba .

sources.summary: The paper treats MRC 1138--262 as the pilot z=2.16 radio galaxy whose field is overdense in Ly-alpha emitters, X-ray sources, EROs, and H emitters.

quotes.quote: MRC 1138--262, z = 2.16 This radio galaxy was the target of our pilot project (see also Sect. \ introduction ). Narrow- and broad-band imaging with FORS1 on the VLT resulted in the detection of 37 candidate \ emitters kur00,kur04b . Subsequent spectroscopy confirmed 15 \ emitters to be near the radio galaxy at z=2.16 pen00a . The density of \ emitters in this field is roughly a factor 4 higher as compared to field studies pen00a,kur04b . This field is also overdense in X-ray sources pen02 and extremely red objects EROs, kur04b .
Also, nine H emitters were spectroscopically confirmed to be associated with the radio galaxy kur04a,kur04b , increasing the number of confirmed protocluster members to > 25.

sources.summary: The paper studies the Spiderweb Galaxy as a massive z=2.2 radio galaxy whose stellar mass may grow substantially through mergers with nearby satellite galaxies in its protocluster.

quotes.quote: We study the stellar mass assembly of the Spiderweb Galaxy (MRC 1138-262), a massive z=2.2 radio galaxy in a protocluster and the probable progenitor of a brightest cluster galaxy. Nearby protocluster galaxies are identified and their properties are determined by fitting stellar population models to their rest-frame ultraviolet to optical spectral energy distributions. We find that within 150 kpc of the radio galaxy the stellar mass is centrally concentrated in the radio galaxy, yet most of the dust-uncorrected, instantaneous star formation occurs in the surrounding low-mass satellite galaxies. We predict that most of the galaxies within 150 kpc of the radio galaxy will merge with the central radio galaxy by z=0 , increasing its stellar mass by up to a factor of 2 . However, it will take several hundred Myr for the first mergers to occur, by which time the large star formation rates are likely to have exhausted the gas reservoirs in the satellite galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses MRC1138-262 as the powerful z=2.16 radio galaxy anchoring the protocluster; imaging around it finds overdense companion populations and measures galaxy properties relative to the radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: We present a high spatial-resolution HST /NICMOS imaging survey in the field of a known protocluster surrounding the powerful radio galaxy MRC1138-262 at z=2.16 .

Broad and narrow-band imaging, both in the optical and near-infrared, of the field surrounding the powerful radio galaxy MRC 1138-262 ( z=2.16 ) have identified more than 100 candidate companion galaxies. There are surface-overdensities of both line-emitting candidates (Lyman- and H ), X-ray point sources, sub-mm selected galaxies and red optical--near-infrared galaxies Pentericcietal02,KurkPhD,Kurketal04,Stevensetal03, Croftetal05 . Fifteen of the Ly and 9 of the H emitters have been spectroscopically confirmed to lie at the same redshift as the radio galaxy Kurketal04b .
In the case of MRC 1138-262, we know that galaxies are overdense on the scale of a few arcminutes Kurketal04, Croftetal05 and are thus well-suited for observations with NICMOS camera 3 on HST .

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 1138 as the central z=2.156 radio galaxy of a proto-cluster, noting its bright, clumpy and distorted morphology and surrounding filamentary overdensity.

quotes.quote: The PKS 1138 262 field is one of the best-studied proto-clusters, centered on a radio galaxy at z=2.156 , and importantly, the H line from this redshift falls in between the night sky emission lines in the NIR and can be observed with ground-based telescopes ( kur04a b). The central radio galaxy (PKS 1138) is one of the brightest radio galaxies known at z 2 with a clumpy morphology in the optical ( pen97 ; 1998) and distorted morphology in radio ( car97 ; 2002). As a part of this project, we observed the field around the z=2.156 radio galaxy PKS 1138 through the J , K_s , and NB2071 filters using the MOIRCS imaging spectrograph on the Subaru Telescope that has a 4 ' 7 ' field.

We find that the PKS 1138 proto-cluster is embedded in a 10 Mpc-scale filament traced by H emitters and extending from north-east to south-west through the radio galaxy. We also find a possible filament running towards south-east from the radio galaxy, suggesting that the PKS 1138 field is located at a node of the cosmic web at z=2.16 .

sources.summary: The review describes the Spiderweb galaxy as a z=2.2 radio galaxy and forming brightest cluster galaxy with clumpy morphology, high stellar and molecular masses, intense star formation, and AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: Perhaps one of the most spectacular examples of such a proto-typical BCG found to date is the `Spiderweb galaxy', a radio galaxy at the center of a z=2.2 protocluster. This system has many intriguing features that both quantitatively and qualitatively suggest that it is a forming BCG (see Figs. fig:bcg_sim , fig:labs ). Its main component in the rest-frame optical has a classical De Vaucouleurs profile, while its larger-scale morphology in the rest UV and optical is extremely clumpy and contains numerous faint satellites and diffuse emission pentericci97,pentericci98,miley06 . It has an exceptionally large stellar mass 10^ 12 M_ ; pentericci97,hatch09 , a high molecular mass M_ H_2 10^ 11 M_ ; emonts13 , a high SFR >1000 M_ year ^ 1 ; stevens03,hatch08,seymour12 , and an SMBH of 10^ 10 M_ nesvadba11a . The radio AGN shows evidence for strong interaction with the surrounding medium pentericci97 , capable of removing a large fraction of the gas and quenching star formation nesvadba06 .

sources.summary: The paper includes MRC 1138-262 as the Spider Web BCG, detected in CO, powerful in radio emission, and showing clumpy stellar substructures in archival HST imaging.

quotes.quote: As discussed in Castignani2019 they are i) the z=1.7 BCG observed in CO(2 1) by Webb2017 , which belongs to the Webb2015 sample of distant BCGs, similarly to our two targets; ii) the z = 1.99 BCG candidate of the cluster ClJ1449+0856, detected in CO(4 3) and CO(3 2) by Coogan2018 ; iii) MRC 1138-262 at z = 2.2 (i.e., the Spider Web galaxy), detected in CO(1 0) by Emonts2013, Emonts2016 ; and iv) Candels-5001 at z = 3.472 , detected in CO(4 3) by Ginolfi2017 .

MRC 1138-262 and the SpARCS1049+56 BCG are powerful radio sources. They have L_ 4.5 GHz =1.9 10^ 28 W/Hz e.g., Carilli1997,Pentericci1997,Miley2006 and L_ 1.4 GHz =4.2 10^ 24 W/Hz Trudeau2019 , respectively.

MRC 1138-262 shows several clumpy substructures likely associated with stellar components, found in the archival HST ACS images (F475W and F814W filters); see also Sect. sec:reff . Although the small sample size and the large scatter in the data points prevent us from drawing firm conclusions, our morphological analysis reveals that five out of the seven considered BCGs (i.e., 71 17 ) either have smaller r_e than those of MS field star-forming galaxies or show a complex morphology with several likely star-forming components.

sources.summary: The paper uses MRC1138-262, the Spiderweb radio galaxy at z=2.156, as the central reference object around which the Spiderweb protocluster was discovered and analyzed.

quotes.quote: This protocluster was first discovered by using narrow and broad-band photometry to identify an overdensity of Lyman- emitters (LAEs) around the radio galaxy MRC1138-262 or Spiderweb galaxy ( Roettgering94 ; Pentericci97 ). These initial reports about the presence of an overdensity of LAEs at z=2.16 were later followed up by who spectroscopically confirmed the cluster membership of 15 LAEs. From that moment, the Spiderweb protocluster has been subject to exhaustive spectrophotometric campaigns to unveil the properties and distribution of its galaxy populations. This includes the characterization of the central radio galaxy ( Pentericci00 ; Carilli02 ; Miley06 ; Hatch08,Hatch09 ; Emonts16,Emonts18 : DeBreuck22 , Carilli22 ), the location of several X-ray emitters ( Pentericci02 ; Croft05 ; Tozzi22 ), the observation of an emerging red sequence within the cluster core ( Kurk04 ; Kodama07 ; Zirm08 ; Tanaka10, Tanaka13 ), the discovery of a network of starbursty submillimeter galaxies (SMGs, Dannerbauer14, Dannerbauer17 ), the location of a rich population of H emitters (HAEs) which represent the bulk of the known cluster members up to date ( Kuiper11 ; Hatch11 ; Koyama13 ; Shimakawa14, Shimakawa15, Shimakawa18b ), and the use of (sub-)millimeter observations to trace the dust content and gas reservoirs of several protocluster members ( Emonts18 ; Tadaki19 ) as well as the mapping of the protocluster large scale structure in CO(1-0) using ATCA ( Jin21 ).

The center of the protocluster is defined by the position of the radio galaxy MRC 1138-262 at z=2.156 (also known as Spiderweb galaxy, Miley06 ). Our spectroscopic sample was selected through narrow-band techniques over a large field of view (see Sect. S:Data ) and thus it spreads to clustercentric distances of up to 5R_ 200 while encompassing a relatively narrow range of systemic velocities with respect to the Spiderweb galaxy.
We took the position and redshift of the Spiderweb radio galaxy (MRC 1138-262 at z=2.156 , Miley06 ) as reference for our measurements.

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 1138--262 as a powerful z=2.156 radio galaxy and likely forming brightest cluster galaxy with an extended Ly-alpha halo and candidate cluster companions.

quotes.quote: Radio, optical and X-ray observations of the powerful radio galaxy PKS 1138--262 at z=2.156 have suggested that this galaxy is a massive galaxy in the center of a forming cluster. We have imaged 1138--262 and the surrounding 38 square arcminute field with the Very Large Telescope Based on observations carried out at the European Southern Observatory, Paranal, Chile, programme P63.O-0477(A). in a broad band and a narrow band encompassing the redshifted \ emission. We detect 50 objects with rest equivalent width larger than 20 \ and a luminous, highly extended \ halo around 1138--262.

There are several indications (e.g. Pentericci et al. \ ) that powerful radio galaxies at high redshift (HzRGs) tend to be in the center of forming clusters. The powerful radio galaxy PKS 1138--262 at redshift 2.156 is a prime example of a forming brightest cluster galaxy and has extensively been studied (e.g. \ Pentericci et al. \ ).

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1138 262 as a massive z=2.16 radio galaxy in a dense proto-cluster, with nearby emission-line galaxies associated with it and enhanced star-formation activity.

quotes.quote: Radio galaxy PKS 1138 262 is a massive galaxy at z = 2.16 , located in a dense environment. We have found an overdensity of \ emitting galaxies in this field, consistent with a proto-cluster structure associated with the radio galaxy.

Note that the redshift of the radio galaxy is 2.156 rot97 , very close to the mean of the selection filter (2.152), and to the mean of the measured redshift distribution (2.154). The distribution is consistent with a group of \ emitters associated with the radio galaxy. The velocity dispersion of this group is 360 \ using the gapper sigma method, bee90 , while the virial radius of the nine emitters is 0.45 Mpc, implying a virial mass of 8 10^ 13 M _ (assuming that all lines can be identified with \ and taking the mean of the two redshifts for object 131).

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 1138--262 as a z=2.16 forming massive radio galaxy in a protocluster, with axis-aligned diffuse X-rays and a significant redshift-space excess of nearby X-ray/Ly-emitting sources.

quotes.quote: carilli02 found diffuse X-ray emission around the radio galaxy PKS 1138--262 at z=2.16 , which is believed to be the forming, massive galaxy at the center of a protocluster laura00,kurk00,kurk03 . However, the extended emission is seen only along the radio axis, and is therefore believed to be associated with shocked material inside the radio source.

Serendipitous X-ray sources laura02 found evidence of an excess of detected X-ray sources in the vicinity of radio galaxy PKS 1138--262 at z=2.16 . They found 16 sources in the soft band (0.5--2 keV) with a minimum flux of 1 10^ 15 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , compared to 10.3 (11) calculated from the Deep Field South (North) for a similarly sized region, and 8 sources with fluxes 3 10^ 15 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 compared to 4 (5.1) for the Deep Fields. Although this may not be considered a substantial overdensity in angular space (an excess of 50 compared to a typical cosmic variance of 20-30 ), it is very significant in redshift space given that six of the serendipitous X-ray sources could be identified with previously discovered Ly or H emitting galaxies in the protocluster surrounding PKS 1138--262 laura02 .

sources.summary: The z = 2.16 radio galaxy is treated as the center of a forming protocluster, with overdense emitters, EROs, X-ray sources, radio-axis alignment, a large halo, and extended X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: \ is a massive radio galaxy at z = 2.16 surrounded by overdensities of \ emitters, \ emitters, EROs and X-ray emitters. Numerous lines of evidence exist that it is located in a forming cluster.

Two of these X-ray sources (not counting \ itself) were previously confirmed to be members of the protocluster; we have discovered that an additional two (both AGN) are members of a filamentary structure, at least 3.5 Mpc in projection, aligned with the radio jet axis, the 150 kpc-sized emission-line halo, and the extended X-ray emission around the radio galaxy.

\ is a massive forming radio galaxy at z = 2.156 pent:98 , which is surrounded by overdensities of \ emitters pent:lyspec , \ emitters kurk:haspec , EROs kurk:haero and X-ray emitters pent:x , several of which are spectroscopically confirmed to be close to the radio galaxy redshift. These overdensities appear to be spatially aligned with each other, and with the radio axis of \ ( sec:over ).

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS1138-262 as the Spider Web radio galaxy in a well-studied high-redshift protocluster, with IRAC-selected galaxies aligned along the radio-axis filament.

quotes.quote: PKS1138-262 ( z=2.16 ) This radio galaxy (also referred to as the ``Spider Web'' galaxy) is associated with a well studied high-redshift galaxy protocluster. Several intense programs have studied this field, identifying a diversity of cluster members: Ly emitters, H emitters Pentericci2000, Kurk2004B , or candidate cluster members: EROs Kurk2004A , Distant Red Galaxies DRGs; Kodama2007 . To date, only two massive red sources have been spectroscopically confirmed to be associated with PKS1138-262 Doherty2010 ; both are red IRAC-selected sources with [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] >0.1 .

Similar to DRGs selected in this field, the (brightest) IRAC-selected galaxies are preferentially found along a 3 long E/W filamentary structure (corresponding to the radio axis) in which the radio galaxy is embedded.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the PKS1138-262 field as one of three radio-galaxy environments with a factor-of-two SMG overdensity above 4 mJy, significant at about 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: We find overdensities of a factor of 2 in the source counts of 3 individual fields (4C+23.56, PKS1138-262 and MRC0355-037) over areas of 200 sq deg.

In the fields towards 4C+23.56, PKS1138-262 and MRC0355-037, however, the numbers of sources with S _ > 4 mJy exceeds the number of expected sources in a blank field by a factor of 2 . The significance of these overdensities is > 3 , which means that the probability of finding overdensities like these ones by chance is < 0.3 .
Only in the surroundings of 4C+23.56, PKS1138-262 and MRC0355-037 did we detect individual overdensity signals of 2 with a significance of 3 .

sources.summary: The paper uses MRC 1138 262 as a prior feedback benchmark, noting that AGN or jet power could drive its gas kinematics while star formation cannot.

quotes.quote: For the ``Spiderweb'' galaxy MRC 1138 262 at z = 2.16, they estimated a bolometric power of the AGN of L_ bol,AGN 7 10^ 13 \ L_ and argued that this would suffice to power the outflow with kinetic energy of L_ kin 8 10^ 12 L_ observed in MRC 1138 262 by nesvadba06a . Hence the AGN as well as the radio jet would seem energetically capable to drive the gas kinematics in this galaxy, if of-order 10 of the bolometric luminosity of the AGN were transformed into kinetic energy of the gas. In turn, the kinetic energy released by the star formation in MRC 1138 262, although one of the most vigorously star-forming high-redshift radio galaxies known, would not be sufficient nesvadba06a, seymour12 .

sources.summary: The paper presents PKS 1138 262 as a high-redshift radio galaxy whose luminous extended X-ray emission could be dominated by scattered AGN radiation.

quotes.quote: Another similar example is the radio galaxy PKS 1138 262 at z=2.2 , from which Chandra also has observed luminous extended X-ray emission (Carilly et al. ). In this case, the gas X-ray luminosity is similar to that of the 3C294 cluster (unfortunately, the gas temperature is unconstrained), but the inferred 2--10 keV (rest-frame) luminosity of the central source is four times higher, 4 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 . Moreover, in this case the gas emission is likely dominated by scattered AGN radiation already at E 5 keV (due to the higher redshift and luminosity), so that a few hundred scattered AGN photons could be detected above 5 keV in a 100 ks XEUS-2 observation of PKS 1138 262 (taking into account the larger collecting area of the telescope at lower energies).

gmw_00000409.name: USS 0211 122
gmw_00000409.overview: USS 0211 122 is a high-redshift radio galaxy at z = 2.34 [arXiv:1610.02057] that exhibits high rest-frame UV polarization [arXiv:1610.01627]. Its extended gas is aligned with the radio jet axis [arXiv:1610.02057] and displays narrow emission lines with pronounced blue wings [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:1610.01627].

sources.summary: USS 0211 122 has unusual narrow emission lines, broad blue wings, resolved gas aligned with the radio axis, and asymmetric line widths across the nucleus.

quotes.quote: USS 0211-122 USS 0211 122 at z = 2.34 has a moderately extended radio source with a size of 16 \ (133 kpc at z = 2.34) and a moderate radio power (for our sample) of 28.5 W Hz ^ 1 at 500 MHz in the rest-frame. Its line emission is however unusual compared to the other sources in our sample. All identified emission lines ( [ OIII ] 4959,5007, H , H , [ NII ] 6583, [ SII ] 6716,6731) are very narrow with FWHM = 275 km s ^ 1 . The wing is well fit with a Gaussian profile with FWHM = 1400 km s ^ 1 and a blueshift of 418 km s ^ 1 from the narrow line component, consistent with a wind originating from the central regions of this galaxy.

The maps of the narrow component show gas extended over 2.3 1.1 \ (corresponding to a deconvolved size of 19 kpc 9 kpc) and elongated along an axis that goes from south-east to north-west, roughly aligned with the radio jet axis. The velocity field shows a fairly regular gradient of 120 km s ^ 1 , although the lowest velocities are found in the nothern part of hte western bubble, and velocities in the outermost parts of the western bubble are comparable to the highest velocities in the eastern emission-line regions. Line widths are very different on the two sides of the nucleus. Towards the east, they have FWHM 300 km s ^ 1 , compared to FWHM =400-500 km s ^ 1 on the western side, with the largest values reached in the most peripheral gas.

sources.summary: The study marks USS 0211 122 as an unusual high-redshift radio galaxy with narrow gas lines, blue [OIII] wings, high UV polarization, and ambiguous jet-versus-radiation powering.

quotes.quote: The sixth source, USS 0211 122 has very low line widths in the extended gas, and pronounced blue wings in the [ OIII ] 4959,5007 line profiles nesvadba16 . This is unusal compared to the remaining sample of HzRGs with SINFONI data from nesvadba16 , where line profiles are overall broad, reflecting the large line widths and strong velocity gradients over most of the bright emission-line gas, but do not show pronounced secondary components, as commonly found in quasars. collet15b propose that this could be an intrinsic difference between radio galaxies and quasars, perhaps indicating different radial sizes of the outflows. If this is correct, then USS 0211 122 could well be a source where the energy injection from the bolometric quasar radiation exceeds over that from the radio jet.

In USS 0211 122, however, the source with particularly high rest-frame UV polarization vernet01 already discussed in ssec:starformation , the bolometric power is greater than the radio power, and the mechanism powering the gas kinematics remains therefore more ambiguous.

One notable exception is USS 0211 122, which has unusual [ OIII ] line profiles more reminiscent of quasars than radio galaxies, and which also has a low expected rate of jet to AGN energy and momentum.

sources.summary: The paper models 0211-122 with multiple shock and radiation components, finding distinct clouds within the galaxy and high-velocity shocks for UV lines alongside lower-velocity optical components.

quotes.quote: For 0211-122 we group the shock velocities into two prototypes. 1000 representing high velocity shocks fits the UV lines, while 500 reproduces the lines in the optical range. The results of selected models appear in cols. 4, 5 and 6. The weighted sum of the line fluxes appears in col. 7 of Table tab2 , Models MH1 and MH2 which refer to = 1000 and 500 , respectively, are shock dominated (SD, F=0), while model MH3 with =300 is radiation dominated (RD) +shock.

gmw_00000410.name: DDO68
gmw_00000410.overview: DDO 68 is an extremely metal-poor dwarf irregular galaxy located in the Lynx-Cancer void [arXiv:1812.03686, arXiv:2110.13175]. It exhibits a highly disturbed cometary morphology and complex gas kinematics, which are attributed to a recent merger with smaller satellite galaxies [arXiv:1611.08489, arXiv:2110.13175].

sources.summary: The paper studies DDO 68 as an extremely metal-poor star-forming dwarf, measuring regional chemical abundances, a radial oxygen gradient, unusual nitrogen ratios, and velocities across its cometary structure.

quotes.quote: We present chemical abundances and radial velocities of six regions in the extremely metal-poor star-forming dwarf galaxy DDO 68. They are derived from deep spectra in the wavelength range 3500 - 10,000 , acquired with the Multi Object Double Spectrograph (MODS) at the Large Binocular Telescope (LBT). In the three regions where the [ O III ] 4363 line was detected, we inferred the abundance of He, N, O, Ne, Ar, and S through the ``direct'' method. We also derived the oxygen abundances of all the six regions adopting indirect method calibrations. We confirm that DDO 68 is an extremely metal-poor galaxy, and a strong outlier in the luminosity - metallicity relation defined by star-forming galaxies.

With the direct-method we find indeed an oxygen abundance of 12+log(O/H)=7.14 0.07 in the northernmost region of the galaxy and, although with large uncertainties, an even lower 12+log(O/H)=6.96 0.09 in the ``tail''. This is, at face value, the most metal-poor direct abundance detection of any galaxy known. We derive a radial oxygen gradient of -0.06 0.03 dex/kpc (or -0.30 dex R_ 25 ^ 1 ) with the direct method, and a steeper gradient of -0.12 0.03 dex/kpc (or -0.59 dex R_ 25 ^ 1 ) from the indirect method.

sources.summary: The GMRT study describes DDO 68 as an extremely metal-deficient, gas-rich dwarf with extended H I, tidal-tail-like arms, and possible late-stage merger morphology.

quotes.quote: DDO 68 DDO 68 has been previously observed at the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope (WSRT) by . Their maps (which have a relatively high resolution of 13.5 arcsec), by and large, trace only the peaks in the \ column density distribution and resolve out emission from the more extended, low-column-density gas seen in the low-resolution GMRT images (e.g., Fig.  fig:lowresddo68HI ). As can be seen in the figure, the \ emission is significantly more extended than the optical emission. \ emission corresponding to the optical tail to the south can also be clearly seen.

At this resolution one can see that the galaxy has two massive \ arms, North-East-South (NES) and South-West-North (SWN), which wind around the main stellar body. The southern part of NES arm is coincident with the optical tail, while SWN arm has no obvious optical counterpart. Since it is highly unusual for a dwarf galaxy to have spiral arms, we interpret these structures (see below) to be tidal tails in a putative merger remnant.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO68 as a nearby Lynx-Cancer void, extremely metal-poor dwarf merger hosting the LBV DDO68-V1 in HII region Knot3.

quotes.quote: DDO68-V1 in a void galaxy DDO68 is a unique extremely metal-poor massive star. Discovered by us in 2008 in the HII region Knot3 with Z = Z /35 [ 12+ (O/H) 7.14 ] , DDO68-V1 was identified as an LBV star.

DDO68, at the distance D=12.75 Mpc, is one of the most metal-poor galaxies ( Z Z /35) residing in the nearby Lynx-Cancer void. DDO68 is a merger of low-mass gas-rich components ( , ). Its very low-Z gas was identified with BTA spectra in 2005. Most of SF regions are found at the periphery, mainly in the `Northern ring' and the `Southern tail' ( , ). In 2008 we discovered in its SF Knot 3 (Fig. fig1 ) a transient which was identified with an LBV ( , see also ).

sources.summary: The paper models DDO 68 as an extremely metal-poor dwarf irregular whose disturbed morphology and kinematics are reproduced by mergers with two smaller satellites.

quotes.quote: Using hydrodynamical N body simulations, we show that the observed structure and kinematics of the extremely metal-poor, dwarf irregular galaxy DDO 68 is compatible with a merger event with at least two smaller satellite galaxies. We were able to obtain a self-consistent model that simultaneously reproduces several of its observed features, including: the very asymmetric and disturbed shape of the stellar component, the overall distribution and its velocity field, the arc-like stellar structure to the west, the low-surface brightness stellar stream to the north. The model implies the interaction of the main progenitor of DDO 68 with two systems with dynamical masses 7 10^8 and almost 10^8 - 1/20 and 1/150 times the dynamical mass of DDO 68, respectively. We show that the merger between DDO 68 and the most massive of its satellites offers a route to explain the large offset of DDO 68 from the mass-metallicity relation.

With an average oxygen abundance as low as 12+ ( )=7.14 0.07 (e.g. Pustilnik2005 ; Izotov2007 ; hereafter A19 Annibali2019 ), DDO 68 (UGC 5340), located at a heliocentric distance D=12.65 hereafter S16 Sacchi2016 , ranks itself as one of the most metal-poor dwarf galaxies known to date Izotov1999b,Pustilnik2005,Pustilnik2008,Izotov2007,Izotov2009b,Izotov2009,Skillman2013,Hirschauer2016,Hsyu2017,Izotov2018,Izotov2019,Kojima2020,Pustilnik2020 . Morphologically speaking, DDO 68 is classified as a dwarf irregular. At first glance, its unusual shape is characterized by the presence of a large, bright and distorted stellar component, the Cometary Tail (see the dashed red line in Fig. fig:ddo68 ), emerging from the south-eastern edge of the galaxy's main body.

sources.summary: The paper presents DDO 68 as a 5.9 Mpc blue compact dwarf with very low oxygen abundance, newly presenting Herschel data while noting low signal-to-noise color uncertainties.

quotes.quote: The Sample Our sample of EMP galaxies contains four objects including Sextans A, ESO 146-G14, DDO 68 and Holmberg II (Ho II). Sextans A is a dwarf irregular at 1.4 Mpc with 12+log(O/H)=7.49 based on the direct method Kniazev05 . ESO 146-G14 with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.61 Bergvall95 is a blue low surface brightness galaxy at a distance of 21.4 Mpc. DDO 68 with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.21 Pustilnik05 is a blue compact dwarf at a distance of 5.9 Mpc. Ho II is a Magellanic irregular dwarf at a distance of 3.3 Mpc McCall12 , with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.92 Croxall09 . We also compared our results to the integrated measurements of the KINGFISH sample Kennicutt11 , the DGS (Dwarf Galaxy Survey) sample Madden13 , and other two EMP galaxies (I Zw 18 SBS 0335-052). These properties are listed in Table tab:properties .

Herschel broad-band images (PI: Y. Shi, PID: OT2 yshi 3) of Sextans A, ESO 146-G14 and DDO 68 were taken at 70 and 160 m through the scan map modes of PACS Poglitsch10 , and 250, 350 and 500 m through the small map modes with SPIRE Griffin10 . The exposure time (excluding overheads) in the two PACS bands is 1.9 hr, 1.6 hr and 2.7 hr for Sextans A, ESO 146-G14 and DDO 68, respectively, and in the three SPIRE bands is 6 min, 6 min and 10 min, respectively. The data of Sextans A and ESO 146-G14 have been partly published in Shi14 where the detailed data reduction was given, while the data of DDO 68 is newly presented here. DDO 68 has low S/N, which may cause large uncertainties on the color ( sec_IR ).

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 68 as a disturbed extremely metal-poor dwarf irregular with peripheral star-forming regions, complex gas kinematics, and arms consistent with a recent gas-rich merger.

quotes.quote: The dwarf irregular galaxy DDO 68 (UGC 5340, VV 542) resides in the nearby Lynx-Cancer void PaperI . It shows a peculiar, disturbed morphology and hosts several prominent young star-forming (SF) regions with the record-low metallicity (12+ (O/H)=7.14). Most of SF regions are found at the periphery, mainly in the `Northern ring' and the `Southern tail' (see DDO68 , hereafter PKP05, IT07 ). In the repeated observations of DDO 68, the unique Luminous Blue Variable star (LBV) was discovered in one of the most metal-poor \ regions in the local Universe LBV,IT09 . The GMRT (Giant Meterwave Radio Telescope) mapping study of DDO 68 revealed its complex structure and a velocity field consisting of two arms winded asymmetrically around the main bright part of the galaxy's optical body.
Their properties do not contradict the assumption that the arms are of tidal origin formed as a result of the recent gas-rich merger Ekta08 .

sources.summary: DDO 68 is described as fairly symmetrical at low HI column density, with northern and eastern high-density regions, a deep HI hole, irregular-looking velocity field, and low rotational support.

quotes.quote: DDO 68: At low column density levels, DDO 68 is reasonably symmetrical. High-column-density regions are found in the north and east of the galaxy. A deep hole devoid of HI emission is located at = 9^h53^m52^s.2 , = 29^ 4'15'' . Low signal-to-noise regions contribute to the irregular appearance of the velocity field. The ratio of rotational to random velocity is low.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 68 as one of the two most metal-poor nearby dwarf galaxies, located in the Lynx-Cancer void and unusually blue in its outer parts.

quotes.quote: The two most metal-poor dwarf galaxies in the Local Volume and adjacent regions, DDO 68 and SDSS J0926+3343, with the parameters 12+ (O/H) of 7.14 and 7.12, respectively, are representatives of this void galaxy population. Moreover, they are situated only at 1.6 Mpc from each other. Both of them are also unusual in the blue colours of their outer parts, indicating ages of the oldest visible stellar population of 1--3 Gyr DDO68_sdss,J0926 , which is in drastic contrast with the colours and ages of the absolute majority of other dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: DDO 68 is cited for very low oxygen abundance in H II region 7, though the paper notes low accuracy and higher abundances in other regions.

quotes.quote: An19 reported the very low oxygen abundance 12 + logO/H = 6.96 0.09 in the H ii region 7 of DDO 68 derived by the direct method. However, the oxygen abundance in this region is derived with low accuracy and needs to be confirmed with higher precision. Furthermore, oxygen abundances in other H ii regions of DDO 68 are considerably higher than in the region 7 P05,IT07,IT09 .

sources.summary: The paper uses DDO 68 as a metal-poor dwarf-galaxy example at 12 Mpc, noting its known LBV star lies in a cluster and gas-cloud environment while a brighter blue object is foreground.

quotes.quote: An example is given by the metal-poor dwarf galaxy DDO 68 (Figure  Fig1 ), the distance to which is 12 0.3 Mpc TGL2014 . In this galaxy, a bright massive LBV (Luminous Blue Variable) star is known PMP2017 , which is marked on the image of the galaxy (Figure  Fig1 ).

A comparison of the positions of two bright blue stars in the DDO 68 galaxy shows that the LBV star is indeed surrounded by a cluster of stars and gas clouds, while the foreground star is completely isolated.

gmw_00000411.name: NGC 891
gmw_00000411.overview: NGC 891 is an edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:0807.3365, arXiv:2112.12212] characterized by a lopsided, dust-rich disk [arXiv:1709.08733, arXiv:2310.04204] and active star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0511334]. Its extensive gaseous halo contains a massive extraplanar component [arXiv:1310.2956], a giant stellar stream [arXiv:1002.0461], and a long neutral hydrogen filament [arXiv:1501.01211].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 891 as an edge-on spiral with a massive extended gas halo, a long high-latitude filament whose fountain origin is energetically unlikely, and counter-rotating clouds indicating gas accretion.

quotes.quote: On the left, the total \ map of the edge-on galaxy NGC 891 (blue + contours) is overlaid onto a DSS optical image (orange). The \ data (obtained with the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope) show a massive and extended \ halo with a mass of 1.2 10^9 or 30 of the total \ mass ( ).

NGC 891 has a long massive filament ( M_ HI 1.6 10^7 ) extending up to about 20 kpc from the plane of the disk (Fig. \ 891and2403 ). NGC 2403 also has a filament with a similar \ mass located in projection outside the bright optical disk and clearly separated in velocity from the disk kinematics (Fig. \ fil2403 ). They are both very similar to Complex C in our Galaxy. We can calculate the energy needed to form these filaments assuming that they come from the disk through a galactic fountain. In the case of the NGC 891 filament it turns out that this energy should be of the order 1 10^ 55 erg. This would correspond to the explosion of about 10^5 supernovae in a specific region of space and over a time-scale shorter than a dynamical time, which is clearly a very unlikely event.

The data of NGC 891 show two such clouds with masses of order 10^6 and counter-rotating velocities of 50 and 90 (Fig. \ avcs891 ). These clouds cannot be produced in any kind of galactic fountain and they are most likely direct evidence of gas accretion.

sources.summary: The paper presents spatially resolved infrared imaging and SED modeling of NGC 891, tracing wavelength-dependent morphology, extraplanar dust and PAH emission, and halo dust conditions without in-situ star formation.

quotes.quote: We present a multi-wavlength infrared study of the nearby, edge-on, spiral galaxy NGC 891. We have examined 20 independent, spatially resolved IR images of this galaxy, 14 of which are newly reduced and/or previously unpublished images. These images span a wavelength regime from 1.2 m in which the emission is dominated by cool stars, through the MIR, in which emission is dominated by PAHs, to 850 m, in which emission is dominated by cold dust in thermal equilibrium with the radiation field. The changing morphology of the galaxy with wavelength illustrates the changing dominant components. We detect extra-planar dust emission in this galaxy, consistent with previously published results, but now show that PAH emission is also in the halo, to a vertical distance of z 2.5 kpc.

For six locations in the galaxy for which the S/N was sufficient, we present SEDs of the IR emission, including two in the halo --- the first time a halo SED in an external galaxy has been presented. We have modeled these SEDs and find that the PAH fraction, f_ PAH , is similar to Galactic values (within a factor of two), with the lowest value at the galaxy's center, consistent with independent results of other galaxies. In the halo environment, the fraction of dust exposed to a colder radiation field, f_ cold , is of order unity, consistent with an environment in which there is no star formation.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 891's resolved stellar structure, detecting a thick disk, spheroid, metallicity gradient, and small-scale halo variations that suggest many incompletely mixed accreted subpopulations.

quotes.quote: We present a structural analysis of NGC 891, an edge-on galaxy that has long been considered to be an analogue of the Milky Way. Using starcounts derived from deep HST/ACS images, we detect the presence of a thick disk component in this galaxy with vertical scale height h_Z=1.44 0.03 and radial scale length h_R=4.8 0.1 , only slightly longer than that of the thin disk. A stellar spheroid with a de Vaucouleurs-like profile is detected from a radial distance of 0.5 to the edge of the survey at 25 ; the structure appears to become more flattened with distance, reaching q = 0.50 in the outermost halo region probed. The halo inside of 15 is moderately metal-rich (median Fe/H 1.1 ) and approximately uniform in median metallicity. Beyond that distance a modest chemical gradient is detected, with the median reaching Fe/H 1.3 at 20 .

We find evidence for subtle, but very significant, small-scale variations in the median colour and density over the halo survey area. We argue that the colour variations are unlikely to be due to internal extinction or foreground extinction, and reflect instead variations in the stellar metallicity. Their presence suggests a startling conclusion: that the halo of this galaxy is composed of a large number of incompletely-mixed sub-populations, testifying to its origin in a deluge of small accretions.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 891 including its bulge, using velocity dispersion, disk thickness, and rotation curves to argue against a maximum disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 This galaxy has a budge ( ), and we took it into account evaluating our numerical models. Published values of the vertical exponential scale height for old stellar disc varies from 400 pc to 650 pc ( ). We assume the set of structural parameters inferred for NGC 891 by . The observed and model radial distributions of the stellar velocity dispersion ( ), stellar disc thickness and rotation curves are shown in Fig. fig12 .

The stellar velocity dispersion in the centre of the galaxy reaches 160 km/s ( ) for stars in the bulge, and the radial component of the disc velocity dispersion is c_r 120 km/s. Existence of a molecular ring in the galaxy at 3.5 kpc enables us to assume that the galaxy harbours a bar ( ). Once the radial distribution of the stellar velocity dispersion is taken into account together with condition of marginally stable stellar disc, successful model should have >1.5 . Note that the use of an alternative RC from gives =1.8 with other similar parameters. Fig. fig12 b shows the radial distribution of the disc exponential vertical scale height for the set of M_h/M_d = 0.4, 0.7 and 1.7. The stellar disc thickness indicates that the dark and luminous masses are roughly equal to each other within R_ . The value of inferred from the disc thickness is slightly less than that estimated from the velocity dispersion probably due to additional factors that increase the disc thickness in addition to the bending instability. The disc mass of 1.5 10^ 11 M_ estimated with the shape of rotation curve ( ) corresponds to a very high value of mass-to-light ratio 13.5 4. Our method yields 5 10 for this ratio. This introduces an argument against the the maximum disc in NGC 891.

sources.summary: The paper models the diffuse ionized medium of NGC 891, arguing that HOLMES increasingly dominate ionization with height and that density decreases with height.

quotes.quote: We revisit the question of the ionization of the diffuse medium in late type galaxies, by studying NGC 891. The most important challenge for the models considered so far was the observed increase of / , / , and / \ with increasing distance to the galactic plane. We propose a scenario based on the expected population of massive OB stars and hot low-mass evolved stars (HOLMES) in this galaxy to explain this observational fact.

We chose to focus on the edge on spiral galaxy NGC 891, a galaxy that has been extensively observed, especially in optical emission lines (Figure fig2 ), providing the best diagnostics for our scenario.

For each observational point in the DIG of NGC 891 we select the models of our finely meshed grid that reproduce simultaneously the values of / , / and / within the observational uncertainties. We find solutions for each value of z . In Figure fig4 we show the acceptable ranges of / , U , n_ e and N/O as a function of z . The models clearly indicate a systematic decrease of the electron density with increasing z .

sources.summary: The paper uses Chandra and XMM-Newton observations to measure NGC 891's hot halo, favoring a low-metallicity single-temperature model and arguing accretion over a steady-state galactic fountain.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 is a nearby edge-on galaxy that is similar to the Milky Way and has a hot X-ray emitting halo that could arise from accretion, a galactic fountain, or a combination of the two. The metallicity of the gas can help distinguish between these models, and here we report on results that use 138 ks of archival Chandra data and 92 ks of new XMM-Newton data to measure the temperature and metallicity of the hot halo of the galaxy. We find good fits for a thermal model with kT 0.2 keV and Z 0.1 Z_ , and rule out solar metallicity to more than 99 confidence. This result suggests accretion from the intergalactic medium as the origin for the hot halo. However, it is also possible to fit a two-temperature thermal model with solar metallicity where kT_1 0.1 keV and kT_2 0.25 keV.

A consideration of the cooling rate and scale height prefers the single-temperature model. We also find that the cooling rate in the hot gas cannot explain the massive H 1 halo in the steady state. In addition, a galactic fountain model cannot eject enough mass to account for the H 1 halo, and we speculate that the neutral halo may be gas from a prior outflow that has since cooled.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 891 as a nearby edge-on Sb spiral, mapping its radio continuum and halo magnetic-field and CRE properties with LOFAR, AMI, and VLA data.

quotes.quote: We obtained new observations of the edge-on spiral galaxy NGC 891 at 129--163 MHz with the LOw Frequency ARray (LOFAR) and at 13--18 GHz with the Arcminute Microkelvin Imager (AMI) and combine them with recent high-resolution Very Large Array (VLA) observations at 1--2 GHz, enabling us to study the radio continuum emission over two orders of magnitude in frequency. The spectrum of the integrated nonthermal flux density can be fitted by a power law with a spectral steepening towards higher frequencies or by a curved polynomial. Spectral flattening at low frequencies due to free--free absorption is detected in star-forming regions of the disk. The mean magnetic field strength in the halo is 7 2 G. The scale heights of the nonthermal halo emission at 146 MHz are larger than those at 1.5 GHz everywhere, with a mean ratio of 1.7 0.3 , indicating that spectral ageing of CREs is important and that diffusive propagation dominates.

A prime target for such studies is NGC 891 that is a fairly nearby edge-on spiral galaxy. NGC 891 is similar to our own Milky Way in terms of optical luminosity deVaucouleurs1991 , Hubble type Sb; vanderKruit1981 , and rotational velocity 225 km s ^ 1 ; Rupen1991 , but has a high star-formation rate (3.3 M _ yr ^ 1 , Arshakian2011 ) compared to the Milky Way the Galactic value is 1.66 0.20 M _ yr ^ 1 ; Licquia2014 ; this is in accordance with the presence of approximately twice the amount of molecular gas of the Milky Way, with the radial distribution of CO remarkably similar in both galaxies Scoville1993 . Due to its proximity and very high inclination, NGC 891 is an observational testing ground for the study of disk and halo interactions and the galactic halo.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 891, a nearby edge-on Milky Way analog, measuring a low-metallicity hot halo consistent with accreted gas while noting its massive H I halo and cold/hot gas imbalance.

quotes.quote: We present a measurement of the metallicity around NGC 891, a nearby, edge-on, Milky Way analog. We find that the hot gas is dominated by low metallicity gas near the virial temperature at kT=0.20 0.01 keV and Z/Z_ = 0.14 0.03 (stat) ^ +0.08 _ 0.02 (sys), and that this gas co-exists with hotter ( kT=0.71 0.04 keV) gas that is concentrated near the star-forming regions in the disk.

The low metallicity gas is consistent with the inner part of an extended halo accreted from the intergalactic medium, which has been modulated by star formation. However, there is much more cold gas than hot gas around NGC 891, which is difficult to explain in either the accretion or supernova-driven outflow scenarios.

Here we address the temperature, metallicity ( Z/Z_ ), and cooling rate in the inner region of a hot halo, using deep data for NGC 891, which is a nearby d=10 Mpc; temple05 , edge-on, Milky Way analog with the brightest X-ray halo among normal galaxies the luminosity of a few 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 is normal for its size; anderson13 . At this distance, 1 arcmin corresponds to about 2.7 kpc. NGC 891 has an inclination of 89.5 ^ rupen91 , which is ideal for separating the disk and halo gas. It is also very similar to the Milky Way, with an apparent diameter ( D_ 25 ) of 40 kpc, a rotation velocity of about 210 km s ^ 1 oosterloo07 , a spectral type of SBb garcia-burillo95 , and a B band luminosity of L_B = 2.6 10^ 10 L_ ,B devaucouleurs91 , although its SFR = 3.8 M_ yr ^ 1 is somewhat higher than in the Milky Way popescu04 . Finally, NGC 891 is surrounded by a massive H 1 halo 1.2 10^9 M_ ; oosterloo07 , whose interaction with the hot halo remains unclear.

sources.summary: SOFIA [ C 2 ] observations of NGC 891 show a thin disk plus a thick extraplanar component, strongest near northern star-forming regions, with emission extending above the midplane and interpreted as chimney or fountain material.

quotes.quote: We used SOFIA to measure the vertical distribution of [ C 2 ] 157.7 m line emission for two nearby, edge-on galaxies, NGC 891 and NGC 5907. We find that for the central region and actively-star-forming regions in the northern portion of NGC 891, and for NGC 5907, a thin ( 0.3 kpc) disk is supplemented by a thick disk with an exponential scale height of 2 kpc. The [ C 2 ] is far more extended than mid-infrared emission (0.1 kpc, tracing present-day massive star formation) but not as extended as the H 1 (100 kpc, tracing low-metallicity circum/inter-galactic matter). The extraplanar [ C 2 ] may arise in walls of chimneys that connect the disk to the halo.

At low altitudes from the NGC 891 midplane, the brightness is primarily from a thin disk with scale height in the range 0.25--0.4 kpc. It is thickest on the northern field, where there are giant H 2 regions in the midplane, and thinner in the more-quiescent southern field. For the active starburst portions of the northern field (in the - z direction) and the central part of the galaxy (both directions), a second component with scale height around 2.8 kpc is required. This wider component contributes only 3 of the [ C 2 ] model brightness in the midplane (before convolution with the PSF), but with its larger scale height it contributes 22 of the total brightness integrated over z and most of the brightness for lines of sight above 1.2 kpc from the midplane.

sources.summary: The paper maps NGC 891 in the far infrared, resolving halo dust, a superbubble, and a dust spur tied to wind feedback and satellite interaction.

quotes.quote: We present deep far-infrared observations of the nearby edge-on galaxy 891 obtained with the Herschel Space Observatory and the Spitzer Space Telescope . The maps confirm the detection of thermal emission from the inner circumgalactic medium (halo) and spatially resolve a dusty superbubble and a dust spur (filament). The dust temperature of the halo component is lower than that of the disk but increases across a region of diameter 8.0 kpc extending at least 7.7 kpc vertically from one side of the disk, a region we call a superbubble because of its association with thermal X-ray emission and a minimum in the synchrotron scaleheight. This outflow is breaking through the thick disk and developing into a galactic wind, which is of particular interest because 891 is not considered a starburst galaxy; the star formation rate surface density, 0.03 \ kpc ^ 2 , and gas fraction, just 10 in the inner disk, indicate the threshold for wind formation is lower than previous work has suggested. We conclude that the star formation surface density is sufficient for superbubble blowout into the halo, but the cosmic ray electrons may play a critical role in determining whether this outflow develops into a fountain or escapes from the gravitational potential.

The high dust-to-gas ratio in the dust spur suggests the material was pulled out of 891 through the collision of a minihalo with the disk of NGC 891. We conclude that 891 offers an example of both feedback and satellite interactions transporting dust into the halo of a typical galaxy.
The edge-on orientation of the nearby galaxy, 891, is ideal for studying the interaction between the disk, CGM, and satellite galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents NIKA2 millimetre observations of edge-on spiral NGC 891, finding dust-like millimetre morphology, nuclear and off-center peaks, and distinct thermal and synchrotron distributions.

quotes.quote: New observations of the edge-on galaxy NGC 891, at 1.15 and 2 mm obtained with the IRAM 30-m telescope and the NIKA2 camera, within the framework of the IMEGIN (Interpreting the Millimetre Emission of Galaxies with IRAM and NIKA2) Large Program, are presented in this work. By using multiwavelength maps (from the mid-IR to the cm wavelengths) we perform SED fitting in order to extract the physical properties of the galaxy on both global and local ( kpc) scales. For the interpretation of the observations we make use of a state-of-the-art SED fitting code, HerBIE (HiERarchical Bayesian Inference for dust Emission). The observations indicate a galaxy morphology, at mm wavelengths, similar to that of the cold dust emission traced by sub-mm observations and to that of the molecular gas. The contribution of the radio emission at the NIKA2 bands is very small (negligible at 1.15 mm and 10 at 2 mm) while it dominates the total energy budget at longer wavelengths (beyond 5 mm). On local scales, the distribution of the free-free emission resembles that of the dust thermal emission while the distribution of the synchrotron emission shows a deficiency along the major axis of the disc of the galaxy.

As a pilot study, we analyze the NIKA2 observations of the edge-on galaxy NGC 891, a SA(s)b type galaxy . Due to its proximity (D = 9.6 Mpc; ) and its edge-on orientation (i 89.8 ^ ; ), NGC 891 is one of the most observed and well studied galaxies of the Local Universe (e.g. ). The NIKA2 observations of NGC 891 were obtained between October 2019 and January 2020 with a total on-source integration time of 7 hours.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 891 as an edge-on CHANG-ES galaxy, finding extended low-frequency radio-halo scale heights and describing special 144 MHz imaging needed near 3C 66.

quotes.quote: With newly reduced radio continuum data of five edge on galaxies (NGC 891, NGC 3432, NGC 4013, NGC 4157, and NGC 4631), we plan to set new constraints on the morphology of radio halos and the physical properties of galactic winds driven by stellar feedback. By distinguishing between the central and outer regions of the galaxies, our study setup allows us to search for variations in the radio halo profile or CR transport along the Galactic disk.

The scale height analysis reveals that most of the galaxies are equally well fitted with an one-component instead of a two-component exponential profile. We find a bi-modality within our sample. While NGC 3432 and NGC 4013 have similar scale heights in the L-band and HBA, the low-frequency scale heights of NGC 891, NGC 4157, and NGC 4631 exceed their high-frequency counterpart significantly.

The imaging process of NGC 891 was challenging due to its proximity to a 10 Jy radio source at 1.4 GHz, 1969ApJ...157....1K , the radio galaxy 3C 66, and some fine tuning of parameters was necessary. Therefore, we use a slightly different strategy when creating a new 144 MHz map for NGC 891 with . Here, we incorporate a Briggs weighting of 0.15 together with multi-scale CLEAN .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 891 far-infrared polarimetry, finding low fractional polarization and magnetic-field geometry that requires turbulence and likely bar-like structure rather than a simple spiral model.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 is a nearby edge-on spiral galaxy at a distance of 8.36 Mpc 2001ApJ...546..681T and a star formation rate of 3.8 M_ yr ^ 1 2004A&A...414...45P . The polarization in NGC 891 has been studied using optical, NIR, FIR, and radio observations e.g., 1996A&A...308..713F, 2014ApJ...786...41M, 2020AJ....160..167J, 2020A&A...639A.112K . 1996A&A...308..713F observed many polarization lines oriented perpendicular to the galactic disk. As discussed in , the optical polarization is probably dominated by scattering and does not accurately represent magnetic field geometry. The fractional polarization in NGC 891 at NIR wavelengths was found to be significantly lower than expected compared to the Milky Way and other nearby spirals such as NGC 4565 1997AJ....114.1393J .

In the high intensity regions in the disk of NGC 891, where the fractional polarization is very low, our observations in Figure fig:polmap show very coherent polarization lines aligned with the disk for most locations.

Our simple modeling shows that the observed magnetic field geometry of NGC 891 can not be fit using a simple spiral pattern but requires a geometry similar to that found in a barred galaxy. Although adding a tilted bar in the center can reproduce the lower fractional polarization in the center, the magnitude of the polarization in our model without turbulence significantly exceeds the observational results over the entire disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses NIKA2 and multiwavelength data to model NGC 891, finding millimeter disk excess, differing dust and radio distributions, and small-grain enhancement in the halo.

quotes.quote: New observations of NGC 891, using the NIKA2 camera on the IRAM 30m telescope, along with a suite of observations at other wavelengths were used to perform a multiwavelength study of the spectral energy distribution in the interstellar medium in this galaxy. This analysis was performed globally and locally, using the advanced hierarchical Bayesian fitting code, HerBIE , coupled with the THEMIS dust model. Our dust modeling is able to reproduce the near-IR to millimeter emission of NGC 891, with the exception of an excess at a level of 25 obtained by the NIKA2 observations in the outermost parts of the disk. The radio continuum and thermal dust emission are distributed differently in the disk and galaxy halo. Different dusty environments are also revealed by a multiwavelength investigation of the emission features.

Finally, we find that emission arising from small dust grains accounts for 9.5 of the total dust mass, reaching up to 20 at large galactic latitudes. Shock waves in the outflows that shatter the dust grains might explain this higher fraction of small grains in the halo. NIKA2 observations have proven essential for a complete characterization of the interstellar medium in NGC 891.

sources.summary: The paper maps the edge-on spiral NGC 891 with NIKA2, combines multiwavelength SED fitting, and finds enhanced cold dust emission and disk structure in millimeter bands.

quotes.quote: A pilot study of IMEGIN has been the analysis of the edge-on galaxy NGC 891, at a distance of 9.6 Mpc . NGC 891 is considered a typical spiral galaxy with indications of more readily discernible structures (like, e.g., central bar, warped disk ). Because of its nearly perfect edge-on orientation, it is one of the most extensively observed nearby galaxies in a wide spectral range (e.g., ).

In this study we make use of this plethora of observations combined with the new NIKA2 observations in order to construct the spatially resolved Spectral Energy Distribution (SED) of the galaxy from mid-infrared (mid-IR) to radio wavelengths and put constraints on both the dust and radio emission of NGC 891. The current analysis uses new NIKA2 observations of NGC 891 at 1.15 mm and 2 mm, at a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 11.1 ^ and 17.6 ^ (or linear resolutions of 0.5 kpc and 0.8 kpc at a distance of 10 Mpc), respectively.

The mm emission of NGC 891 measured in the NIKA2 bands (Fig. fig:obs_sed ) has revealed regions of enhanced cold dust emission towards the inner parts of the galactic disk. It shows a primary peak at the bulge of the galaxy (region C) and two secondary maxima at about 3 kpc from the galactic center along the major axis (e.g., region B). The disk extension at both mm wavelengths reaches galactocentric distances up to 13 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC891 from UV through sub-millimeter wavelengths, finding a standard dust and stellar radiation model underpredicts the observed sub-mm SED and requiring added components to match FIR/sub-mm emission.

quotes.quote: We use this tool to analyse the well-studied nearby edge-on spiral galaxy NGC891. First we investigate whether the old stellar population in NGC891, along with a reasonable assumption about the young stellar population, can account for the heating of the dust and the observed far-infrared and sub-mm emission. The dust distribution is taken from the model of Xilouris et al. (1999), who used only optical and near-infrared observations to determine it. We have found that such a simple model cannot reproduce the SED of NGC891, especially in the sub-mm range. It underestimates by a factor of 2-4 the observed sub-mm flux.

In this paper we have modeled the edge-on spiral galaxy NGC891 from the UV to the FIR and sub-mm with the purpose of understanding what is the origin of the FIR/sub-mm emission. The strategy of the paper was to use a model for the intrinsic distribution of older stars and associated dust as derived from fitting the optical and NIR images of NGC891 by Xilouris et al. (1999), which, supplemented by a distribution of newly-form stars, should predict the diffuse FIR emission. By comparing the prediction of the ``standard model'' with the observations we tried to identify whether this model can reproduce the observed dust emission.

sources.summary: The paper applies a two-dust-disk SED model to NGC 891, deriving its optical depth, UV luminosity, FIR components, dust heating, and agreement with ISO/SCUBA radial profiles.

quotes.quote: We first applied the above method to the well-known edge-on spiral galaxy NGC 891. This is one of the most extensively observed edge-on galaxies in the nearby universe, which makes it ideal for a verification of our modelling technique.

The best solution for NGC 891 (Fig. 1) has a central face-on V-band optical thickness ^f_V=3.1 and a corresponding non-ionising UV luminosity 8.2 10^ 36 W. The luminosity of the diffuse component is 4.07 10^ 36 W, which accounts for 69 of the observed FIR luminosity, and the luminosity of the HII component is 1.82 10^ 36 W, making up the remaining 31 of the FIR luminosity.

Recent deep observations of NGC 891 with the ISOPHOT instrument at 170 and 200 m (Popescu et al. 2001) offer a still stronger test of the model, as the FIR emission here depends on the distribution of both stellar luminosity and dust. Simulated FIR maps were obtained using the actual pointing data to scan the diffuse disk model. The model map was then convolved with empirical PSFs derived from point source measurements. The comparison between the observed map (Fig. 6a) and the simulated one (Fig. 6b) show a remarkable agreement.

sources.summary: The paper maps NGC 891's far-infrared dust emission, describing it as a nearby edge-on Sb spiral with extraplanar gas, multiple halos, molecular gas, and ISO-detected cold dust.

quotes.quote: We present deep maps of dust emission from the edge-on spiral galaxy NGC 891, obtained with the ISOPHOT instrument on board the Infrared Space Observatory in broad band filters with reference wavelengths centered on 170 and 200 m. Using new processing methods to remove the effects of detector transients from the data, we detect cold dust at high dynamic range. The observed surface brightness distribution and colour profile of the far-infrared (FIR) emission are found to be in good agreement with predictions for their counterparts derived from the model of Popescu et al. (2000a). Thus, NGC 891 is the first galaxy for which an intrinsic distribution of dust and stars could be found which simultaneously accounts for both the optical/near-IR and FIR morphologies.

Popescu et al. 2003 NGC 891 is one of the most extensively observed and studied edge-on spiral galaxy in the nearby universe. At a distance of 9.5 Mpc (van der Kruit Searle 1981), it has been classified as an Sb galaxy by Sandage (1961) and is often quoted to be very similar to our own Galaxy. However it has also some unusual characteristics. For example NGC 891 contains one of the most spectacular layers of extraplanar diffuse ionised gas (DIG) (Dettmar 1990; Rand et al. 1990; Keppel et al. 1991; Pildis et al. 1994; Rand 1997, 1998; Hoopes et al. 1999; Howk Savage 2000, Otte et al. 2001).

sources.summary: The paper presents HST WFPC2 observations of NGC 891's disk-halo interface, showing an extended diffuse ionized gaseous halo, collimated filaments, midplane bubbles, and extraplanar dust.

quotes.quote: We present deep narrowband observations with high spatial resolution of extraplanar diffuse ionized gas in the halo of NGC 891, obtained with the WFPC2 on-board the HST. Our H observations, centered on the northern part of NGC 891, reveal an extended gaseous halo, which fills almost the entire field of view of our WFPC2 observation.
