Our new results enable us to perform the same studies on a sample of five new galaxies as done previously for NGC 891 and NGC 4631 (DLG95), with which they can be compared: Check whether there is a relation between the maximum radius at which massive SF is observed, r_ SF , and the radial extent of the radio halo, r_ halo (Sec. par:radext ); calculate the average energy input rate from high-mass SF in the disk, , and determine the threshold value for the onset of halo emission (Sec. par:einput ).

Galaxies with widespread SF (in the present sample NGC 4666, NGC 4700 and NGC 7090; NGC 891 and NGC 4631 from DLG95) exhibit a significant difference between r_ SF and r_ CR (see Table tab:data ). This suggests either radial electron diffusion in the disks or low-level SF in the outer parts of their disks, or a combination of both.

sources.summary: The paper presents ISO spectroscopy of UIR bands across NGC 891's disk, finding the bands contribute 9 percent of its infrared radiation and resemble diffuse Milky Way emission.

quotes.quote: The spectrum of the unidentified infrared (UIR) emission bands between 5.9 and 11.7 m has been observed for the first time in the disk of an external galaxy. We have used the low-resolution spectrometer of the ISOPHOT instrument aboard ISO. The UIR bands at 6.2, 7.7, 8.6, and 11.3 m have absolute intensities which are similar to the values observed for the diffuse emission of our own Galaxy. The UIR bands between 5.9 and 11.7 m contribute 9 of the total IR radiation of NGC 891. The intensity ratios and band widths in the NGC 891 disk emission are similar to the diffuse emission of the Milky Way, pointing to a common carrier for the UIR bands in the two galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC891 as a successful SED-model test, reporting good agreement between observed and predicted far-infrared radial profiles and a small profile asymmetry.

quotes.quote: As an example of a successful application of the model we show the model fit to the SED of NGC891 (Fig. 2). Further applications of the model can be found in Misiriotis et al. (2001). The models have been also generalised to predict the attenuation of stellar light (Tuffs et al. 2004) and the effect of dust on the observed scalelength and central surface-brightness (M ollenhoff et al. 2006) of disk galaxies as a function of inclination, wavelength and total central face-on opacity ^f_B = ^ f,1 _B + ^ f,2 _B .

In Fig. 3 one can see the comparison between the observed and predicted radial profiles of the diffuse component of NGC891 at 170 and 200 m (Popescu et al. 2004). A very good agreement has been achieved between the observations and the model predictions. The small asymmetry in the observed profiles, at the level of about 10 of the total emission, is consistent with our model predictions for the emission from localised sources.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 891 as a key edge-on system with deeply observed extraplanar gas, disk-linked halo emission, and a small gas-rich companion.

quotes.quote: For a small number of systems (Table t_extraplanar ) seen at various inclination angles and, in particular, for the edge-on galaxy NGC 891 (see Fig. \ f_n891 ) there is now detailed information on the \ structure and kinematics. This has been obtained from very deep observations with the WSRT and the VLA.
One is the distribution of the extra-planar \ in NGC 891, which is concentrated very close to the star-forming disk (see n891 ).
NGC 891 has also a small, gas-rich companion (UGC 1807) at about 80 kpc (projected distance) and 100 higher radial velocity and with about one tenth of the total mass of NGC 891.

sources.summary: The model uses NGC 891 as a well-studied prototype disk galaxy, deriving reference geometry and illustrating dust/PAH emission behavior for grains at different positions in its diffuse ISM.

quotes.quote: In Sect.  sec:illustration we illustrate our model for the case of the well studied galaxy NGC 891.
In our calculations we take h^ disk _s(B)=h^ disk _ s, ref =5670 pc, the fixed reference scalelength of our model galaxy, as derived for NGC 891.

In Fig.  fig:infrared_brightness we show examples of calculated infrared brightnesses (from Eq.  eq:infraredbrightness ) for grains of different sizes and compositions placed at different positions within the diffuse ISM of our prototype galaxy NGC 891. For the calculations we used the probability distributions of temperature shown in Fig.  fig:temp_distrib .

sources.summary: NGC 891 is the disk-dominated member of the edge-on pair; its rotation curve differs from NGC 7814, showing a less centrally concentrated mass and light distribution.

quotes.quote: The two edge-on galaxies NGC 891 and NGC 7814 are representative of two extreme morphologies: the former is disk-dominated while the latter is almost entirely bulge-dominated.

We have derived new rotation curves from recent, high-sensitivity \ observations and have found that the shapes of the rotation curves are significantly different for the two galaxies. They indicate that in NGC 7814 the mass is more concentrated to the centre as compared to NGC 891. This reflects the distribution of light which is more centrally concentrated in NGC 7814 than in NGC 891.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 891 to illustrate disk-parallel and X-shaped halo magnetic fields, with rotation measures indicating a coherent, even-parity ASS field whose radial component points outward.

quotes.quote: Also the rotation measures RM in NGC 891 show an ordered, large-scale pattern along the major axis of the galaxy with negative values in the norh-east and positve values in the south-west. This indicates a coherent magnetic field pattern within the galactic disk. It is generally thought to be generated by a mean-field dynamo for which the most easily excited field pattern is the axismmetric spiral (ASS) field (e.g. Beck et al. 1996). As there is no change in the sign of the RM visible across the major axis of NGC 891, the ASS field is of even parity in this galaxy. Taking into account that the north-eastern side of NGC 891 is the approaching side (e.g. from HI, Rupen 1991) (with RM beeing negative, hence the magnetic field is pointing away from us) and the south-western side is the receeding one (with RM beeing positive) we conclude that the radial component of the ASS magnetic field points outwards .

sources.summary: NGC 891 is used as a deep H I comparison spiral, with halo and disk-halo gas mostly tied to the disk or satellite interaction and exceeding M31's amount because of higher star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 has also been mapped to 10^ 18 cm ^ 2 levels at this resolution, but this galaxy has a much higher SFR than the Milky Way oosterloo07 .

Both of these galaxies are shown in the top panels of Figure fig:other . The majority of their halo gas is connected to the HI disk (like most other galaxies), and/or could be associated with a satellite interaction.
NGC 891 shows a larger amount of halo and disk-halo gas, which is consistent with its higher star formation rate oosterloo07 .

sources.summary: The paper notes cirrus near NGC 891, measures its UV halo properties, and argues its bright H-alpha halo exceeds expectations from halo star formation alone.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 has more cirrus emission on the west side of the galaxy (but the surface brightness is still very low relative to the galaxy). This was partially corrected by using different background regions on each side of the galaxy.

For example, in NGC 891, the FUV halo flux integrated above 1 kpc reqires a halo SFR of 0.028 M_ yr ^ 1 using the formulas in kennicutt98,parnovsky13 . We would expect some associated H emission, but NGC 891 has a bright H halo; using the scale height of 520 pc and total L_ H 9 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 from howk00 , the diffuse H above 1 kpc is over 7 times higher than that expected from halo star formation.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 891 as a prototypical edge-on spiral and presents Herschel far-infrared cooling-line spectroscopy, deriving PDR gas densities and incident FUV radiation-field strengths across its disc.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 is a prototypical example of a near perfect edge-on ( i > 89 ^ , e.g. xilouris1998 ), non-interacting spiral galaxy (SA(s)b, RC21976 ) located right in our neighbourhood ( D = 9.6 Mpc, e.g. strickland2004 ), and which exhibits many properties similar to our own Milky Way.
1.2 We present Herschel PACS and SPIRE FTS spectroscopy, focussing on the most important far-infrared cooling lines in NGC 891: 158 m, 122, 205 m, 63, 145 m, and 88 m.

We compare the observed flux of the FIR cooling lines and total IR emission with the predicted flux from a PDR model to determine the characteristics of the gas such as density, temperature and the incident far-ultraviolet radiation field, G_ 0 , resolving details on physical scales of roughly 0.6 kpc. A pixel-by-pixel analysis reveals that, with the exception of the central region, the majority of the PDRs in NGC 891's disc have hydrogen nuclei with densities ranging from 1 < n/ cm ^ 3 < 3.5 with a mean of n/ cm ^ 3 3 , and experience an incident FUV radiation field with a strength between 1.7 < G_0 < 3 normalised to the habing1968 Field.

sources.summary: The paper identifies [ TT2009 ] 25 as NGC 891 dwA, a low-surface-brightness transition dwarf probable companion of NGC 891, with faint UV flux and absorption-line velocity supporting that status.

quotes.quote: [ TT2009 ] 25 = NGC 891 dwA. This is one of two dwarf galaxies with low surface brightnesses discovered [ 23 ] in the neighborhood of the spiral galaxy NGC 891. Even earlier it was noted as a probable companion of NGC 891 in a survey with the MegaCam on the 3.6-meter CFHT telescope [ 25 ] . Judging from the smooth structure and the presence of a faint UV flux, [ TT2009 ] 25 can be classified as a dwarf system of type dTr, intermediate between dSph and dIr. Only absorption lines show up in its spectrum; this indicate a heliocentric velocity for this galaxy of 692 58 km/s, which is consistent with its status as a companion of the spiral NGC 891, for which V_h = 526 km/s.

sources.summary: The chapter describes NGC 891 as a Milky-Way analog whose deep Subaru imaging resolved RGB stars and revealed looping halo features plus a super-thick disk, both suggesting recent accretion.

quotes.quote: Perhaps the most spectacular example of this from ground-based observations is the Milky Way analog NGC 891 NGC 891 , at a distance of 10 Mpc, which was surveyed by mouhcine2010 with Suprime-Cam on the 8.2m Subaru telescope. With very long ( >11 hr) exposures, this study resolved stars to 2 magnitudes below the RGB tip RGB tip stars (TRGB) in NGC 891, covering a 90 90 kpc area around the galaxy down to i band magnitudes fainter than 28th mag (see Figure n891 ). Surface density maps of RGB stars show a complex of features looping throughout the halo of NGC 891 NGC 891 , suggesting that the halo of this galaxy contains numerous accretion remnants. The disk of NGC 891 also appears to be ``super-thick'' mouhcine2010 , providing further evidence of recent accretion.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 891 as a rejected Planck blackbox candidate: a nearby infrared-bright starburst whose microwave emission comes from synchrotron, free-free, and dust components and shows positive spectral curvature.

quotes.quote: Three of the sources (NGC 253, NGC 891, and M82) are nearby infrared-bright starburst galaxies Sanders03 .

The 100 GHz luminosity of these galaxies is a combination of synchrotron emission from cosmic ray electrons, free-free emission from H II regions, and thermal emission from dust Williams10 . The 217 GHz luminosity is mostly the thermal glow of dust heated by starlight in the starburst (and Seyfert) regions Williams10,Peel11 .
This is demonstrated by the positive curvature in their microwave spectra, in contrast to the negative curvature expected of a blackbox spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 891 as an edge-on galaxy with eDIG velocity gradients, deep detections to about 10 kpc, and HOLMES-based explanations for line ratios.

quotes.quote: Another possible additional heating source are hot, low-mass evolved stars (HOLMES) in the thick disk and stellar halo, which have been proposed to explain the emission line ratios in NGC 891 2011MNRAS.415.2182F .

In a few studies 2006ApJ...647.1018H,2006ApJ...636..181H,2007A&A...468..951K,2007ApJ...663..933H , the outer regions of several galaxies, NGC 5775, NGC 891, and NGC 4302, were found to have negative velocity gradients with distance in the eDIG. These gradients were inconsistent with the ballistic model of 2002ApJ...578...98C for a star formation driven disk-halo flow.
Previous very deep observations of NGC 891 and NGC 5775 have detections out to about 10 kpc 1997ApJ...474..129R,2000ApJ...537L..13R .

sources.summary: The catalog describes NGC 891 as an edge-on spiral whose dust lane obscures the nucleus; a Spitzer centroid is adopted and an X-ray counterpart is classified as an AGN candidate.

quotes.quote: NGC 891 is a nearly edge-on spiral, and the nucleus is masked by a dust lane, which is visible on 2MASS images even in the K_ s (2.16 ) band, challenging the validity of using its position. On Spitzer images at 3.6 , the dust lane is gone, and the centroid of the nucleus is found to be R.A. = 2 ^ h 22 ^ m 33 03, Dec. = + 42 20 53 2 (J2000.0), which is 0 76 away from the 2MASS position for extended sources. We therefore quote this as the center for NGC 891. An X-ray counterpart is found around it and is still classified as an AGN candidate.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts NGC 891 with NGC 3198, describing a lopsided, less extended H I disk and arguing stellar processes likely disturb its gas distribution.

quotes.quote: For example, in a deep \ observation of the edge-on spiral NGC 891 (which has a noticeably lopsided \ distribution between the two sides), Oosterloo et al (2007) found that the radial extent of the gas increased very little from earlier studies, but discovered a low column density component that extended to large distances above the plane; they argued that this was evidence against the CIB-photoionization model for the radial edges of the \ disk, which were likely to be physical edges. We note simply that (a) this is very plausible, but (b) the presence of gas at such large heights above the plane indicates physical conditions very different from those required for the models of Maloney (1993) to apply, e.g., the presence of a galactic fountain, in which case the high-altitude atomic hydrogen is almost certainly cooling and condensing and unevenly distributed in a very clumpy medium. In addition, the \ disk of NGC 891 is much less extended compared to the optical disk than that of NGC 3198: on the northern side of the disk it only extends about 4.5 optical scalelengths, while for the southern side it is about 6. Hence it is more likely that the \ distribution in NGC 891 has been disturbed by stellar processes; the very extended \ disk of NGC 3198 (more than 11 disk scalelengths) is precisely why it was chosen by van Albada et al for their deep imaging experiment.

sources.summary: The paper studies compact 8 micron cores in NGC 891, finding 173 cores, a 105 pc half-thickness that increases with radius, and loop-like extraplanar core structures.

quotes.quote: To study the vertical distribution of the earliest stages of star formation in galaxies, three edge-on spirals, NGC 891, NGC 3628, and IC 5052 observed by the Spitzer Space Telescope InfraRed Array Camera (IRAC) were examined for compact 8 m cores using an unsharp mask technique; 173, 267, and 60 cores were distinguished, respectively.

The rms dispersion of the midplane distance, considered to be the half-thickness of the disk distribution of 8 m cores, is 105 pc for NGC 891, 438 pc for NGC 3628, and 74 pc for IC 5052. For NGC 891 and IC 5052, these are good representations of the disk half-thickness, but NGC 3628 is warped as a result of a recent interaction it is part of the Leo triplet, Arp 317, arp66 , and it is much thicker on the western side.
NGC 891 has a steadily increasing distance to the midplane that varies as H( pc )=(49.9 32.9)+(6.3 3.1) R( kpc ), which is from 71 pc inside 6 kpc (the first three crosses) to 142 pc outside 12 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper compares synthetic far-infrared polarization with HAWC+ data for NGC 891, finding broad agreement but tension in edge-on line-of-sight averaging at high intensities.

quotes.quote: While HAWC+ has indeed been able to trace the magnetic field structure in M51 and NGC 891 2020JonesHAWCFarinfraredObservations , these results probe a relatively limited part of the magnetic field geometry compared with similar extinction measurements.
We compare our predictions with HAWC+ data for M51 and NGC 891, and investigate how the synthetic polarisation signal varies as a function of the instrument resolution and wavelength range.

Our edge-on results show a clear tension with the NGC 891 results. While in this case the polarisation geometry agrees well, there is no evidence of line-of-sight averaging at high intensities in our edge-on images, while such a signal is indeed observed in NGC 891.

sources.summary: Using Herschel profiles, the paper finds that NGC 0891 requires an additional thicker dust component: the vertical emission dominates above the model at 1-2 kpc, making it an outlier from single-component fits.

quotes.quote: The vertical profiles for 6 galaxies (ESO 209-009, NGC 0891, NGC 3628, NGC 4013, NGC 4437, and NGC 4631) show the presence of another (thicker) dust emission component in all or almost all wavebands. This component clearly dominates over the model at larger vertical distances above the galaxy mid-plane, usually starting at z=1-2 kpc. For these galaxies, the average ^2=19.8 10.1 compared to ^2=1.04 0.45 for the whole sample without these outliers. This proves that another dust component is required to better describe the observed profiles of these six galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 891 as an external edge-on spiral comparison for halo magnetic fields, depolarization, and the vertical rise of polarized radio emission.

quotes.quote: Polarized radio emission, indicating the presence of a regular magnetic field, has been detected high in the haloes of the edge-on spiral galaxies to z 4 in NGC 891 and to z 8 in NGC 4631 (Hummel, Beck Dahlem 1991).

Polarized radio emission from the haloes of the edge-on spiral galaxies NGC 891 and NGC 4631 is significantly depolarized by turbulent magnetic fields (Hummel et al. \ 1991) and any large scale fields present in the halo must become tangled by turbulence and Parker and thermal instabilities (Beck et al. \ 1996), so we expect to see a turbulent component of the magnetic field at all z .

Observations of polarized radio emission from edge-on galaxies, such as NGC 891 (Hummel et al. \ 1991, Dumke et al. \ 1995) and NGC 4631 (Hummel et al. \ 1991, Golla Hummel 1994, Dumke et al. \ 1995) show the degree of polarization rising with z from a minimum at z=0 . The observed small P at the midplane, e.g. \ P _ z=0 1 in NGC 891 (Hummel et al. \ 1991), can be explained by strong Faraday depolarization by both regular and turbulent fields in the disc.

sources.summary: The paper applies a multi-component vertical disk model to NGC 891, treating stellar-velocity and luminosity scalelengths freely and finding moderate stellar scaleheight flaring within the optical radius.

quotes.quote: We next applied this general model to two external, prototypical edge-on spiral galaxies: NGC 891 and NGC 4565, for which gas density values are known from observations - see Narayan Jog (2002a) for details. Hence, our model is applied to NGC 891 and NGC 4565, treating the above ratio as a free parameter. The resulting stellar scaleheight is not strictly constant but instead shows flaring by a factor of 2 within the optical radius (Fig. 2).
The above model is applied to two external galaxies, NGC 891 and NGC 4565, and the resulting stellar scaleheight is shown to be not constant with radius, instead it shows flaring.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 891 as an edge-on spiral with extra-planar gas whose observed rotational velocity decreases with height, reproduced using baroclinic stationary fluid solutions.

quotes.quote: We now apply the general results of Sect. modelli to the modeling of the extra-planar gas of NGC 891 in an attempt to reproduce its major features and in particular the decline of the rotational velocity with increasing z . The rotational velocity field resulting from this modeling will be compared with the rotation curves derived by Frat.05 . The galaxy model We consider a very idealized mass model for NGC 891, consisting of three components: an exponential stellar disk, a dark matter halo with an asymptotically flat rotation curve, and a centrally peaked density distribution.
Application of our method to the case of the edge-on galaxy NGC 891 has shown that with baroclinic solutions it is possible to reproduce the observed vertical decrease of the gas rotational velocity.

sources.summary: The review highlights NGC 891 as an edge-on galaxy with a thick gaseous envelope, high HI filaments, and declining rotation inconsistent with a simple galactic fountain.

quotes.quote: The example of the edge-on galaxy NGC 891 is spectacular (Fraternali et al. 2007). Not only a thick gaseous envelope is seen at a thickness of 8kpc, but HI gas filaments are obvious up to 20kpc height above the galaxy plane. The rotational velocity of this gas decreases with z, and its origin cannot be due to a galactic fountain phenomenon, coming from the star formation feedback, which will conserve angular momentum.

sources.summary: The paper compares Milky Way H I modeling with NGC 891, noting beards in position-velocity diagrams, low-level vertical emission, and deeper mapping that found vertical low-level emission while radial limits remained.

quotes.quote: Such features at levels below -20 dB were first observed in NGC 891 Swaters1997 , the so-called beards Fraternali2001 .
At low levels it is far easier to find extended emission perpendicular to the disk as shown recently by Oosterloo2007 in the case of NGC 891.

Maloney's interpretation was questioned recently by Oosterloo2007 who found that a deeper mapping of NGC891 led predominantly to the detection of unknown low level emission in the vertical direction, while the radial limits remained.

sources.summary: The GHOSTS survey finds NGC 891 has a roughly -2.5 outer-envelope profile at 20 kpc, an outer minor-axis excess above extrapolation, and high stellar-envelope metallicity.

quotes.quote: At 20 kpc NGC 891, NGC 4565, and NGC 7814 have power law profiles with a slope of about -2.5.
Many galaxies show deviations from smooth radial profiles, most notably NGC 891, where our outer minor axis field is much higher than expected based on an extrapolation of the inner halo profile.
The more massive galaxies (NGC 891, NGC 3031, NGC 4565, NGC 7814) have significantly higher metallicities than the lower mass galaxies (IC5052, NGC4244, NGC7793) as has been seen before e.g., Mou07 .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 891 as a local Milky Way analogue whose spheroid flattening matches the mean flattening found in the simulated galaxy sample.

quotes.quote: The flattening of the spheroid of NGC 891, a local Milky Way analogue, has been measured by ibata09 . They find a flattening of b/a 0.5 out to a distance of 25 kpc. Both M31 and NGC 891 therefore show a level of flattening that is comparable to the mean value derived from our simulated galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 891 as a comparison system whose observed lag has been reproduced by infall models and whose DIG and H i lags agree.

quotes.quote: Explanations involving infalling gas such as interaction of disk-halo cycled gas with a possible hot, low angular momentum, low metallicity halo predicted by cosmological simulations 2011MNRAS.415.1534M and the hydrodynamic simulation of disk formation in 2006MNRAS.370.1612K have successfully reproduced the observed lag in NGC 891. However, while much attention has been focused on results for NGC 891, observed lags differ among the three galaxies considered in 2007ApJ...663..933H and one would like to understand the physical cause of such variations.
These include NGC 891 2007AJ....134.1019O , where DIG and H i lags agree, pointing to similar origins for both components, and other galaxies summarized in Section6.4 .

sources.summary: The paper notes its NGC 891 absorption measurement, prior CO observations of this edge-on galaxy, and that the emission extends along the disk but concentrates in the nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 891: Our 1_ 10 1_ 11 absorption measurement was reported in Mangum2008 (Figure fig:ExgalFormSingles-OnePage ). GarciaBurillo1995 measured the CO J= 2-1 and 1-0 emission toward this edge-on galaxy. The emission is extended along the galactic disk to spatial scales 120^ 20^ , but is mainly concentrated in a nuclear condensation 20^ 10^ in extent.

sources.summary: The paper reports a new ammonia detection toward NGC 891 and infers only an upper limit on kinetic temperature, with dense gas concentrated near the nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 891: This is a new detection of the NH _3 (1,1) transition toward this galaxy. With only a limit to the (2,2) and (4,4) intensities we derive only an upper limit to the kinetic temperature. High spatial resolution measurements of dense gas tracers in NGC 891 see summary in Mangum2013 suggest that the molecular emission is mainly confined to a nuclear condensation that is 20^ 10^ in extent.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 891 as one of three galaxies with clear extraplanar dust; it has among the highest thick-dust scale heights and UV halo excess not explained by PSF wings.

quotes.quote: Three galaxies (NGC 891, NGC 3628, and UGC 11794) show clear evidence for the existence of extraplanar dust layer.
NGC 891 has the highest z_d^ thick as does NGC 3628.
The vertical profiles of NGC 891, NGC 3628, and UGC 11794 clearly show the halo emission excess which cannot be explained with the PSF wing.

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 891 as a spiral with a spectroscopically confirmed thick DIG layer, halo-temperature evidence, and a TAURUS data cube used to demonstrate sky correction.

quotes.quote: The first imaging detection of a 1 kpc thick layer of DIG in NGC 891 (Rand et al. 1990, Dettmar 1990) was meanwhile confirmed spectroscopically (Rand 1997) extending the detection limit out to at least 5 kpc above the plane.

However, it is unclear what additional process(es) (turbulence, dust heating, shocks, magnetic reconnection) contribute(s) to the heating, in particular since there is more and more observational evidence that the temperature is increasing with height above the plane z (Reynolds et al. 1999, for the Milky Way; Rand 1997, for NGC 891; T ullmann and Dettmar 2000, for a sample based on ESO/La Silla observations).

To demonstrate the gain in S/N for the detection of emission lines in the presence of bright night sky lines by using a Fabry-Perot we reproduce in Fig. 3 a channel of a TAURUS data cube of NGC 891 before and after correction for the night sky contribution.

gmw_00000412.name: COSMOS J100043.15+020637.2
gmw_00000412.overview: COSMOS J100043.15+020637.2 is a galaxy merger remnant at z=0.359 that features a tidal tail and two closely separated stellar bulges [arXiv:0906.3517, arXiv:2307.05852]. High-resolution imaging confirms that only the southeastern core hosts an active, unobscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1412.0004, arXiv:2307.05852].

sources.summary: CID-42 is described as an unusual merger remnant with spatial and spectroscopic AGN offsets; gas-rich major-merger simulations match its morphology while recoiling and dual-AGN interpretations remain viable.

quotes.quote: The galaxy CXOC J100043.1+020637, also known as CID-42, is a highly unusual object. An apparent galaxy merger remnant, it displays signatures of both an inspiraling, kiloparsec-scale active galactic nucleus (AGN) pair and of a recoiling AGN with a kick velocity 1300 . Among recoiling AGN candidates, CID-42 alone has both spatial offsets (in optical and X-ray bands) and spectroscopic offsets. In order to constrain the relative likelihood of both scenarios, we develop models using hydrodynamic galaxy merger simulations coupled with radiative transfer calculations. Our gas-rich, major merger models are generally well matched to the galactic morphology and to the inferred stellar mass and star formation rate.

For the dual AGN scenario, an unusually large broad-line offset is required, and the best match to the observed morphology requires a galaxy that is less luminous than CID-42. Further, the lack of X-ray emission from one of the two optical nuclei is not easily attributed to an intrinsically quiescent SMBH or to a Compton-thick galactic environment. While the current data do not allow either the recoiling or the dual AGN scenario for CID-42 to be excluded, our models highlight the most relevant parameters for distinguishing these possibilities with future observations.

sources.summary: The paper presents COSMOS J100043.15+020637.2 as a z=0.36 merger-remnant galaxy hosting dual AGN with spatial and velocity offsets supported by imaging, spectra, and multiwavelength detections.

quotes.quote: We present strong evidence for dual active galactic nuclei (AGN) in the z=0.36 galaxy COSMOS J100043.15+020637.2. COSMOS Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) imaging of the galaxy shows a tidal tail, indicating that the galaxy recently underwent a merger, as well as two bright point sources near the galaxy's center. Both the luminosities of these sources (derived from the HST image) and their emission line flux ratios (derived from Keck/DEIMOS slit spectroscopy) suggest that both are AGN and not star-forming regions or supernovae. Observations from zCOSMOS, Sloan Digital Sky Survey, XMM-Newton , Very Large Array, and Spitzer fortify the evidence for AGN activity.

With HST imaging we measure a projected spatial offset between the two AGN of 1.75 0.03 h^ 1 kpc, and with DEIMOS we measure a 150 40 km s ^ 1 line-of-sight velocity offset between the two AGN. Combined, these observations provide substantial evidence that COSMOS J100043.15+020637.2 is a dual AGN in a merger-remnant galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper studies CID-42 with deep VLA radio data, associating the 3 GHz emission with the southeastern AGN component and finding the data consistent with a recoiling black-hole scenario.

quotes.quote: We present deep 3 GHz VLA observations of the potentially recoiling black hole CID-42 in the COSMOS field. This galaxy shows two optical nuclei in the HST/ACS image and a large velocity offset of 1300 km s ^ 1 between the broad and narrow H emission line although the spectrum is not spacially resolved ( civano10 ).

With a 7 detection we find that the entire observed 3 GHz radio emission can be associated with the South-Eastern component of CID-42, coincident with the detected X-ray emission. We use our 3 GHz data combined with other radio data from the literature ranging from 320 MHz to 9 GHz, which include the VLA, VLBA and GMRT data, to construct a radio synchrotron spectrum of CID-42. The radio spectrum suggests a type I unobscured radio-quiet flat-spectrum AGN in the South-Eastern component which may be surrounded by a more extended region of old synchrotron electron population or shocks generated by the outflow from the supermassive black hole. Our data are consistent with the recoiling black hole picture but cannot rule out the presence of an obscured and radio-quiet SMBH in the North-Western component.

sources.summary: JWST imaging confirms CID-42 is an advanced merging pair at z=0.359 with two stellar bulges, a tidal tail, and only the SE core hosting an active AGN.

quotes.quote: The galaxy CID-42 (CXOC J100043.1+020637.2) at z=0.359 has been proposed to contain a promising candidate for a gravitational wave recoiling supermassive black hole (SMBH), a slingshot SMBH from a triple-SMBH interaction, or a kpc-scale dual Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN).

With improved spatial resolution, depth, and IR wavelength coverage, NIRCam multi-band imaging from the COSMOS-Web JWST treasury program well resolved the two cores in CID-42, revealing a significant stellar bulge for both cores (with stellar masses of 10^ 10 M_ for both). JWST imaging further revealed that only the SE core contains an unobscured AGN point source, based on both image decomposition and spectral energy distribution fitting. There is no evidence for AGN activity in the NW core. These new observations unambiguously rule out the GW-recoiling and slingshot SMBH scenarios, and establish CID-42 as a low-redshift merging pair of galaxies, with only one active AGN in the system.

CID-42, a galaxy merger at z=0.359 displaying two closely-separated bright nuclei, a prominent tidal tail, a significant velocity offset between the broad and narrow components of the line, and X-ray detection for only one of the nuclei, represents one of the most studied candidates for a GW recoiling SMBH.

sources.summary: The paper describes CID-42 as a peculiar COSMOS galaxy with two compact optical sources, a large velocity offset, unusual X-ray iron features, and possible recoil or slingshot SMBH scenarios.

quotes.quote: Source CXOC J100043.1+020637, also known as CID-42, presents two compact optical sources embedded in the same galaxy. The distance between the two, measured in the HST/ACS image, is 0.495" 0.005" that, at the redshift of the source, corresponds to a projected separation of 2.46 0.02 kpc. A large ( 1200 km/s) velocity offset between the narrow and broad components of H has been measured in three different optical spectra from the VLT/VIMOS and Magellan/IMACS instruments. CID-42 is also the only X-ray source in COSMOS having in its X-ray spectra a strong redshifted broad absorption iron line, and an iron emission line, drawing an inverted P-Cygni profile. The \ and \ data show that the absorption line is variable in energy by E=500 eV over 4 years and that the absorber has to be highly ionized, in order not to leave a signature in the soft X-ray spectrum. That these features, the morphology, the velocity offset and the inverted P-Cygni profile, occur in the same source is unlikely to be a coincidence.

sources.summary: The review presents CID-42 as an X-ray COSMOS source with two close compact nuclei, offset optical lines, variable iron absorption, and possible recoil or dual-AGN interpretations.

quotes.quote: This result is not dissimilar to what has been found using SDSS data by other groups ( and references therein satyapaletal14,Ellison2011 ) It is worth mentioning the case of the X-ray COSMOS source CID-42 at z=0.36 that was found to host two compact nuclei at close separation ( 0.5 arcsec, corresponding to about 2.5 kpc; see Civano2010 ). Optical spectroscopy indicates the presence of a large 1200 km s ^ 1 velocity offset between the narrow and broad components of the H emission line (see also Comerford2009 ). The X-ray spectra show an absorbing feature (with an inverted P-Cygni profile) at the energy corresponding to iron line, variable in energy and intensity on year-timescale, indicative of a high-velocity inflow (v/ 0.02-0.14; Civano2010, Lanzuisi2013 ). Two scenarios may explain these results: either a GW recoiling black hole (e.g., Peres1962,Campanelli2007,Blecha2011 ) or a dual AGN system, where one of the two is recoiling because of slingshot (i.e., a 3-body BH scattering) effect.

sources.summary: The paper treats CID-42 as a leading offset-AGN recoil candidate whose evidence can fit a rapidly recoiling black hole, while noting a dual black hole alternative.

quotes.quote: Arguably the most promising candidates are those with both spatial and velocity offset signatures civano10, civano12, chiabe17 ; CID-42 is one such example (Figure fig:candidates ). Simulations indicate that this system can be well-modeled as a rapidly recoiling BH with a plausible offset AGN lifetime blecha13a . These combined lines of evidence present a compelling case for a rapidly recoiling BH in CID-42, but the data could also be explained as an inspiraling, kpc-scale BH pair in which one BH is quiescent or intrinsically faint.

gmw_00000413.name: NGC 6764
gmw_00000413.overview: NGC 6764 is a composite active galactic nucleus and starburst galaxy [arXiv:1009.0702] classified as a Seyfert or LINER [arXiv:1807.00979]. It hosts a parsec-scale synchrotron jet [arXiv:1009.0702] and a thick nuclear disk with large molecular-line velocity widths [arXiv:1602.06452].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 6764 as a composite AGN-starburst galaxy whose parsec-scale synchrotron jet may connect to, and power, larger radio bubbles.

quotes.quote: We have observed the composite active galactic nucleus (AGN)-starburst galaxy NGC 6764 with the Very Long Baseline Array at 1.6 and 4.9 GHz. These observations have detected a ``core-jet'' structure and a possible weak counterjet component at 1.6 GHz. The upper limits to the core and jet (1.6 4.9 GHz) spectral index are 0.6 and 0.3, respectively. Taken together with the high brightness temperature of 10^ 7 K for the core region, the radio emission appears to be coming from a synchrotron jet.

At a position angle of 25 , the parsec-scale jet seems to be pointing closely toward the western edge of the southern kpc-scale bubble in NGC 6764. A real connection between the parsec- and sub-kpc-scale emission would not only suggest the presence of a curved jet, but also a close link between the AGN jet and the radio bubbles in NGC 6764.
The jet energetics in NGC 6764 suggest that it would take 12 21 Myr to inflate the (southern) bubble.

sources.summary: The paper spatially resolves HCN and HCO+ emission in NGC 6764, finding similar line distributions and kinematics that require a thick nuclear disk with large molecular-line velocity width.

quotes.quote: NGC 6764 The 3 mm continuum map is shown in the bottom left panel of Figure fig:conhcnhco . Its FWHM of 1.04 0.82 and PA of 8 indicate it is spatially unresolved. Within a 3 aperture, the continuum flux density is 0.6 mJy. Figure fig:conhcnhco also shows the integrated HCN (1-0) and HCO ^+ (1-0) maps in the bottom middle and right panels, and the measurements and the line fluxes are summarised in Table tab:emi_info . The HCN (1-0) emission has an extent of 1.7 1.3 at PA 54 , similar to the 1.7 1.2 at PA 66 of the HCO ^+ (1-0) line.
We conclude that the observational constraints require a thick disk model to explain the line emission distribution and kinematics, and in particular the large velocity width of the molecular lines.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6764 as a Seyfert/LINER with nuclear starburst, parsec-to-kiloparsec radio emission fit by a precessing jet, and limited radio flux attributable to stellar winds.

quotes.quote: A precessing radio jet could also explain the radio emission on parsec-, sub-kpc- and kpc-scales in the Seyfert / LINER galaxy with an ongoing nuclear starburst, NGC 6764 Kharb10 . Interestingly, the precession model best-fit values of jet inclination ( 18^ ) and jet speed ( 0.028c ) could explain the observed jet-to-counterjet surface brightness ratio ( R_J 1.2 ) in NGC 6764. We examined the starburst-wind contribution to the radio emission in NGC 6764 and another Seyfert + starburst composite galaxy, NGC 3079 see Irwin03 . We found that only about 25-30 of the total radio flux density appears to arise in clear equatorial emission that could be attributable to stellar winds, in both these sources.
In Mrk 6 and NGC 6764 and possibly NGC 3079, precessing jets launched from their black hole accretion disk systems, could be powering the KSRs and dissipating on sub-kpc or kpc-scales.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 6764 as a starburst/composite galaxy, separating narrow star-forming lines from broader Liner-like emission in its spectra.

quotes.quote: NGC 6764 has been called a Seyfert 2 galaxy by Rubin et al. (1975), in spite of \ being stronger than 5007; this classification was based on the width of the \ and \ lines ( 750 ) but Wilson Nath (1990) found these lines to be much narrower ( 380 \ FWHM). Koski (1978) noticed the presence of weak \ absorption lines and, from the line intensity ratios, concluded that it was very much like ``normal'' galaxies, while for Shuder Osterbrock (1981), it is not a Seyfert 2. Using the line ratios published by Koski (1978), Ferland Netzer (1983) classified it as a Liner. Osterbrock Cohen (1982) have detected in the spectra of this object the 4650 Wolf-Rayet emission feature.

sources.summary: The paper reports parsec-scale core-jet structure in NGC6764, large jet-lobe misalignment, and Chandra evidence that its X-ray gas is shock-heated by an AGN outflow.

quotes.quote: Phase-referenced VLBI observations at 1.6 and 4.9 GHz of two Seyfert galaxies with lobes, namely NGC6764 and Mrk6, have revealed the presence of parsec-scale core-jet structures in them Kharb10a,Kharb14 . These jets show a large misalignment ( 50^ 60^ ) with the kpc-scale lobes.

Chandra observations of Mrk6 and NGC6764 indicate that the X-ray emitting gas in these Seyferts is shock-heated by an AGN outflow Mingo11,Croston08 . Therefore, in these Seyfert galaxies and others, there are indications that the radio lobes are AGN outflow rather than starburst wind driven.

gmw_00000414.name: NGC 7673
gmw_00000414.overview: NGC 7673 is a nearby starburst galaxy [arXiv:1011.4220] characterized as a clumpy, rotating disk galaxy [arXiv:0708.2703, arXiv:astro-ph/0411465] with a faint, extended outer disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0411597]. The galaxy exhibits a disturbed appearance [arXiv:0708.2703, arXiv:1412.4739] and contains centrally peaked neutral hydrogen emission [arXiv:0708.2703], likely influenced by its nearby companion, Mrk 326 [arXiv:0708.2703, arXiv:1412.4739].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 325 as a clumpy star-forming disk with disturbed, centrally peaked HI and a nearby companion whose emission affects the system mapping.

quotes.quote: Mrk 325 Mrk 325 has clumpy star forming regions throughout its disk 1999ApJ...522..199H, 1976A&A....47..371C , and is commonly cited as a Clumpy Irregular Galaxy e.g. 1987IAUS..115..599H , while NED and HyperLeda classify it as a spiral. The disturbed appearance of the \ in Mrk 325, as well as its starburst activity, may be explained by its companion, Mrk 326, roughly 7 ^ to the southeast 2000AJ....119...79C, 1999ApJ...522..199H . Figures f:MRK325.HI.MOM0 and f:MRK325.HI.DSS show centrally peaked, but disturbed, \ emission from Mrk 325.

Figure f:MRK325.HI.MOM1 shows a fairly constant \ velocity, with a small gradient, across Mrk 325. Figure f:MRK325.HI.MOM0 also shows the diffuse \ emission of the companion, Mrk 326. However, note that the \ emission shown for Mrk 326 is not representative of the actual gas distribution as some emission has been subtracted off as part of the continuum subtraction for the target, Mrk 325. The actual \ distribution of Mrk 326 is symmetric and centered on the optical emission.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 7673 as a nearby starburst LCBG, mapping extinction, star formation, metallicity, clumpy structure, and evidence for interaction-driven activity.

quotes.quote: Physical properties of the star-forming regions in the local Luminous Compact Blue Galaxy (LCBG) NGC 7673 are studied in detail using 3D spectroscopic data taken with the PPAK integral field unit at the 3.5-m telescope in the Centro Astron omico Hispano Alem an. We derive integrated and spatially resolved properties such as extinction, star formation rate (SFR) and metallicity for this galaxy. Our data show an extinction map with maximum values located at the position of the main clumps of star formation showing small spatial variations ( E(B-V)_ t =0.12-0.21 mag ). We derive a H based SFR for this galaxy of 6.2 0.8 M_ /yr in agreement with the SFR derived from infrared and radio continuum fluxes.

NGC 7673, also known as UCM2325+2318 and MRK325, is a nearby ( z = 0.011368 ) starburst galaxy widely studied in the past e.g., duflot82,homeier02,pasquali08,homeier99 . The small size, high surface brightness, strong emission lines and blue colours make NGC 7673 a prototypical LCBG (see Table table:NGC7673properties ). The starburst activity in this galaxy is located within a circle of 3.7 kpc radius ( 2 R_ e , where R_ e is the effective radius). It shows a clumpy structure with star-forming regions visible in optical as bright knots in the galactic disk detected 87 distinct star-forming knots in a deep H image of this galaxy perez-gonzalez03 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7673 as a nearby starburst with UV-bright clumps resolved into many clusters, a largely rotating face-on disk, and cluster spectra used to probe its ultraviolet light.

quotes.quote: NGC 7673 is a fascinating, nearby ( d=49 Mpc) starburst, whose star formation activity is concentrated in large complexes, the distinctive "clumps", clearly visible in the optical images as several bright knots in the galactic disk. Since initial investigations, these clumps were found to be hyperactive star-forming regions. Benvenuti et al. (1982) discovered that they radiate in the far UV, on average, 100 times more than the giant H ii region 30 Doradus. Recently, these clumps have been resolved by HST/WFPC2 images into a large number of star clusters ( ).

Despite the disturbed optical appearance (the spiral pattern is asymmetric) and the large-scale starburst, H _ kinematics ( ) demonstrates that NGC 7673 is a relatively unperturbed, rotating disk galaxy seen nearly face-on. Thus an ongoing interaction of NGC 7673 with its neighbour NGC 7677 can be ruled out as the trigger of the recent star formation in NGC 7673, though a past interaction or a minor merger with a dwarf companion cannot be excluded ( ). 31 of the star clusters in NGC 7673, fall inside the IUE large aperture which was used to acquire an integrated UV spectrum of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 325 as a disturbed rotating galaxy with an H I-detected companion, complex velocity field, minor-merger evidence, and possible warp.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Mrk 325 is at the J2000 (RA, Dec) position (23:27:41.0, +23:35:21)

quotes.quote: Mrk 325 (NGC 7673) is a disturbed galaxy that has a companion, Mrk 326, that was observed in H I by N97 and that we also detect in our H I observations. PG10 observed Mrk 325 in H and found that, while it has an asymmetric, complex velocity field likely resulting from a minor merger, it is a rotating galaxy with a position angle of 168 ^ and V_ rot \ = 30 km s^ 1 before correcting for inclination. The minor merger may be with an infalling dwarf galaxy, which PG10 find has a separate kinematic component from the rest of Mrk 325. CG07 observed Mrk 325 in H I with the VLA in C configuration with a beam size of 20 and found that its emission is disturbed and shows a small velocity gradient. Our H I map, which combines the CG07 map with new B configuration data and archival D configuration maps from N97 , shows similar morphology and velocity information, and does not feature an obvious major axis of rotation.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7673 as a nearby LCBG-like comparison object with suitable compact blue properties, a faint outer disk, and clear inner starburst-region rotation.

quotes.quote: NGC 7673 has been suggested by Homeier et al. (2002) as a nearby example. Indeed this source has the right color, surface-brightness, size, luminosity and integrated line-width, and has a faint, extended outer disk. However, Homeier Gallagher's (1999) H velocity map shows clear evidence for rotation in the inner, star-burst region. Our re-analysis confirms this: We would see similar structure if it existed in our STIS spectra.

gmw_00000415.name: PGC 045080
gmw_00000415.overview: PGC 045080 is a giant, isolated low-surface-brightness galaxy [arXiv:0705.1417, arXiv:0707.3527] featuring a highly inclined disk [arXiv:0705.1417], a short bar, and no bulge [arXiv:1607.01889]. It hosts a massive, lopsided H I disk with low star formation [arXiv:0707.3527] and exhibits extended radio lobes that suggest weak active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:0705.1417, arXiv:0707.3527].

sources.summary: The paper describes PGC 045080 as a giant low-surface-brightness galaxy with extended radio lobes, possible weak AGN activity, and a warped, lopsided H I disk.

quotes.quote: We present radio observations and optical spectroscopy of the giant low surface brightness (LSB) galaxy PGC 045080 (or 1300+0144). PGC 045080 is a moderately distant galaxy having a highly inclined optical disk and massive HI gas content. Radio continuum observations of the galaxy were carried out at 320 MHz, 610 MHz and 1.4 GHz.
Continuum emission was detected and mapped in the galaxy. The emission appears extended over the inner disk at all three frequencies. At 1.4 GHz and 610 MHz it appears to have two distinct lobes.
Hence we conclude that a weak or hidden AGN may be present in PGC 045080. The extended radio emission represents lobes/jets from the AGN.

sources.summary: The paper describes PGC 045080 as an edge-on, distant, isolated giant LSB galaxy with little star formation, a lopsided massive HI disk, and extended radio lobes near its nucleus.

quotes.quote: (i) PGC 045080 is close to edge on and fairly distant ( v_ sys =12,264   ). Early optical studies did not detect an AGN in this galaxy (Sprayberry et al. 1993) but weak AGN activity may be present (Das et al. 2007). The galaxy is poor in star formation, fairly isolated and has a lopsided, massive HI disk.

(i) PGC 045080 was observed at 1.4 GHz, 610 MHz and 325 MHz (Das et al. 2007). The emission is extended and at 610 MHz appears to have two lobes associated with the nucleus (Figure 1a). The spectral index between 1.4 GHz and 325 MHz is =-0.63 (where S_ ^ ).

sources.summary: The paper notes poor H-band conditions for 1300+0144, describes a short bar without a bulge, flags high-inclination uncertainty, and finds a flat bulge-bar color gradient.

quotes.quote: 1300+0144- The sky conditions were bad during the H band observations. The galaxy is has a short bar but no bulge.
Unfortunately some of our galaxies 0223-0033, UGC 2936, UGC 9634, UGC 8794 and 1300+0144 have high inclination angles; hence, the results for these galaxies will have greater error.

(i) For the galaxies CGCG 381-048, UGC 11754, PGC 68495, UGC 10405, UGC 5035, 0223-0033, UGC 9087, UGC 3968, 1300+0144, PGC 60365, CGCG 006-023, 1252+0230 the (J-K _s ) color of their bulge and bar regions are almost the same i.e. they have a flat color gradient. We can conclude that the bulge and bar have almost similar stellar populations and low dust content.

gmw_00000416.name: UGC 1922
gmw_00000416.overview: UGC 1922 is a giant low surface brightness galaxy characterized by a featureless disk and a peculiar, bright bulge [arXiv:0707.3527, arXiv:2004.14458]. The galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0707.3527, arXiv:1108.0763], with its molecular gas concentrated entirely within the high-density bulge [arXiv:astro-ph/0304021, arXiv:1108.0763].

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 1922 as a distant giant LSB galaxy with a bright nucleus, molecular gas concentration, optical and radio AGN, and extended 610 MHz continuum emission.

quotes.quote: (ii) UGC 1922 has a bright nucleus or bulge and a very low surface brightness disk. It is also fairly distant ( v_ sys =10,894   ). It is one of the rare LSB galaxies that have been detected in CO and there is a significant concentration of molecular gas in the galaxy nucleus (O'Neil Schinnerer 2003). The galaxy hosts an AGN that is visible in optical emission as well as radio continuum.

(ii) The continuum emission at 610 MHz in UGC 1922 is also extended and the peak is offset from the galaxy center by a few arcseconds (Figure 1b). The spectral index between 1.4 GHz and 610 MHz is 3 suggesting that it may be a Giga-Hertz Peaked Spectrum (GPS) radio source.

sources.summary: The paper maps CO in UGC 01922, finding centrally peaked molecular gas, an H2 mass near 1.1e9 solar masses, and CO confined to the high-density bulge.

quotes.quote: The studied galaxy, UGC 01922, was chosen for these observations because both of its previous CO detection with the IRAM 30m telescope and its classification as a Malin 1 `cousin' -- an LSB galaxy with M _ HI 10 ^ 10 M _ . The OVRO map detected approximately 65 of the CO(1 0) flux found earlier with the single dish measurements, giving a detected gas mass equivalent to M_ H_2 = 1.1 10^9 . The integrated gas peak lies at the center of the galaxy and coincides with both the optical and 1.4 GHz continuum emission peaks. The molecular gas extends well beyond the OVRO beam size ( 4 \ or 3 kpc), covering 25 of the optical bulge.

UGC 01922, with its low density LSB disk surrounding a bright, high density nucleus, is an excellent test case for the theory laid out in oneil02 . In agreement with their theory, the only CO seen within UGC 01922 is in the higher density bulge, with no CO seen in the galaxy's low density disk.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 1922 as a peculiar bulge embedded in an LSB disc with high stellar velocity dispersion, counter-rotating outer gas, a large disc, and membership in a small group.

quotes.quote: On the right-hand is UGC 1922 which seems to be a bulge embedded in the LSB disc, and has peculiar morphology. Its stellar velocity dispersion is high and corresponds to the strongly overheated disc. We also found counter-rotation of the outer gaseous disc with respect to the inner one (see Fig. profiles ). The radius of the disc of UGC 1922 is also higher than that of UGC 1378 (see Table properties ). UGC 1922 belongs to a group that includes 7 spectroscopically confirmed members, one of which is a giant elliptical galaxy that dominates the group Saburova2018 .

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 1922 as a featureless-disk LSB galaxy with a bright active bulge, diffuse X-ray emission, molecular gas, and an intermediate-mass black hole.

quotes.quote: UGC 1922: The disk of this galaxy is featureless but has a bright bulge that also shows diffuse x-ray emission. It is classified as an LSB galaxy with an active nucleus by . UGC 1922 is also one of the few LSB galaxies that have a significant concentration of molecular gas oneil.schinnerer.2003 .
The masses are 0.3 10^6 \ M_ for the galaxies UGC 1922, UGC 6968 and F568-6, and lie on the lower mass tail of the low-mass blackhole sample of which has a median mass of 1.3 10^6 M _ .

gmw_00000417.name: UGC 6614
gmw_00000417.overview: UGC 6614 is a giant, face-on low-surface-brightness galaxy [arXiv:0707.3527, arXiv:1802.09967] featuring a central bulge [arXiv:0707.3527, arXiv:astro-ph/0107366], extended spiral arms [arXiv:0707.3527], and a kinematically decoupled, counter-rotating central component [arXiv:2004.14458]. Its active galactic nucleus exhibits a variable X-ray spectrum [arXiv:1001.5096], a radio jet [arXiv:0707.3527], and gas outflows [arXiv:1510.07743, arXiv:1108.0763].

sources.summary: The paper studies UGC 6614 as a low-surface-brightness galaxy with a bright, variable X-ray nucleus whose spectrum resembles a Seyfert I AGN.

quotes.quote: We report a study of the X-ray emission from the nuclear region of the low surface brightness (LSB) galaxy UGC 6614. Very little is known about the central objects in LSB galaxies especially their X-ray properties and X-ray spectra. In this study we have used XMM-Newton archival data to study the characteristics of the X-ray spectrum and the X-ray flux variability of the AGN in the LSB galaxy UGC 6614. The nucleus of UGC 6614 is very bright in X-ray emission with an absorption corrected 0.2-10.0 keV luminosity of 1.1 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 .

The X-ray spectrum is found to be power-law type with a moderate column density. A short time scale of intensity variation and large X-ray flux is indicative of the presence of a black hole at the centre of this galaxy. Using the method of excess variance, we have determined the black hole mass to be 0.12 10^ 6 M_ . The X-ray spectral properties are similar to that of the Seyfert I type AGNs.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 6614 as a nearby face-on giant LSB galaxy with a prominent bulge, extended spiral arms, compact radio/optical AGN, and a 610 MHz radio jet.

quotes.quote: (iii) UGC 6614 is a relatively nearby LSB galaxy ( v_ sys =6,351   ). It is close to face on, has a prominent bulge and fairly distinct spiral arms that extend well into the disk. The AGN is visible in optical emission and appears as a compact core in the NVSS map and at millimeter wavelengths as well (Das et al. 2006).

(iii) The 610 MHz continuum map of UGC 6614 reveals a radio jet that extends well into the disk (Figure 1c). One side appears brighter than the other and may represent the nearer jet. The core is bright and has a peak brightness of 3 mJy at 610 MHz. The spectral index is flat above 1.4 GHz but at 610 MHz is -0.53.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 6614 as a giant low-surface-brightness galaxy whose inner decline and outer rise reduce bulge-disc degeneracy and support an inner baryon-dominated region.

quotes.quote: UGC 6614 UGC 6614 belongs to the class of giant LSB galaxies 1995AJ....109..558S ( fig:bulgeexamples , left). Giant LSB galaxies have very extended LSB discs and central light concentrations, which can be due to either a single large bulge MSB95 or a more complicated HSB structure, like a small bulge plus a compact disc 2007AJ....133.1085B, 2010A&A...516A..11L . In the case of UGC 6614, bulge and disc are not strongly degenerated because the rotation curve shows an inner decline driven by the bulge and a subsequent rising portion driven by the disc.

When disc and bulge are maximized, can explain the observed rotation curve out to 20 kpc. The bulge dominates out to 15 kpc. The maximal mass-to-light ratios are = = 0.77 0.05 . The former value is near-identical to stellar population synthesis models.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 6614 as a low-surface-brightness AGN host with blue-shifted emission-line outflows, a broad H component, and a low estimated Eddington ratio.

quotes.quote: Another galaxy, UGC 6614 (identification number 24) has a clear signature of outflow, blue shifted component in [ O iii ] doublet lines as well as in H and H emission lines, along with a broad H component. The three galaxies in our sample, UGC 6614 (identification no 24), UGC 6284 (identification no 28) and UGC 5035 (identification no 30), which are classified to be LINERs show blue-shifted component in H emission lines. UGC 6614 and UGC 6284 show the presence of outflow components in [ O iii ] emission line region along with H region.

UGC 6614 shows outflow feature in H , [ O iii ] and H regions. The measured Eddington ratio and BH mass for UGC 6614 are 0.024 and 4.44 10^6 \ respectively. The outflow component as measured by the blue shifted H line is moving at 3604.77 86.39 kms ^ 1 with a FWHM of 3213 200 kms ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies U6614 as the sample's giant LSB galaxy, models its dominant bulge and disk, and argues the bulge-dominated inner rotation disfavors cuspy halos.

quotes.quote: U6614 This is the only giant LSB in Sample I. The analysis is complicated by the presence of a dominant bulge, which we have modelled as an exponential spherical bulge with h=3.0'' and _0(R)=18.4 mag arcsec ^ 2 .

The disk has parameters h=19'' and _0(R)=21.3 mag arcsec ^ 2 . The rapid rise and subsequent dip in the rotation velocity at small radii clearly suggest the dominance of the bulge in this giant LSB galaxy. We have assumed the bulge to be maximal at (R)=3.7 . As the bulge accounts for most of the rotation velocity in the inner parts, this seems to argue against cuspy halos in giant LSB galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 6614 as a well-studied giant LSB galaxy with an optical/radio/X-ray AGN, a larger black hole, and possible outflow or jet emission.

quotes.quote: UGC 6614: This is a well studied giant LSB galaxy deblok.etal.1995 . It is close to face on in morphology and has a large bulge surrounded by a ring like feature ( Rahman.etal.2007 , Hinz.etal.2007 ). The disk has faint but tightly wound spiral arms pickering.etal.1997 . The bulge hosts an AGN that is bright at optical schombert98 , radio das.etal.2006 and x-ray naik.etal.2010 wavelengths.
UGC 6614 harbors a BH of mass 3.8 10^ 6 M_ ; it also shows an interesting feature blueward of \ and \ implying outflow of gas or a one-sided jet streaming towards us.

sources.summary: The paper reports a larger integrated H I flux for UGC 6614 than earlier Arecibo values, consistency with VLA data, and a poorly constrained H I diameter because of a central depression.

quotes.quote: UGC 6614 : Our new integrated \ flux of 15.94 \ is significantly larger than previous measurements from Arecibo (Bothun et al. 1985a; Haynes Giovanelli 1989). However, Pickering et al. (1997) measured S_ HI =15.0 \ from VLA data, consistent with our results. Our \ diameter estimate for this object is very poorly constrained, as we find a Gaussian \ model gives a poor fit to the data. This is reaffirmed by the VLA data of Pickering et al. (1997), which show a signficant central depression in the \ distribution of this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses UGC 6614 to show that even a bulge-dominated giant LSB galaxy has a shallow inferred inner dark-matter slope in the minimum-disk case.

quotes.quote: Minimum disk does provide relevant limits on the inner slopes as is particularly well illustrated by UGC 6614. This is a bulge-dominated giant LSB galaxy, and we expect the bulge to contribute significantly to the dynamics at small radius for any plausible stellar mass-to-light ratio. However, even in the minimum disk case we find a shallow slope =-0.3 . This means that for any non-minimum disk situation the dark matter must be depressed even further away from the NFW case (i.e. \ the true slope must be even flatter).

sources.summary: The paper reports a kinematically decoupled, counter-rotating central component in UGC 6614 but says more stellar-population and gas-metallicity information is needed before choosing between catastrophic and non-catastrophic formation scenarios.

quotes.quote: We revealed the presence of a kinematically decoupled central component in the inner regions of UGC1922, UGC1382 and UGC6614, where we detected counter-rotating kinematical components.

UGC 6614 . For this galaxy we need more information to choose the scenario (properties of stellar population and gas metallicty in the region of LSB-disc). We can not exclude both catastrophic and non-catastrophic scenarios.

gmw_00000418.overview: The galaxy 0647+693 is a wide-angle tail radio source located in the Abell 562 cluster [arXiv:2009.04265]. It is nearly at rest relative to its host cluster, and its twin radio jets exhibit a characteristic bent shape [arXiv:2009.04265].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0647+693 as Abell 562's prototypical WAT radio source, finds its host nearly at rest in the cluster, and attributes jet bending to merger-driven ICM flows.

quotes.quote: This radio source is also known as 3C169 1961ApJ...134..970V or 0647+693 1985AJ.....90..954O and exhibits the typical bent shape of its twin jets. 2006MNRAS.368..609J calculated that a galaxy velocity of 870 km s ^ 1 would be needed to bend this source based on the radio properties of the jet and the X-ray properties of the surrounding gas. They argued that such a velocity is probably too large for a centrally located galaxy and can only be produced by a cluster merger.

We measure a negligible peculiar motion of the WAT with respect to the cluster location. The recessional velocity of the WAT is 32,750 60 km s ^ 1 and the cluster location has a value of 32,643 120 km s ^ 1 . Thus, the peculiar velocity is 107 134 km s ^ 1 (corrected to the cluster rest frame). Thus, the WAT galaxy is at rest with respect to the cluster.

gmw_00000419.name: IC 2476
gmw_00000419.overview: IC 2476 is a massive, quenched elliptical galaxy located at the center of a group of 12 spectroscopic members [arXiv:2403.17145]. It is associated with the remnant radio galaxy B2 0924+30, whose jets switched off approximately 50 million years ago [arXiv:2403.17145].

sources.summary: The paper uses IC 2476 as an example group-dominant elliptical hosting remnant radio galaxy B2 0924+30, whose old jets deposited AGN energy into the surrounding IGrM.

quotes.quote: To illustrate this point, in the left-hand panel of Fig. fig:ic2476 we show a composite image of the galaxy IC 2476. IC 2476 is a massive ( M_ =11.39 M_ ), quenched ( SFR=-2.124 M_ /yr) elliptical galaxy at z=0.0472 Salim:2016 . The blue background image is an SDSS i band image, which highlights the position of the galaxy at the center of the image. While the galaxy lives in a rather poor optical environment, spectroscopic data tell us that it is the dominant galaxy of a group of 12 spectroscopic members Tempel:2017 .

The bright remnant radio galaxy B2 0924+30 Jamrozy:2004,Shulevski:2017 , which extends over >100 kpc from the nucleus, is associated with IC 2476. A spectral ageing analysis shows that the radio jets were active for a period of 100 Myr and switched off 50 Myr ago . The image clearly shows that the bulk of the AGN energy is injected at large distances from the central galaxy within the IGrM, which reheats the surrounding medium and eventually quenches star formation McNamara:2007 .

gmw_00000420.name: ON 231
gmw_00000420.overview: ON 231 is an intermediate-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae blazar [arXiv:1109.6000, arXiv:1108.0607] located at the center of an elliptical host galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0412296, arXiv:1309.2774] at a redshift of z = 0.102 [arXiv:1109.6000, arXiv:1701.04305]. It exhibits strong multiwavelength variability [arXiv:1109.6000, arXiv:1102.4428], characterized by historical optical outbursts [arXiv:1005.4475, arXiv:1608.08860] and repeated very-high-energy gamma-ray flares [arXiv:1109.6000, arXiv:1110.0040].

sources.summary: VERITAS detected W Comae as a VHE-emitting IBL, observed repeated flaring, and multiwavelength data showed strong gamma-ray and X-ray variability modeled with jet emission.

quotes.quote: W Comae W Comae (ON 231, z =0.102) was the first IBL to be detected at VHE . It was discovered as a VHE source by VERITAS during observations carried out over a 4-month period (Jan 2008 to Apr 2008). During this time, a strong gamma-ray outburst was measured over a period of 4 days, when the source went into a flaring state in the middle of March reaching 9 of Crab Nebula flux.

In June 2008, the source again went to a high state and was approximately three times brighter than during 2008 March observations, triggering a multiwavelength campaign which included AGILE, Swift and XMM-Newton. VERITAS observed W Comae for 230 minutes in between June 7 and 9. The average flux on June 7-8 was 2.5-3 times higher than that observed during March 2008.

Figure wcom_mwl shows the multiwavelength light curve of W Comae (MJD 54622 to 54634) . The initial detection of the flare by VERITAS was followed by observations in high-energy gamma rays (AGILE, E _ > 100 MeV), X-rays ( Swift and XMM-Newton), UV and ground based optical and radio monitoring instruments through the GASP-WEBT consortium. The measurements revealed strong variability in both gamma rays and X-rays on timescales of days.

sources.summary: The paper reports VERITAS VHE emission and flaring from W Comae, describes soft spectra and multiwavelength SED modeling, and says an external-Compton component gives the preferred interpretation.

quotes.quote: W Comae is a bright, nearby ( z = 0.102 ), EGRET-detected IBL. It was observed with VERITAS for 40 h of good-quality live time between January and April 2008 . A weak detection ( 5 ) of VHE ray emission (VER J1221+282) is found in the overall data set, with 70 of the excess (275 rays, 8.6 ) occurring during a 4-night flare in March 2008. During the brightest two nights, the VHE flux is 9 Crab above 200 GeV, and the VHE spectrum is characterized by a soft power law ( = 3.8 0.4 ). While the contemporaneous MWL SED can be described by an SSC model, an SSC model with an additional external-Compton (EC) component yields a more natural set of fit parameters and is therefore preferred.

W Comae is the first IBL detected at VHE energies, and the improvement in the fit from adding an EC component, not needed in typical HBL modeling, suggests there may be different underlying processes generating the VHE ray emission in different parts of the blazar sequence. In June 2008, a second VHE flare, 3 times as bright as the first, was observed from W Comae .

sources.summary: The paper describes ON 231 as a z=0.102 IBL quasar/blazar with historical optical outbursts and monitored multi-band variability without detected time lags.

quotes.quote: ON 231 was discovered as a variable star by 1916AN....202..415W , and was reported as a new quasar by 1971Natur.231..515B . The detection of a few weak emission lines in its spectrum made it possible to determine a redshift of z = 0.102 1985ApJ...292..614W . The synchrotron peak frequency log _ peak = 14.84 was calculated by 2006A&A...445..441N and it is classified as an IBL.
Our optical , i , and o bands observations of ON 231 were carried out for 76, 80, and 78 nights, respectively, between 2009 May 23 and 2011 May 15.

One can see from Table observations that the variation amplitude decreases towards longer wavelengths, which is in accord with 2013MNRAS.429.2773C . We did not find any IDV because of the low sampling on each single night. No inter-band time lags were found at long and short timescales for this source (see Table alldelay ).

sources.summary: The paper reports a June 2008 TeV flare from W Comae, AGILE follow-up detection, a broad multiwavelength campaign, and SSC plus external-Compton SED modeling.

quotes.quote: Multi-wavelength campaigns involving ray observations, together with MAGIC and VERITAS TeV observations of Mrk 421 and W Comae were performed in June 2008. On 8 June 2008, VERITAS announced the detection of a TeV flare from the IBL object W Comae ( ), with a three times higher flux with respect to the flare observed in March 2008 ( ). About 24 hours later, \ re-pointed towards the source and detected it with a flux for E > 100 MeV of (90 32) 10 ^ 8 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , roughly a factor of 1.5 larger than the highest flux observed by EGRET (see ). The VERITAS observations triggered a multi-wavelength campaign including also Swift , XMM- Newton and GASP observations, covering the entire electromagnetic spectrum from radio to TeV.

The SED of W Comae during the VHE ray flare can be modelled by a simple leptonic SSC model, but the wide separation of the two peaks in the SED requires low ratio of the magnetic field to electron energy density ( _B = 2.3 10^ 3 ), far from the equipartition. The SSC+EC model returns magnetic field parameters closer to equipartition, providing a satisfactory description of the broadband SED ( ).

sources.summary: The paper models W com in low and flare stages, finding valid PLLP EBL results for the low stage and a more reasonable BPL-derived density for the flare stage.

quotes.quote: 6. W com As a TeV IBL object with the redshift of 0.10, we take t_ v,min =12hr and '_ min =200 in low and flare stages. For the low stage, as shown in Fig.6, both SEDs have lost the observed data between 10^ 24 10^ 25 Hz band. In the Fig.7, like PKS 0447-439, the EBL results are valid in PLLP EEDs and about 10-20 n W m ^ 2 sr ^ 1 , which are similar to the result given by .

For the flare stage, as shown in Fig.7, the fitting SEDs seem to cover multi-waveband spectra although insufficient observation in GeV-TeV band. Unlike other sources, the reduced EBL density in BPL EEDs is more reasonable than that in PLLP EEDs, which is similar to the result given by , and it is about 10 n W m ^ 2 sr ^ 1 . We also note that the EBL density in PLLP EEDs looks very weird.

sources.summary: The paper observes ON 231 during its 1998 optical outburst, finding a concave X-ray spectrum with synchrotron and inverse-Compton components plus rapid soft-X-ray variability.

quotes.quote: ON 231 was observed with Beppo SAX in May and June 1998, following an exceptional optical outburst which occured in April--May. We measured the X--ray spectrum from 0.1 up to 100 keV. In both occasions the spectrum had a concave shape, with a break detected at about 4 and 2.5 keV, respectively. We interpret the steeper component at energies below the break as due to synchrotron emission and the extremely flat component at energies above the break as due to inverse Compton emission.
During the May observation we also detected a fast variability event with the flux below 4 keV increasing by about a factor of three in 5 hours. Above 4 keV no variability was detected.

sources.summary: The paper identifies ON 231, also W Comae, as a BL Lac with a 1995-1998 optical outburst and declining luminosity, and reports a power-law XMM variability profile.

quotes.quote: ON 231 ON 231 was previously known as the ``variable star'' W Comae (and is still also called by that name). In 1971, because of its odd properties, particularly a strong optical variability and an apparently line-less continuum, it was suggested that it was an extragalactic source and was assigned to the then rather new class of BL Lac objects (Biraud 1971; Browne 1971).

In 1995, ON 231 started a phase of strong brightness and activity that culminated in an outburst in April--May 1998, when it reached its brightest magnitude at R = 12.2 (Massaro et al. \ 1999) comparable to that measured at the beginning of the past century when it was discovered as a variable star (Wolf 1916). After the optical outburst, ON 231 showed a slow decline in its mean luminosity (Tosti et al. \ 2002).
It reveals that the overall profile of the broad-band variability of ON 231 can be described by a single power-law with a slope =-2.11 0.32 , extending down to timescales of 1/f 1000 s.

sources.summary: The paper reports W Comae as an IBL detected at very high energy and by AGILE, with a broad multiwavelength campaign across radio to TeV bands.

quotes.quote: hhh W Comae was the first IBL object to be detected at very high energy (VHE; Acciari et al. 2008). It was discovered at TeV energies by VERITAS during observations carried out over January--April 2008, with an integrated flux of 9 of the Crab flux during a 4 days flare in mid-March. On 8 June 2008, VERITAS announced the detection of a second TeV flare from W Comae (Swordy 2008), with a three times higher flux with respect to the previous one. About 24 hours later, AGILE re-pointed towards the source and detected it with a flux of (90 32) 10 ^ 8 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 for E > 100 MeV, roughly a factor of 1.5 larger than the highest flux observed by EGRET (see Verrecchia et al. 2008). A multiwavelength campaign that included Swift , XMM- Newton , and GASP observations was triggered, covering the entire electromagnetic spectrum from radio to TeV.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1221 + 2813 as the nearest sample source and first TeV-detected intermediate BL Lac, with repeated confirmed variability and an internight fading event.

quotes.quote: J1221 + 2813 (W Comae/ON231, z=0.102 ) This is the nearest source in our sample and also the first intermediate type BL Lac (IBL) to be detected at TeV energies. Gupta et al. \ (2008) have reported R-band monitoring of this blazar on 11 Jan. \ 2007 for 3.24 hours, but no INOV was detected. We monitored this blazar on 4 epochs. It showed a confirmed variablity on 3 epochs using C_ eff and on all 4 epochs when the F test was applied (Table 3). In addition, a clear event of internight variation was seen when the source faded by 0.1mag between 19 and 20 Mar 2004.

sources.summary: The paper models W Comae as a VERITAS-detected intermediate-synchrotron-peaked blazar whose SED resists pure SSC and hadronic fits without an extra component.

quotes.quote: In particular, it has been used to interpret the SEDs of blazars detected in very high energies (VHE, E > 100 GeV) by the Very Energetic Radiation Imaging Telescope Array System (VERITAS), e.g., W Comae WComa,WComb , 1ES 0806+524 0806 , PKS 1424+240 1424 , RGB J0710+591 0710 , and 3C 66A 3C66A . We found that the following objects fulfilled our criteria: The FSRQs 3C 273, 3C 279, 3C 454.3, PKS 1510-089, PKS 0420-01, and PKS 0528+134; the LBLs BL Lacertae, AO 0235+164, S5 0716+714, and OJ 287; and the IBLs W Comae and 3C 66A. While BL Lac objects detected at > 100 GeV rays by ground-based Atmospheric Cherenkov Telescopes have often been found to be well represented by pure SSC models, the careful analysis of the simultaneous SEDs of the VERITAS-detected IBLs W Comae and 3C 66A WComb,3C66A revealed that also in these two cases, a pure SSC fit would require rather extreme parameters with magnetic fields far below equipartition.

However, the unusual, apparent upward curvature of the Fermi LAT spectrum of 3C 66A and W Comae can not be satisfactorily represented with our model, irrespective of the dominant target photon field for external Compton scattering. However, even though the addition of photopion induced cascade emission to a proton synchrotron component is, in principle, able to reproduce concave ray spectra, the steep upturn of the Fermi-LAT spectrum of 3C 66A and W Comae at GeV energies is still not well represented also by this model, and may indicate the need for an additional emission component (possibly from muon/pion synchrotron radiation, with consequences for the values of the fit parameters).

sources.summary: The paper revises W Comae's adopted accretion luminosity downward, notes its sub-Eddington hadronic jet-power possibility, and says adiabatic cooling dominates its model discrepancy.

quotes.quote: However, the exception is W Comae, for which the L_ acc estimate used by B13 is 40 times higher than that given in ZCST14, which is the upper limit of ghisellini10 , hereafter G10. B13 used the estimate of xie08 , who refer in turn to two other papers, which do not seem to give that information. Thus, we use here the value of G10.
Still, we do not exclude that a sub-Eddington jet powers can be obtained for some objects, as it is, e.g., the case for W Comae in our sample.
The adiabatic cooling is, in fact, dominant in AO 0235+164 and W Comae, in which case the proton-synchrotron estimate gives values 200 and 50 times too low, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper extends VERITAS low-flux-state observations of W Comae, deriving its first low-state VHE spectrum, a 6.2 sigma detection, and a power-law spectral fit.

quotes.quote: Beyond the previous low-flux-state detection of W Comae in 2012, this analysis includes two seasons of observations (2012/13 and 2013/14) from the upgraded VERITAS array, resulting in 39 hours of total livetime after data quality cuts. All observations by VERITAS of W Comae over the span of 2010-2014 are defined as the low-flux state, which was determined by comparing the integral flux of the high-flux state (June 2008 flare) to the daily light curve from 2010 to 2014 (see Figure fig:lc2 ).
